## [v0.17.140] — 2026-06-07 — *Live Telemetry: badge turn-cached tool calls*

The turn-cache (v0.17.130) and failure-loop-breaker (v0.17.131) skip the MCP round-trip when the model re-requests an identical read, but the Live Telemetry panel still showed the call with no sign it was served from cache. This release badges them: a cache-served tool call now renders a small `cached` chip next to its name in the telemetry timeline, so you can tell a deduplicated hit from a real dispatch at a glance.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent chat route + UI, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.139] — 2026-06-07 — *Cleanup: stale prompt approval line + Cortex XDR empty-args crash*

Two small fixes from the campaign loose-end sweep:

**Cortex XDR `xdr_incidents_list` no longer crashes on empty args.** Calling it with no `limit` (the connector runtime passes `None` for unspecified optional args) hit `int(None)` and threw a `TypeError`. The four `limit`/`alerts_limit` clamp sites in the connector now route through a None/invalid-safe coercion that degrades to the default — so `xdr_incidents_list`, `xdr_alerts_list`, incident-extra-data, and XQL-results paging all tolerate a missing or non-numeric limit.

**Removed a stale approval requirement from the bundled prompt asset.** `bundles/spark/prompts/system.md` still said "require approval before launching Caldera operations or sending webhook logs into XSIAM" — a requirement removed back in v0.1.22 (approval gating now applies only to agent self-modification: jobs, settings, personality, instances, credentials). Corrected to match the current policy.

Scenario 1 upgrade (connector + bundled prompt asset, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.138] — 2026-06-07 — *Data sources: fix PAN-OS pack `origin` value (completes the validator-green fix)*

The 6 PAN-OS NGFW packs added in v0.17.136 carried `origin: manual` from their generator, but the bundle schema only allows `origin: bundle | user` — so they failed the validator's `data_source.yaml validate` check once it re-ran. Set them to `origin: bundle`. With this plus v0.17.137, the AI-Layer validator is green on `main`: all 294 bundled data sources pass schema validation, with unique field names and valid field types.

Scenario 1 upgrade (bundled content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.137] — 2026-06-07 — *Data sources: fix 4 bundled YAMLs that failed schema validation*

Three bundled data sources (Microsoft Defender for Cloud, Tanium Threat Response, Tanium Integrity Monitor) listed each field name two or three times — a generation artifact — and three (those plus Radware AppWall) used an invalid `number` field type. This kept the bundle's CI validator red on `main` (its `data_source.yaml validate` and `no duplicate field names` checks).

This release cleans all four: `number` → `float`, and duplicate-named fields collapse to one entry each, keeping the most-informative copy (a specific type over a generic `string`, then the longest description). Field counts drop accordingly (e.g. Defender for Cloud 193 → 103) with no loss of *distinct* fields, and the validator is green again.

Scenario 1 upgrade (bundled content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.136] — 2026-06-07 — *Data sources: add Palo Alto Networks NGFW (PAN-OS) firewall — 6 datasets*

Phantom's bundle had no Palo Alto PAN-OS firewall data source (only Prisma SaaS Security), which is why the chat agent fabricated a non-existent `palo_alto_networks_pan_os_raw` dataset when asked where PAN-OS logs land. This release adds the real thing: six PAN-OS NGFW data sources — Traffic, Threat, URL, File Data, GlobalProtect, and HIP Match — with their authoritative Cortex dataset names (`panw_ngfw_traffic_raw`, `panw_ngfw_threat_raw`, `panw_ngfw_url_raw`, `panw_ngfw_filedata_raw`, `panw_ngfw_globalprotect_raw`, `panw_ngfw_hipmatch_raw`) and full CEF-key field schemas (17–49 fields each) derived from the demisto/content PAN-OS modeling rules.

Each source documents its routing in `how_to_use`: simulate as CEF to your XSIAM Broker with header vendor=`panw`, product=`ngfw_cef`; the `log_type` extension splits events into the per-type dataset (with the Cortex PAN-OS content pack installed) or the catch-all `panw_ngfw_cef_raw` without it. They ship as **not-yet-validated** (`validated: false`) — promoting them to the green "Validated" pill requires a live-tenant XDM saturation run, which is gated on the tenant's PAN-OS pack + available XQL quota.

The agent now lists and cites a real PAN-OS source instead of inventing a dataset name.

Scenario 1 upgrade (bundled content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.135] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: never render a blank assistant turn after a tool ran*

A round-3 smoke probe (export a data source as YAML) had the agent fetch the schema via two read tools and then return a blank message — no recap, no data. The chat route already replaces a strictly-empty final response with a recap of what ran, but a whitespace-only response slipped past that guard and rendered blank.

This release trims the final response before deciding: any empty-or-whitespace turn after tool calls now falls through to the recap ("I checked N tools but didn't generate a follow-up message…") instead of a silent blank.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent chat route only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.134] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: answer "where do these logs land" from installed sources, not native-support knowledge*

A deploy-verification of v0.17.133 caught one residual fabrication: asked where Palo Alto PAN-OS firewall logs land, the agent confidently named a `palo_alto_networks_pan_os_raw` dataset and claimed the operator "tested it recently" — but no PAN-OS firewall data source is installed in this environment (only SaaS Security), and the agent had no tool result supporting either claim. The root cause is subtle: Cortex XSIAM *does* natively parse PAN-OS, so the agent answered from general product knowledge instead of from what's actually installed here.

This sharpens the routing rule: a "where do X's logs land" question is answered from the installed data sources only. If the vendor isn't in `data_sources_list` / `data_sources_installed_as_vendors`, the agent says it isn't an installed data source in this environment rather than citing a dataset name from general Cortex knowledge — and never claims the operator tested something no tool result shows.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent system prompt only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.133] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: cite real identifiers from tool results, never invent them*

A third round of deep chat smoke caught the agent stating specific identifiers it had tool access to but got wrong — FortiGate fields like `FTNTFGTsrcip` that don't exist (the real field is `srcip`), a `palo_alto_networks_pan_os_raw` dataset no installed source defines, and Caldera adversary profiles (`APT29`, `FIN7`) that `caldera_get_adversaries` never returned.

This extends the v0.17.132 tool-use discipline with an explicit rule: any specific field name, dataset name, adversary/scenario name, worker id, or count the agent states MUST come verbatim from a tool result it actually received that turn — not a plausible-looking guess. If the thing isn't in any tool result, the agent says it isn't available in this environment rather than inventing a name.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent system prompt only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.132] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: tool-use discipline — no retry loops, no fabricated results, honest narration*

A multi-lens analysis of a deep chat smoke surfaced a cluster of agent-behaviour issues. This release hardens the system prompt with an explicit tool-use discipline section:

- **No retry loops.** When a tool fails the same way — an error or a soft `{ok:false}` "not found" / "invalid" — the agent no longer re-fires the identical call; it switches tactics or reports the blocker. (Pairs with the mechanical backstop in v0.17.131.)
- **Compose XQL once.** For Cortex/XQL queries the agent follows the `build_xql_query` skill to build one correct query instead of firing a dozen trial-and-error variants — one probe drove 18 XQL calls for a single question.
- **No fabricated results.** If a verification query fails (e.g. quota exceeded), the agent now says so instead of inferring an answer from the data it generated — it previously reported a "top source IP" that no query ever returned.
- **Honest narration.** The agent no longer writes "I am about to call `tool_x`" for a call it does not make that turn; it either calls the tool or asks plainly, so its narration matches its real actions.
- **Blocker + creation reporting.** When a prerequisite is unmet (offline Caldera agents, exhausted quota) it stops and reports the blocker; when it creates a job or worker it confirms the id/schedule explicitly.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent system prompt only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.131] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: stops re-trying a tool call that already failed the same way this turn*

A deep chat smoke caught the agent re-firing an *identical failing* tool call — most starkly, `data_sources_install` **seven times** while answering a read-only "list this source's fields" question, because the install kept returning a soft "not found" each time and the agent kept retrying it rather than switching tactics.

The turn-cache shipped earlier only remembers *successes*, so it couldn't catch this. This release adds the matching backstop: within one turn, once the **same tool with the same arguments** has failed twice, any further identical call is short-circuited with a "don't retry — change approach" answer instead of hitting the backend again. A failure here means either a thrown error or the explicit `{ok:false}` result envelope Phantom tools return on a soft failure.

It stays precise: poll/wait tools (which re-issue the same call by design) are exempt, a retry with *different* arguments still runs, and a soft-failed read is no longer mistakenly cached as a success. Net effect: pathological retry loops are capped at two attempts, and the agent is nudged to try a different tool or report the blocker.

This release also clears an internal hygiene gremlin — the previous release's tool-call cache key used a stray NUL byte as its separator, which made the source file register as binary to some tooling (harmless at runtime, now replaced with a space).

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent chat route only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.130] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: redundant catalog/knowledge reads in one turn no longer cost a round-trip*

A follow-up to v0.17.129. The soft instruction added there ("list the data-source catalog once per turn and reuse it") helped, but a model can ignore prose. Live chat smoke still showed the agent re-requesting `data_sources_list` 3–4 times — and `knowledge_search` 3 times — while answering a single question.

This release adds the **mechanical backstop**: within one chat turn, an identical read-only call (same tool, same arguments) for static platform metadata is **served from a per-turn cache instead of being re-dispatched to the MCP**. The covered reads are the ones that can't change mid-turn — the data-source catalog, a source's schema, installed vendors, the marketplace list, your log destinations, settings, skills, knowledge search, field info, and the technology stack. Repeating any of them in the same turn is now free of MCP work and latency.

It stays correct: a catalog or config change you make in the same turn (installing a source, editing a destination) clears the snapshot so a follow-up read sees fresh state, and two *different* lookups (the schema for source A then source B) both run as before — only verbatim repeats are collapsed. The cache lives for exactly one message and never crosses turns or operators.

Note on what you'll see: the model still *requests* the tool, so the Live Telemetry panel still lists the call — it just no longer reaches the MCP. Marking cached requests visually distinct in telemetry is tracked as a follow-up.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent chat route only, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.129] — 2026-06-07 — *Chat agent: auto-retry an empty model turn; stop re-listing the data-source catalog per vendor*

Two chat-agent reliability/efficiency fixes surfaced by a UI chat smoke:

**No more silent "I didn't generate a response this turn."** The model occasionally returned a completely empty turn — no text and no tool calls — for a perfectly valid request, leaving you to retry by hand (the same prompt then worked on resend). The chat route now **auto-retries an empty turn once** before falling back to that message, recovering transparently from the transient model hiccup.

**Multi-vendor requests no longer re-list the whole catalog per vendor.** A request like "simulate Okta + Cisco + Palo Alto" had the agent call `data_sources_list` (which returns the entire catalog) once per vendor — seven times in one observed turn — inflating latency. The agent is now instructed to list the catalog once and reuse it, then look up each source's schema individually.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent chat route + system prompt, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.128] — 2026-06-07 — *phantom-updater reconciles connector containers to their pins on a timer, not just at startup*

Per-instance connector containers are recreated by phantom-updater when their pinned image digest changes — but until now only at updater **startup** (a one-shot ~30s after boot). Because phantom-updater rarely restarts (its own image isn't rebuilt on the dev cycle), a connector whose pin changed *between* restarts kept running its old image until the next restart. In practice this left connector containers stale: a connector recently pinned to a newer image could still be running an older one, with the newer tools unreachable from chat until someone manually recreated the container.

phantom-updater now also reconciles **on a periodic loop** (default every 5 minutes; override with `PHANTOM_UPDATER_RECONCILE_INTERVAL_S`). Each pass compares every `phantom-connector-*` container's running digest to its pin and recreates only the divergent ones — sequential, with per-container error isolation, exactly as the startup and manual `POST /api/v1/connectors/reconcile/digests` paths already did. Connector containers now track their pinned digest without needing an updater restart.

Scenario 1 upgrade (updater code, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.127] — 2026-06-06 — *Chat agent: routing answers consult the data source's how_to_use; simulation confirmations name the worker*

Two chat-agent response-quality fixes, both surfaced by post-release smoke:

**Routing questions now consult the authoritative `how_to_use`.** When you ask where a data source's events land (which XSIAM dataset), how to onboard it, or whether events will land in a given dataset, the agent reads that source's `how_to_use` via `data_sources_get_schema` before answering — instead of guessing the dataset from the vendor/product name. This matters for renamed/acquired vendors (the dataset keys on the original CEF literal, not the marketing name) and for channel-split sources like Windows ADFS/AMSI/DNS/Sysmon and several Azure logs, which are ingested via the native path (Windows agent/WEC, Azure diagnostic settings) and don't route by a simple CEF header. The agent previously answered such questions from name-derivation and could point you at a dataset your events never reach.

**Simulation confirmations now name what ran.** When the agent finished a simulation turn without a closing sentence, its fallback recap ("✓ Done. Applied N changes: …") omitted the data worker it had started — a "simulate Okta → XSIAM" turn recapped only an incidental `memory_store`. The recap now counts the simulation and catalog/data-source mutation tools, so it confirms the action you asked for.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent system prompt + chat route, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.126] — 2026-06-06 — *Scheduled prompt jobs run again; chronically-failing jobs back off instead of flooding*

**Scheduled `prompt` jobs work again.** Since v0.9.1 — when the agent added server-side auth on `/api/chat` — the job scheduler's loopback call carried no credentials, so every scheduled prompt job failed with a 401 (`no_session_cookie`). The scheduler now authenticates that internal call with the container's `MCP_TOKEN` (the same loopback trust the agent's internal hook-dispatch endpoint already uses), and the agent middleware accepts it. A scheduled prompt that runs a skill, queries XSIAM, or drives a simulation now executes the same pipeline an interactive chat does. Credential-management routes stay blocked even for the internal token — the security invariant is unchanged.

**A failing job now backs off instead of flooding.** Previously the scheduler only auto-disabled a job when its target *tool* was missing; a job that failed for any other persistent reason (a 401, a provider error) re-fired every cron tick indefinitely — one leftover test job had logged ~24,000 consecutive failed runs in the audit log. The scheduler now disables a non-one-shot job after 10 consecutive failures, records the reason on the job, and stops re-firing; re-enable it from the job's page once the cause is fixed.

Scenario 1 upgrade (scheduler + middleware fix, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.125] — 2026-06-06 — *XSIAM + Cortex XDR incident / alert / endpoint tools are reachable from chat again*

The agent can now call the **45 XSIAM tools** added across v0.15.x (incidents, alerts, IoCs, endpoints, response actions, scripts, audit logs, distributions, alert exclusions, hash analytics, exploits, parsers, datamodel, broker) **and the Cortex XDR tools** added in v0.14.1+ — directly from chat. Before this fix they returned `Unknown tool: 'xsiam_incidents_list'` / `'xdr_incidents_list'` even though the tools existed, so a chat request to pull an XSIAM case, list alerts, or isolate an endpoint failed or silently fell back.

**Root cause** was a tool-name mismatch introduced when those tools were authored: their `connector.yaml` entries already carried the connector prefix (`xsiam_…`, `xdr_…`), which produced a *doubled* legacy alias (`xsiam_xsiam_incidents_list`) — so the natural single-prefix name the agent actually calls was never advertised, and the call 404'd at the agent layer. The XSIAM tools additionally mis-dispatched on the wire because their connector container registers them under the bare name (`incidents_list`). The fix de-doubles the legacy alias for every connector and renames the 45 XSIAM `spec.tools[].name` entries to the bare form so the wire name matches the container. The 14 long-standing XSIAM tools (XQL, cases, datasets) were never affected and continue to work.

Scenario 1 upgrade (agent-side connector dispatch fix, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.124] — 2026-06-06 — *Every bundled data source now ships reverse-engineered how_to_use, realistic synthetic values, and end-to-end routing validation*

Every one of the **288 bundled data sources** now carries a `how_to_use` guide and a reverse-engineered field inventory in the drawer (up from the validated set plus a handful). The **251 non-validated** sources were reverse-engineered from their vendor parsing/modeling rules: the CEF routing literal (vendor/product → dataset), the modeling-rule gate, the ingestion shape (flat vs JSON-native), the XDM field inventory, and a verify query. The drawer is no longer sparse for any source.

**Synthetic simulation now generates realistic typed values.** The data worker emits real-looking values per field semantic type — IPv4/IPv6 addresses, two-letter country codes, HTTP methods, ports, byte counts, MACs, URLs, hashes — instead of generic placeholders. Value-dependent modeling-rule reads (regex matches, enum gates, typed extractions) now fire, so simulated events populate far more `xdm.*` fields. Measured lift on the live tenant: Office 365 general 53 → 65 and SharePoint 38 → 50 distinct XDM fields; Azure WAF's `xdm.source.ipv4` / `location.country` / `network.http.method` now populate from realistic values.

**Routing validated end-to-end across all 288 sources** (250 swept live on the tenant): every asserted CEF routing literal lands its events in the correct dataset, and every flagged display-name divergence was confirmed to misroute — zero wrong routing literals. The sweep surfaced and **fixed 6 false routing claims**: `microsoft_adfs` / `amsi` / `dns` / `sysmon` and `msft_azure_app_service` / `devops` previously claimed a CEF header that "normalizes to" their dataset, but they are channel-split sources (ingested via the Windows agent/WEC or Azure diagnostic settings and routed by event channel). Their `how_to_use` now explains the real native-path ingestion instead of a header that would never land their events.

**Prisma Cloud Compute validated** end-to-end (29 `xdm.*` fields); **Imperva WAF** schema corrected to use CEF wire keys rather than logical field names. Validated set: **36 → 37**.

**All 22 previously-validated sources leveled up to a rich `how_to_use`** — the 12 with thin or empty routing leads (Jira, ServiceNow, Qualys, Proofpoint Email/TAP, AWS WAF, CyberArk, O365 sharepoint/emails/dlp, Azure WAF/AKS) now open with vendor-specific routing prose matching the Okta/Alibaba standard.

Scenario 1 upgrade (bundled data-source content + simulation generator, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.123] — 2026-06-04 — *Six cloud-provider data sources validated end-to-end for XDM (Google Cloud + Microsoft Azure)*

Six GCP/Azure cloud-log sources now generate CEF logs that route into their XSIAM dataset, bind their installed content-pack modeling rule, and map a rich set of `xdm.*` fields — validated live on the tenant via the simulate → XSIAM → XQL loop. Each carries the green **Validated** pill on the Data Sources page and a validated **how_to_use** (CEF header, modeling-rule gate, JSON-composite note, verify XQL).

Measured XDM coverage on the live tenant (with the content pack installed):

- **Google Cloud Logging** (`google_cloud_logging_raw`) — 52 `xdm.*` fields (logName-gated; deeply-nested `protoPayload` JSON).
- **Google Cloud DNS** (`google_dns_raw`) — 30 fields (logName-gated).
- **Google Workspace Drive** (`google_workspace_drive_raw`) — 22 fields.
- **Google Cloud Security Command Center** (`google_scc_raw`) — 21 fields.
- **Microsoft Azure Firewall** (`msft_azure_firewall_raw`) — 20 fields (gate `category=AZFWApplicationRule`).
- **Google Apigee** (`google_apigee_raw`) — 10 fields (rule fully mapped).

**Onboarding note baked into each `how_to_use`:** cloud-provider sources are JSON-native — they map XDM only once their content pack is installed in XSIAM, which binds the modeling + parsing rules to the dataset. The worker emits nested-JSON composites on the CEF wire that the rules parse, so **no HTTP collector is required** — CEF-over-syslog suffices.

Validated data-source set grows **30 → 36**.

Scenario 1 upgrade — re-run your existing installer.

---

## [v0.17.122] — 2026-06-03 — *Three more marquee data sources validated end-to-end for XDM (Cisco Firepower, Cisco Duo, Microsoft 365 Defender)*

Three of five marquee SOC sources now generate logs that route into their XSIAM dataset, clear the modeling-rule gate, and map a rich set of `xdm.*` fields — validated live on the tenant via the simulate → XSIAM → XQL loop. Each carries the green **Validated** pill on the Data Sources page, and each ships a gate-aware **how_to_use** that tells the agent the exact CEF header, the modeling-rule gate, and how to verify.

Measured XDM coverage on the live tenant:

- **Cisco Firepower** (`cisco_firepower_raw`) — 39 `xdm.*` fields (unconditional rule; CEF header `Cisco`/`Firepower`).
- **Cisco Duo** (`duo_duo_raw`) — 54 fields (gate `eventtype=authentication`; CEF header `duo`/`duo`).
- **Microsoft 365 Defender** (`microsoft_365_defender_raw`) — 40 fields (unconditional; CEF header `microsoft`/`365_defender`).

**Two sources reached the broker but did NOT map — and their `how_to_use` now records exactly why and what onboarding they need:**

- **Netskope** (`netskope_netskope_raw`) — synthetic CEF lands and the broker extracts the columns correctly (`source_log_event=application`, the gate value), but `datamodel` returns zero modeled rows over a 365-day window: the broker-auto-created dataset is raw-only and its modeling rule never associates. Onboarding the Netskope content pack on the tenant is the fix.
- **GitHub audit** (`github_github_audit_raw`) — JSON-native (HTTP collector). The tenant INGEST rule parses a JSON `created_at` rather than CEF extensions, so the modeling rule's `action` column is absent and nothing maps. Needs the JSON/HTTP-collector ingestion path, not syslog-CEF.

**Verification note baked into each validated `how_to_use`:** query XDM over a **wide (≥7-day) window**. Synthetic events can carry a `_time` several days in the past, so a default 24-hour window can read zero rows even on a clean mapping — the trap that briefly made Cisco Firepower look unroutable before a 30-day window revealed all 39 fields.

Validated set: **27 → 30**.

Scenario 1 upgrade (bundled data-source content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

## [v0.17.121] — 2026-06-02 — *Hardening from the v0.17.120 validation retro: tighter schema compaction + reliable fresh-event verification*

Two refinements surfaced while validating the v0.17.120 sources end-to-end with freshly-generated events:

- **The compact schema fetch now fits even a 222-field vendor.** v0.17.120's `data_sources_get_schema(compact=true)` dropped per-field descriptions, but FortiGate's 222-field schema still bumped the agent's tool-result size cap and truncated to ~103 fields. Compact mode now also omits `is_array`/`is_meta` when they're `false` — lossless, since the simulator defaults both to false — which roughly halves per-field bytes so the *complete* field list reaches the worker. (FortiGate already mapped 76 XDM even truncated; this removes the ceiling for it and any future 200+-field vendor.)
- **The simulate-to-XSIAM skill now teaches reliable fresh-event verification** (lesson L24). In a tenant with accumulated test data, count XDM over `sort desc _time` rows, and don't filter on `xdm.event.type != null` as a universal "mapped" proxy — firewall/endpoint rules (FortiGate, Microsoft Windows) never populate it; they map `xdm.event.outcome` / `xdm.event.id` instead, so that filter returns zero rows and reads as total failure. This codifies the method that confirmed all five v0.17.120 sources map *fresh* events: FortiGate 76, Salesforce 43, SentinelOne 34, Windows 29, Zscaler 17.

Scenario 1 upgrade (code + skill content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

## [v0.17.120] — 2026-06-02 — *Five new data sources validated end-to-end for XDM mapping (FortiGate, SentinelOne, Microsoft Windows, Zscaler, Salesforce)*

The five data sources you installed on XSIAM now generate logs that route into their dataset, clear the modeling-rule gate, and map a rich set of `xdm.*` fields — validated live on the tenant via the simulate → XSIAM → XQL loop, the same bar as the existing 22. Each carries the green **Validated** pill on the Data Sources page.

Measured XDM coverage on the live tenant:

- **FortiGate** (`fortinet_fortigate_raw`) — 67 `xdm.*` fields.
- **Salesforce** (`salesforce_realtime_raw`) — 32 fields.
- **Microsoft Windows Events** (`microsoft_windows_raw`) — 27 fields.
- **SentinelOne** (`sentinelone_xdr_raw`) — ≥25 fields (via the two-event split below).
- **Zscaler cloud-NSS** (`zscaler_cloudnss_raw`) — 17 fields.

**What was fixed to get there:**

- **Routing + gate + fields.** Each source's CEF `vendor`/`product` literals were corrected so the broker derives the right dataset; the modeling-rule gate field now carries an example that clears the filter (SentinelOne `eventType=Threat`, Windows `channel=System`, Salesforce `api_object_type=LoginEvent`, Zscaler `sourcetype=zscalernss-web`); and missing timestamp/routing fields were added. Each source's `how_to_use` (data-source drawer) names the exact CEF header, gate seed, and `xdm.*` fields the agent should populate.
- **Large-schema fix (the one that unblocked SentinelOne).** Fetching a 100+-field schema to drive a vendor-faithful simulation overflowed the agent's tool-result size cap, so the agent forwarded an incomplete field list and XDM silently capped. `data_sources_get_schema` gained a `compact` mode (returns only the keys the simulator consumes) so the whole field list survives. Without it, SentinelOne landed raw rows but mapped 0 XDM.
- **SentinelOne split note.** SentinelOne's field set is large enough that a single CEF event exceeds the 1500-byte UDP MTU; its `how_to_use` documents sending two complementary events (identity + threat composites) whose union maps the full Threat branch (≥25 fields).

The five are added to the CI-enforced validated manifest, so their modeling-rule gates are re-checked against the live `.xif` snapshots on every build and cannot silently drift.

Scenario 1 upgrade (code + content, installer unchanged) — re-run your existing installer.

## [v0.17.119] — 2026-06-01 — *The validated sources' XDM gates are now correct in the content and CI-enforced against the live modeling rules*

v0.17.118 made the simulate skill *seed* the modeling-rule gate. This release makes the *content* correct and adds a build-time guarantee that it can never silently drift again. Two bundled validated sources carried a gate example that matched **no** branch of their actual XSIAM modeling rule — so even a perfectly-seeding agent produced 0 XDM:

- **Alibaba ActionTrail** (`alibaba_action_trail_raw`) — `event_eventtype` example was `authentication`, but the rule gates on `event_eventtype in ("ApiCall", "ConsoleOperation", "AliyunServiceEvent", "PasswordReset")`. Fixed to `ApiCall`.
- **Azure Kubernetes Service** (`msft_azure_aks_raw`) — `category`/`Category` example was `access_control`, but the rule gates on `coalesce(category, Category) in ("kube-apiserver", …)`. Fixed to `kube-apiserver`.

Each fix flags the field's `description` as the modeling-rule GATE and adds a *"Make it map to XDM"* section to the source's `how_to_use` (visible in the data-source drawer) naming the exact gate values and the `xdm.*` fields that populate once the gate clears.

### What ships

- **Two corrected gate examples** (Alibaba, AKS) so a seeded simulation actually fires the modeling rule and saturates XDM.
- **Hardened `scripts/maintainer/reverse_engineer_gate.py`** — classifies every dataset's gate from the live `.xif`: RAW (`field in (...)` / `= "x"`), FUNCTION (a `coalesce`-derived field like `get_category` resolved back to its raw inputs `category`/`Category`), META (`_`-prefixed, stamped by the ingestion layer), COMPUTED (derived non-literal like `is_auth`), or UNCONDITIONAL (no leading filter, or a `not in (...)` catch-all branch makes every event map). Handles versioned rule files (`_1_3`, `_2_0`), `;`-separated multi-statement blocks, and comment-prefixed filters.
- **New CI guard `check_gate_fields_satisfied`** (`tooling/validate/validate_all.py`) — for every validated source with a RAW or FUNCTION gate, it asserts the bundled `data_source.yaml` carries a field whose `example` clears the gate, **re-deriving the gate from the live `.xif` each run — there is no static vendor→value table to drift.** META gates are allow-listed (not settable from the payload); unconditional/computed datasets are skipped. A hand-edited example the modeling rule no longer accepts now fails the build.

Across the 22 validated sources the guard sees: **7** have a RAW/FUNCTION gate (all now seed correctly), **1** is META (CloudTrail `_log_type` — broker-onboarding only), **1** is COMPUTED (CyberArk `is_auth`), and **13** map unconditionally. Code + content + CI-tooling change; re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1). **Completes the XDM-gate arc (#115).**

Refs #115.

## [v0.17.118] — 2026-06-01 — *Simulated logs now reliably populate XDM — gate-seeding is mandatory in the simulate skill*

End-to-end testing surfaced that simulated logs landed in their raw dataset but **did not populate the XDM data model** for several validated sources. Root cause (operator-corrected — it is **not** a CEF-vs-JSON transport issue): every XSIAM modeling rule opens with a `filter <field> = "<value>"` **gate**. If the simulated event does not carry that exact value, the rule never fires and `xdm.*` stays empty even though raw landing succeeds. Phantom's content already carried the gate guidance (each `data_source.yaml`'s gate field is flagged; the skill's L21 lesson named the classifier) — but seeding it was **advisory**, so the agent skipped it under load and XDM came back 0.

Proven live: Azure FlowLogs over the same CEF/syslog path went from **0 → 25+ `xdm.*` fields** the instant the agent seeded `observables_dict={"category":["NetworkSecurityGroupFlowEvent"]}`.

### What ships

- **Gate-seeding is now MANDATORY in `stream_simulate_to_xsiam`.** Step 4 carries a hard "⚠️ MANDATORY for XDM" directive: before firing `phantom_create_data_worker` for a verification run, the agent MUST seed the modeling-rule gate field — the schema field whose `description` begins *"Modeling-rule GATE — must equal …"* (or the `how_to_use` classifier) — via `observables_dict`. The previously-advisory `observables_dict` comment is now marked mandatory. Sources that gate on a `_`-prefixed META field (e.g. CloudTrail's `_log_type`, stamped by the broker at onboarding, not the payload) are documented as the exception.
- **New maintainer tool `scripts/maintainer/reverse_engineer_gate.py`** — extracts any data source's modeling-rule gate from its live `.xif` (reads the rule each run; no static mapping).

Net effect: "simulate `<vendor>` into XSIAM and verify XDM" now seeds the gate so the modeling rule fires — turning "raw lands, 0 XDM" into real XDM saturation. Agent-baked skill change; re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1). Part of the XDM-gate arc (#115).

Refs #115.

## [v0.17.117] — 2026-06-01 — *The agent reliably creates data workers even with large field schemas (Gemini tool-call recovery)*

End-to-end UI testing surfaced a silent failure on the chat path: ask the agent to *"simulate ServiceNow logs into XSIAM, confirm the fields landed, then stop the worker"* and it would correctly plan the run — read the data source, its full schema, the log destination — then **its `phantom_create_data_worker` call would vanish.** The agent printed the call as text inside its thinking block and the turn just ended. No worker, no events, no error — a plan that never executed.

Root cause is a quirk of Gemini's "thinking" models (which power the chat). When a tool call carries a very large argument — here the full ~70-field ServiceNow schema passed as `schema_override`, roughly 3,900 tokens — the model sometimes serializes the call as reasoning *text* instead of emitting it as a structured function call. Phantom's chat loop only dispatches structured calls, so the call was dropped and the turn ended silently. Small calls (and the very same tool with a small argument) were never affected — which is why 16 other tool calls in the same test session, including five worker-creations inside an attack-scenario run, all dispatched cleanly.

### What ships

- **Automatic tool-call recovery on the chat path** — when a turn returns no structured function call but the model's own reasoning shows it tried to make one (Gemini's `default_api` call marker leaked into the thinking text), Phantom retries that turn once with **forced function-calling** so the model re-emits a clean structured call, which then dispatches normally. The recovery is bounded (at most 2 retries per turn) and only fires on the leak condition — every normal turn is untouched, and extended-reasoning is left at full budget. `mcp/agent/app/api/chat/route.ts`.

Net effect: *"simulate ServiceNow into XSIAM, verify the fields landed, then stop the worker"* now runs end-to-end from the chat UI instead of stalling at the plan, even when the agent attaches a large vendor field schema.

Code-only change (agent); re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1).

Refs #114.

## [v0.17.116] — 2026-06-01 — *The agent routes simulated logs to the right dataset first try — and fires without re-asking*

End-to-end agent testing surfaced two skill-adherence gaps when the chat agent drives `phantom_create_data_worker`:

- **It invented prettified CEF product names.** For the "Okta — SSO" data source the agent passed `product="Okta SSO"` (the UI label) instead of the exact literal `product="Okta"`. The broker normalizes `<vendor>_<product>_raw`, so events routed to `okta_okta_sso_raw` (a dataset nothing parses) → 0 in `okta_sso_raw`.
- **It re-asked before firing.** Even on "send N Okta events to XSIAM **right now**", the agent paused with "— fire it?" and waited for a second confirmation before calling the tool (`phantom_create_data_worker` isn't gated, so this was its own over-caution).

### What ships

- **CEF-literal guardrail at the call site** — `phantom_create_data_worker`'s `vendor`/`product` arg descriptions (in `connector.yaml` + the Pydantic model) now state plainly: pass the EXACT `vendor`/`product` literal from `data_sources_get_schema`, never a prettified or display name. The Okta trap is called out explicitly (product is `Okta`, NOT `Okta SSO`; the SSO split is driven by the `eventType` discriminator, not the product). `observables_dict` now documents its parsing-rule-discriminator role with routing examples.
- **Skill: fire directly, don't re-confirm** — `stream_simulate_to_xsiam` Step 4 now states that an explicit send/stream/fire/simulate request IS the go-ahead: call the tool immediately, never pause to ask "shall I fire it?". It reinforces the never-invent-the-product rule with the Okta example.

Net effect: ask the agent to "send Okta SSO logs to XSIAM" and it fires once, with `product=Okta` + the right discriminator, landing in `okta_sso_raw`.

Connector + agent-baked skill change; re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1).

Refs #113.

## [v0.17.115] — 2026-06-01 — *Hot-fix: `phantom_kill_worker` cleanup error (v0.17.114 flatten regression)*

End-to-end agent testing through the chat API surfaced a regression introduced by v0.17.114's connector-tool flatten: **`phantom_kill_worker` returned `NameError: name 'request' is not defined` on every call.** The worker *did* stop (the GraphQL kill executed), but the tool reported an error — so an agent cleaning up after a simulation run (the `stream_simulate_to_xsiam` skill's "stop the worker" step) saw a failure and could leave workers emitting to the broker.

Root cause: v0.17.114 flattened the signature from `request: KillWorkerRequest` to a bare `worker_id` and rewrote the GraphQL variables, but missed the return's `payload.get("worker", request.worker_id)`. Python evaluates a `.get()` default eagerly, so the dropped `request` name raised on every call regardless of the GraphQL result.

### What ships

- **`phantom_kill_worker` cleanup is clean again** — the return uses the flat `worker_id` param instead of the dropped `request.worker_id`. Agent-driven worker cleanup now reports success, so no more "kill failed" confusion or leaked workers after a simulation.
- **CI guard hardened** — the connector flat-args validator (`tooling/validate/validate_all.py`) now also fails the build if any connector tool's **body** references a `request` its flattened signature no longer provides (nested-scope-aware, so a legitimate inner-function `request` param — e.g. a Playwright route handler — doesn't false-positive). This closes the exact gap that let the regression ship in v0.17.114: the check verified the *signature* matched `connector.yaml` but never scanned the body for leftover `request` references.
- **Bug-family audit** — this was the only incomplete-flatten instance across all connectors.

Connector-only change; re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1).

Closes #112.

## [v0.17.114] — 2026-05-31 — *Connector tools all use one calling convention — the agent reliably reaches every tool*

Testing v0.17.113 surfaced that the chat agent couldn't reliably call several connector tools — XQL queries, field discovery, and observable generation failed with a validation loop (the agent itself diagnosed *"the proxy expects `query`, the connector expects a `request` object"*), and it flailed between tool variants. Root cause: connector tools used **two different argument conventions** — every tool advertised flat named args in its `connector.yaml`, but most of the underlying Python functions still took a single nested `request: {...}` object, so the agent's (correct) flat call was rejected.

This release makes **every connector tool use ONE convention: flat named arguments** that exactly match its `connector.yaml`. The agent now calls XQL, field-info, observables, scenario, lookup, asset, and case tools the same way it already called `create_data_worker` — no guessing, no `{request:{…}}` wrapper.

### What ships

- **26 connector tools flattened** — xsiam (11), xlog (9), caldera (5), cortex-docs (1). For each, `connector.yaml` args, the Python signature, and the internal validation model are now identical. Behavior is unchanged (the Pydantic model is rebuilt internally from the kwargs).
- **Long-standing `connector.yaml` name drift fixed** in the same sweep — e.g. xlog `get_field_info` advertised `vendor` but the tool keys on `log_type`; caldera `create_adversary` advertised `ability_ids` vs the signature.
- **New CI guard** (`tooling/validate/validate_all.py` → `check_connector_tool_args_flat`): the build now fails if any connector tool takes a single `request:` model, or if a tool's signature doesn't match its `connector.yaml` — so this consistency can't silently drift again.

This is what unblocks the XQL-verification + field-discovery flows that failed in the skill/XQL test session. Connector-only change; re-run your existing installer (Scenario 1).

**Also in this release — factory-default contract, now CI-enforced.** A customer install is a verified clean slate: it ships the marketplace catalog (all data sources), connectors, skills, and the XQL knowledge base as *content*, with **nothing installed** (no data sources, no connector instances, no chats/jobs/runs, no log destinations, no tech stack). Two new validator guards lock this in:
- **`validated data-sources manifest`** — the set of data sources flagged `validated: true` must equal the canonical tested-list (`tooling/validate/validated_data_sources.txt`, the 22 end-to-end-smoked data sources). No unverified "Validated" pill can ship, and no tested one can be silently dropped.
- **`factory-default clean slate`** — fails the build if any operator-state SQLite db is committed into an image build context, or if the installer `.env` template pre-sets `WEBHOOK_ENDPOINT`/`WEBHOOK_KEY`/`TECHNOLOGY_STACK` (which would auto-seed state on first boot).

Verified for this release: validated set = exactly the 22 tested data sources; fresh-install clean-slate intact.

**Also — marketplace vendor logos restored.** The v0.17.25 upstream catalog refresh had accidentally deleted the theme-aware logo infrastructure (`baked/vendor_map.yaml` + `baked/vendor_svgs/`), so the marketplace had been falling back to generic logos since then. This release restores it and extends it to cover **all 259 catalog packs → 136 vendors**, each with light + dark SVG variants, so every data source in the marketplace renders its real vendor logo in both themes. The `pack theme variants` validator (which had been red since v0.17.25) is green again.

Refs #111.

## [v0.17.113] — 2026-05-31 — *Store-driven log destinations — the agent resolves where logs go (no hardcoded destinations)*

When you ask Phantom to generate logs, the agent now resolves the **destination** from your configured **Log Destinations** instead of inventing a hardcoded `udp:host:port` or silently falling back to a single env webhook. End-to-end:

- **Selection (the agent's job):** it works out the transport you asked for (syslog, or an XSIAM webhook) — or infers it from the data source's "How to simulate" notes — then lists your destinations. **Exactly one** match → used without asking. **Two or more** → the agent asks which (unless you named one by name or IP). **None** → the agent offers to create a *secretless* syslog destination for you, or guides you to add a credentialed one on `/log-destinations`.
- **Resolution (the platform's job):** the agent passes an opaque `logdest:<id>` handle. The MCP resolves it to the concrete address — and, for an XSIAM HTTP destination, injects the endpoint + auth key — **server-side**, before the worker starts. The auth key travels only MCP → connector → xlog; it never crosses the agent's tool surface, so the credential boundary holds.

So "send 50 FortiGate logs to syslog" just works when you have one syslog destination, and asks a clean question when you have two — with no destination secret ever passing through the chat.

### What ships

- **New agent tool** `log_destinations_create(name, host, port, protocol)` — the agent can create **secretless syslog** destinations on your behalf; credentialed types stay operator-only (REST + UI).
- **MCP resolver** at the connector-proxy chokepoint resolves `logdest:<id>` (syslog → `<proto>:<host>:<port>`; xsiam_http → `XSIAM_WEBHOOK` + injected url/key) before forwarding to the xlog connector.
- **xlog** `createDataWorker` + `createScenarioWorkerFromQuery` accept a per-destination `webhookUrl`/`webhookKey` (raw `Authorization`, never `Bearer`; env default preserved when absent).
- **Skills + system prompt** teach the resolution rule; `phantom_create_data_worker`'s destination docstring teaches the `logdest:<id>` reference (replacing the old "format the string yourself" guidance, which could never work for credentialed destinations).
- **Help docs:** `/help/architecture#log-destinations` gains a "Store-driven log generation (the resolution chokepoint)" section and corrects the dead `send()` drift; `/help/user#log-destinations-ux` rewrites "Asking the agent to send logs to a destination"; new `simulate-to-log-destination` journey.

Scope: covers `phantom_create_data_worker` (both its `createDataWorker` and `createScenarioWorkerFromQuery` routes). The file-scenario tool and the `webhook`/`splunk_hec` destination types are not yet wired into generation.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py`, `usecase/connector_loader.py` — `log_destinations_create`
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/log_destination_resolver.py`, `pkg/connector_proxy.py` — resolver + chokepoint
- `xlog/app/types/sender.py`, `xlog/app/schema.py` — webhook url/key passthrough (both worker paths)
- `bundles/spark/connectors/xlog/src/workers.py`, `connector.yaml` — forward `webhookUrl`/`webhookKey` + `logdest:<id>` docstring
- `bundles/spark/skills/generate-logs.md`, `run-scenario.md`, `mcp/agent/lib/system-prompt.ts`
- `mcp/agent/app/help/architecture/page.tsx`, `app/help/user/page.tsx`, `lib/journeys.ts`

Refs #110.

## [v0.17.112] — 2026-05-30 — *Agent: read-tool narration is now deterministically suppressed in chat*

Follow-up to v0.17.111. The system-prompt instruction alone did **not** stop the agent from narrating read-only tool calls — verified against the live agent, which still emitted "I'll call `log_destinations_list`." as response text. Models narrate tool calls by habit and don't reliably obey a "don't narrate" rule.

This adds a **deterministic filter** in the chat route: when a model turn's tool calls are **all non-gated** (read / auto-approved), the accompanying narration text is dropped from the stream and the saved transcript. The tool call + result still appear in the live telemetry; the actual answer (which arrives in a later, call-less turn) is untouched. Approval-gated actions keep their plain-language intent line — the filter only fires when every call in the turn is a read. The fallback preamble (synthesized when the model is silent before a gated call) is likewise scoped to gated calls so it can't re-introduce read narration.

### What ships

- `mcp/agent/app/api/chat/route.ts` — per-turn read-narration filter (correlates text with the turn's gated/non-gated tool calls) + gated-only fallback preamble.

Refs #109.

## [v0.17.111] — 2026-05-30 — *Agent: read-only tool calls are silent in chat (no more "I'll call …" noise)*

The chat agent no longer narrates read-only tool calls. Previously it announced every call ("I'll call `log_destinations_list`", "Let me check…") — but those calls and their results are already streamed into the **live telemetry / Tools panel**, so the chat duplicated what you could already see.

Now read-only / non-gated tools (`*_list`, `*_get`, `run_xql_query`, `data_sources_*`, `phantom_get_*`, …) are called **silently** and only the outcome is reported. Approval-gated actions still get a short plain-language intent line (the *why*) before their approval card — not the mechanical tool name.

### What ships

- System-prompt carve-out scoping tool-call narration to gated actions only; read-only calls go silent.

### Files

- `mcp/agent/lib/system-prompt.ts`

Refs #109.

## [v0.17.110] — 2026-05-30 — *Data sources: Azure WAF Category field passes schema validation*

The Azure WAF `Category` classifier field (added in v0.17.107) was declared `type: enum` but **without `enum_values`**, so it failed `data_source.schema.json` validation — the AI Layer validator's "bundled data_source.yaml validate" check was red (1 of 288 YAMLs failing). This adds the two valid Front Door log categories so all 288 bundled YAMLs validate again and the field carries correct enum metadata.

### What ships

- `Category` enum field now carries `enum_values: [FrontDoorAccessLog, FrontDoorWebApplicationFirewallLog]` — restores green bundled-YAML schema validation.

### Scope note

This fixes the field's schema validity and metadata. It does **not** by itself close Azure WAF's XDM-0 residual from the #106 campaign: that smoke already forced a valid `Category` value (via `observables_dict`) and still produced 0 XDM rows, so the residual has a deeper cause (the modeling rule's classification isn't satisfied by the Category value alone) and remains open for follow-up.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/data-sources/AzureWAF__AzureWAF__msft_azure_waf_raw/data_source.yaml`

Refs #106, #107.

## [v0.17.109] — 2026-05-30 — *UI: API-key Create panel is theme-aware + roomier*

The **Create API Key** slide-over now respects the light theme. Previously its panel background and body text were hardcoded dark, so the modal stayed dark even when the rest of the app was in light mode. It now routes through the same Material 3 theme tokens as every other surface, so background and text flip correctly with the sidebar theme toggle.

The create panel is also **wider** — about half the viewport (up from a fixed 480px) — and lays the scope picker out in **two columns**, so the label, scopes, and Generate button all fit without scrolling. The existing-key detail panel keeps its compact width.

### What ships

- **Theme-aware create + detail slide-overs** — the hardcoded dark panel background and `white/*` utility colors are replaced with semantic `on-surface` / `surface-container` tokens that follow `[data-theme="light"]`. Zero visual change in dark mode.
- **Wider create panel** — `50vw` (clamped to `600–960px`), with a two-column scope grid; no vertical scroll on a normal display.
- The Create-Key button keeps white text on its blue gradient (correct in both themes).

### Files

- `mcp/agent/app/api-keys/page.tsx` — theme tokens, panel width, two-column scope grid.

Refs #108.

## [v0.17.108] — 2026-05-30 — *Auth: API-key bearer access to the agent API*

You can now authenticate to the agent's API surface (`/api/chat` + `/api/agent/*` + `/api/skills/*`) with an **API key** instead of the username/password session cookie. Mint a scoped, revocable key in **/api-keys**, then send `Authorization: Bearer phantom_ak_…` — useful for scripts, schedulers, CI, and any programmatic integration that drives the agent.

Phantom already had the API-key system (mint/revoke UI, hashed storage, per-key scopes) for the MCP REST surface; this release extends it to the Next.js agent surface.

### What ships

- **Coarse scopes** chosen at mint time: `agent:read` (GET routes + chat read), `agent:write` (mutations + `/api/chat`), `agent:*` (both). `*` remains full admin.
- **Security invariant:** credential-management routes (`/api/agent/providers/*`, `/api/agent/instances/*`, `/api/agent/api-keys/*`) **reject API keys even with `agent:*`** — they stay session-cookie-only, so a leaked key can never mint more keys or read provider secrets.
- The session-cookie path for the human UI is **unchanged** (zero regression).

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/ui_auth.py` — `POST /api/v1/ui/auth/verify_key` (MCP_TOKEN-gated).
- `mcp/agent/lib/auth-store.ts` — `validateApiKey()` (verify_key loopback, 30s cache).
- `mcp/agent/lib/agent-scopes.ts` — pure coarse-scope + credential-route helper.
- `mcp/agent/middleware.ts` — API-key bearer branch before the cookie check.
- `mcp/agent/app/api-keys/page.tsx` — `agent:read/write/*` scope options.

Closes #107.

## [v0.17.107] — 2026-05-29 — *Data sources: XDM-classifier seeding guidance for the validated sources*

Content + skill pass that turns the v0.17.104–106 generator fixes into reliable XDM saturation for the chat-driven simulation path. Many Cortex modeling rules gate XDM emission on a **classifier field** — `filter <field> in (<allow-list>)` — so an event with a random classifier value lands raw but produces **zero** XDM rows. The deep-dive smoke campaign measured the exact value each validated source needs and confirmed the lift (e.g. Okta 0 → 46, Azure Flow Logs 0 → 48, CyberArk 0 → 40 XDM).

### What ships

- **`how_to_use` classifier guidance** for the sources that needed it — Azure Flow Logs (`category=NetworkSecurityGroupFlowEvent`), Azure WAF (`Category=FrontDoorAccessLog`), Azure AKS (corrected from the wrong `kube-audit` to `kube-apiserver`). The other enum-gated sources already named their classifier field.
- **Azure WAF `Category` field** added to the schema (it was missing, so the override couldn't seed it).
- **`stream_simulate_to_xsiam` skill** updated: a new lesson **L21** (read the MR's classifier and seed a valid value via `observables_dict` — the #1 XDM lever) with a per-vendor value table; **L22** (pass the FULL `fields[]` incl. dotted leaves — composites synthesize from them); **L20/L13 corrected** (composites synthesize as real JSON since v0.17.104 — nested-JSON vendors no longer cap at ~10); the validated-vendor table's AKS row fixed.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/data-sources/{AzureFlowLogs,AzureWAF,AzureKubernetesServices}__…/data_source.yaml` — `how_to_use` classifier guidance; azure_waf `Category` field.
- `bundles/spark/mcp/skills/workflows/stream_simulate_to_xsiam.md` — L21/L22 + corrections.

Refs #106.

## [v0.17.106] — 2026-05-29 — *Simulation: the xlog connector forwards `observables_dict` on the vendor-faithful path*

Connector fix that completes the v0.17.105 wiring. The xlog **connector** (`phantom_create_data_worker`) builds the GraphQL mutation it sends to the xlog service. When a `schema_override` is supplied (the vendor-faithful streaming path the simulation UI + skill use), the connector built the mutation variables with `type/count/interval/destination/vendor/product/schemaOverride` but **omitted `observablesDict` entirely** — so the forced-field overrides never left the connector, regardless of the v0.17.105 service-side fix.

The two fixes compose into one working path:
- **v0.17.105** (xlog service): the `createDataWorker` resolver reads `observables_dict` and threads it into `OverrideSender`.
- **v0.17.106** (xlog connector, this release): the `schema_override` branch now includes `observablesDict` in the mutation variables, so the value actually reaches the service.

With both, a classifier value seeded via `observables_dict` (e.g. `eventType=user.session.start` for Okta, `category=NetworkSecurityGroupFlowEvent` for Azure Flow Logs) reaches the synthesized event — letting modeling rules that gate on `filter <field> in (…)` classify and populate XDM, and letting shared-CEF-header siblings (Okta SSO) route to the correct dataset.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/connectors/xlog/src/workers.py` — `observablesDict` added to the `schema_override` mutation variables.
- `bundles/spark/connectors/xlog/tests/` — regression test asserting the schema-override path carries `observablesDict`.

Refs #106.

## [v0.17.105] — 2026-05-29 — *Simulation: streaming workers honor forced-field overrides (`observables_dict`)*

Generator fix in `xlog`. When you stream a simulated source, `observables_dict` lets you pin specific field values — a threat-intel IP, a username, or a **classifier value** that a parsing/modeling rule keys on. The *inline* generator already honored these; the *streaming* worker path (`phantom_create_data_worker`) silently dropped them. Two consequences fixed here:

- **Sibling datasets that share one CEF header now route.** Okta's System-Log and SSO streams both carry the `Okta`→`Okta` header, so the parsing rule splits them by reading `eventType`. Without the override reaching the event, every Okta event fell to the System-Log dataset and SSO never landed. (Most multi-dataset packs — the O365 workloads, Entra audit vs sign-in — carry *distinct* CEF product literals and already routed by header; this fixes the shared-header case.)
- **Modeling rules that gate on a classifier value now fire.** Many MRs open with `filter <field> in (<allow-list>)`. A randomly-generated value misses the list and the rule emits zero XDM. Seeding a valid value via `observables_dict` lets the rule classify and map.

Two code fixes enable both:
- **Streaming workers now apply `observable_overrides`.** The `OverrideSender` path builds them exactly like the inline path and threads them into record generation.
- **List-valued overrides unwrap to a scalar.** `observables_dict` conventionally maps a field to a *list* of candidates (`{"eventType": ["user.authentication.sso"]}`). The generator now picks one element, so the field lands as the scalar the rule's filter matches — not a bracketed `["…"]` literal.

### Files

- `xlog/app/schema.py` — `createDataWorker` builds + passes `observable_overrides` into the `OverrideSender` branch.
- `xlog/app/dynamic_schema.py` — `_generate_value` unwraps list-valued overrides (rosetta-consistent pick-one).
- `xlog/tests/test_dynamic_schema.py` — single- + multi-element list-override coverage.

Refs #106.

## [v0.17.104] — 2026-05-29 — *Simulation: nested-JSON (composite) fields generate real objects*

Generator fix in the log-synthesis backend (`xlog`). When you simulate a data source whose schema declares a **composite** field — a `type: json` object like Okta's `actor`, AWS WAF's `httpRequest`, ServiceNow's `record`, or Azure AD's `targetResources` — the simulator was emitting a *random string* in that field's place. The XSIAM modeling rule reads those composites with `json_extract_scalar(actor, "$.id")`, which returns null against a random string, so every XDM field sourced from a nested object stayed empty. Flat-field vendors saturated; nested-JSON vendors capped near zero.

Now the generator **synthesizes a real nested JSON object** for composite fields by folding the schema's dotted-leaf children into their parent: `actor.id`, `actor.alternateId`, `actor.type` become `actor = {"id": …, "alternateId": …, "type": …}`, each leaf typed and value-generated. Arrays-of-objects, leaf-only composites (no declared parent), and deeply-nested paths all materialize. On the CEF wire the composite serializes as **compact** JSON (`actor={"id":"…"}` — no internal spaces) so it survives CEF's space-delimited extension parsing as one value, which is exactly what the modeling rule's `json_extract_scalar` then parses.

This unblocks XDM saturation for every nested-JSON vendor in the validated set — the dominant blocker in the deep-dive smoke campaign.

### Files

- `xlog/app/dynamic_schema.py` — `_build_nested` helper; composite-parent grouping + folding + leaf-only emission in `generate_records_with_override`.
- `xlog/app/override_sender.py` — `_flatten_extension` serializes dict/list extension values with compact separators.
- `xlog/tests/test_dynamic_schema.py`, `xlog/tests/test_override_sender_format.py` — composite-synthesis + compact-CEF coverage.

Refs #106.

## [v0.17.103] — 2026-05-29 — *Data sources: consistent "how to simulate" across all validated sources*

Content pass. Four validated sources — **Jira, ServiceNow, Qualys, Proofpoint TAP** — had a `how_to_use` that jumped from the shared transport header straight to the structured bullets with no vendor-specific lead, so they read thinner than the others in the drawer. Added a vendor-specific routing/simulation lead to each (what the source captures, the discriminator or timestamp gotcha, the dominant event shape), matching the Okta / Alibaba / AWS CloudTrail bar.

Now **all 22 validated data sources** open with a substantive "how to simulate this" narrative. Every validated technical specific (CEF header, discriminators, timestamp formats, MR patterns, XDM ceilings) is unchanged — the leads only *surface* facts already in each source's bullets.

The 18 other validated sources already carried vendor-specific routing guidance and were left as-is (no padding).

### Files

- `scripts/maintainer/enhance_validated_vendor_yamls.py` — `routing_notes` added to 4 `ENTRIES`.
- 4 regenerated `data_source.yaml` (Jira, ServiceNow, Qualys, Proofpoint TAP) — `how_to_use` only.

Closes #105.

## [v0.17.102] — 2026-05-29 — *Fix: data source drawer opens for every catalog dataset*

Hot-fix. Opening a data source's drawer for a bundled dataset that cortex-content doesn't enumerate (e.g. Okta's SSO stream `okta_sso_raw`) errored with *"Could not load schema — dataset not found … available: [okta_okta_raw]"*. The Browse catalog lists every bundled dataset (it reads the YAML loader), but the schema drawer resolved only via the installed store → cortex-content, so a second dataset under one pack+rule that cortex doesn't expose had no schema to show.

### Fix

The schema surfaces now consult the **bundled/user YAML loader** (the same source of truth the catalog uses) before falling back to cortex-content:
- UI schema route: installed store → **bundled/user YAML** → cortex-content preview.
- Agent tool `data_sources_get_schema`: same YAML fallback (so the agent can read any catalog source's schema, not just installed ones).

`okta_okta_raw` (System Log, 59 fields) and `okta_sso_raw` (SSO, 52 fields) are two distinct validated Okta streams; both now open with their full field schema. General fix — applies to any YAML-backed dataset cortex doesn't enumerate.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/data_sources.py` — `_yaml_ds_to_schema_payload` helper; YAML path in the schema route + the `data_sources_get_schema` tool.

Closes #104.

## [v0.17.101] — 2026-05-29 — *Data sources: export any version (versioning arc complete)*

The final piece of the data-source versioning arc (v0.17.99 edit + versioning, v0.17.100 history + rollback). You can now **export any version** of a data source's YAML — not just the current one.

### What ships

- **Export any version.** Each row in the version-history panel has an **Export** button that downloads that version's YAML (`<dataset>.v<n>.yaml`).
- **Default export = current content.** The card's main Export button now downloads the **current version** — after an edit, that's the edited content (matching the drawer + the loader overlay), not the original shipped file. This fixes a quiet inconsistency where Export kept returning the pristine file even after you'd edited the source.
- **REST:** `GET .../export?version=n` (verbatim version snapshot; default = current).

### Capability acceptance criteria (full arc — COMPLETE)

- [x] Edit a system source's `how_to_use` from the UI (v0.17.99).
- [x] The original is preserved as version 1; re-editing increments the version (v0.17.99).
- [x] View a source's full version history (v0.17.100).
- [x] Roll back to any prior version; history is preserved (v0.17.100).
- [x] Export a specific version; default export = current (v0.17.101).

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/data_sources.py` — `_resolve_export_content` helper + `?version=` on the export route.
- `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/data-sources/[pack]/[rule]/[dataset]/export/route.ts` — forwards `?version=`.
- `mcp/agent/app/data-sources/page.tsx` — per-version Export action in `VersionHistoryModal`.

Refs #103.

## [v0.17.100] — 2026-05-29 — *Data sources: version history + rollback*

Builds on v0.17.99's editing + versioning foundation. You can now **see a data source's full version history** and **roll back** to any prior version — directly from the card or via the chat agent. Rollback is **non-destructive**: it brings the chosen version's content back as a new current version while keeping every version in history, so you can always roll forward again. This completes the **edit + roll back** capability (export-by-version is the last piece of the arc).

### What ships

- **History panel.** The data source drawer gains a **History** button (beside Edit guidance, for system sources). It lists every version — version number, author (`bundle-baseline` for the original v1, `operator`/`agent` for edits), change note, and timestamp, with the newest tagged **Current**. **View** expands any version's full YAML snapshot read-only.
- **Roll back.** Each non-current version has a **Roll back** action. Rolling back to vK copies vK's content forward as a new current version; vK and the intervening versions stay in history.
- **Agent tools.** `data_sources_list_versions` (inspect history) and `data_sources_rollback` (revert) — catalog-side, so the chat agent can do it too (&ldquo;show me the ServiceNow version history&rdquo;, &ldquo;roll ServiceNow back to version 1&rdquo;).
- **REST:** `GET .../versions`, `GET .../versions/{n}`, `POST .../rollback {version}`.

### Capability acceptance criteria (full arc)

- [x] Edit a system source's `how_to_use` from the UI (v0.17.99).
- [x] The original is preserved as version 1; re-editing increments the version (v0.17.99).
- [x] View a source's full version history (UI + agent tool).
- [x] Roll back to any prior version; history is preserved (roll-forward works).
- [ ] *(next, SP-6)* Export a specific version via a version picker.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/data_source_versions_store.py` — `rollback(..., author=)` parametrized.
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/data_sources.py` — `_apply_rollback` helper, `versions` / `versions/{n}` / `rollback` REST routes, `data_sources_list_versions` + `data_sources_rollback` agent tools.
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py` — registers the two new tools.
- `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/data-sources/[pack]/[rule]/[dataset]/{versions,versions/[version],rollback}/route.ts` — Next.js proxies.
- `mcp/agent/app/data-sources/page.tsx` — `VersionHistoryModal` + the History button.

Refs #102.

## [v0.17.99] — 2026-05-29 — *Data sources: edit any source's guidance, with versioning (foundation)*

You can now edit a **system (bundled) data source's** &ldquo;How to simulate&rdquo; guidance directly from the card — previously only operator-uploaded sources were editable. Every edit is **versioned**: the shipped file on disk is never modified, each save is a snapshot served as an overlay, and the pristine original is always preserved as **version 1** so nothing is lost.

This is the **foundation** of the data-source versioning arc. This release ships editing + the version store + the loader overlay. The **history view + rollback** UI and the **export-by-version** picker follow in the next releases of the arc.

### What ships

- **Edit guidance from the card.** The data source detail drawer gains an **Edit guidance** button for system sources (in both Browse-preview and Installed states). It opens a `how_to_use` editor + optional change note, with a banner explaining the override semantics. **No delete** — editing is always additive.
- **Versioning overlay.** A new version store (`data_source_versions.db`) holds every version of every edited source as a full-YAML snapshot. The loader resolves each source as *store-current → user YAML → bundle YAML*, so an edit overlays the file without touching it. Degrades gracefully to the file on any error.
- **v1 = the original.** The first edit of a source snapshots the pristine doc as version 1 (`bundle-baseline`) before writing the edit as version 2. Unedited sources cost zero storage.
- **Agent can edit too.** A new `data_sources_edit` MCP tool lets the chat agent edit a source's guidance on your behalf (&ldquo;update the ServiceNow how-to-use to mention our broker&rdquo;). Catalog-side — no secrets.
- **Validation before write.** Edits are validated against `data_source.schema.json` + field-name uniqueness; a rejected edit writes no version.

### Capability acceptance criteria (full arc)

- [x] Edit a system source's `how_to_use` from the UI; the drawer reflects it immediately.
- [x] The original is preserved as version 1; re-editing increments the version.
- [x] The agent can edit via `data_sources_edit`.
- [ ] *(next)* View version history + roll back to a prior version (non-destructive).
- [ ] *(next)* Export a specific version via a version picker.

### Files

- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/data_source_versions_store.py` — new version store (SQLite, snapshot/get_current/list_versions/rollback).
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/data_sources_yaml_loader.py` — `_apply_version_overlay` in `list_all()`; store-current overlay resolution.
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/data_sources.py` — `_apply_edit` shared helper, `PUT .../edit` REST route, `data_sources_edit` agent tool.
- `bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py` — wires the version store singleton at boot; registers `data_sources_edit`.
- `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/data-sources/[pack]/[rule]/[dataset]/edit/route.ts` — Next.js proxy.
- `mcp/agent/app/data-sources/page.tsx` — `EditDataSourceModal` + the Edit guidance button.

Refs #101.

The login screen's animated capability cycle ("Phantom can simulate logs · create workers · … · orchestrate workflows") had one entry noticeably longer than its siblings. Shortened `orchestrate workflows` → `run workflows` so the rotating text reads at an even length.

## [v0.17.97] — 2026-05-29 — *Data source guidance: one `how_to_use` section, no hardcoded broker address*

A data-source card's "How to simulate" guidance carried TWO overlapping sections — `## Simulating this data source` (which hardcoded the lab broker `10.10.0.8:514`) and `## Sending these logs to Cortex XSIAM` — that re-explained the same CEF vendor+product → dataset routing twice, with a contradictory "destination-neutral" preamble in between.

### What ships

- **Merged the two into one** `## Sending these logs to Cortex XSIAM` section across all 22 validated data sources.
- **Removed the hardcoded broker IP + port** (`10.10.0.8:514`, `:514`). The broker is now described generically ("your XSIAM broker's syslog destination") because the address is per-environment and changes.
- **Preserved every vendor's specific guidance** — Required CEF header, discriminator / routing notes (e.g. Okta's `eventType` split), MR pattern, Composite-field packing, saturation strategy, and the Verify query.
- **Fixed the generator** (`enhance_validated_vendor_yamls.py`) to emit the single merged section and refreshed its post-dedup slug keys, so any future regeneration stays clean.
