### Forbidden post-v0.5.58

- **Don't re-introduce the in-modal Test Connection panel.** The dual-action create+test inside one modal is what produced the original confusion. Per-instance test goes on the instance card, after the modal closes. If a future use case argues for a "try-before-save" flow, that's a separate spec — open an issue, don't resurrect the deleted code path.
- **Don't probe `docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/`** (the HTML root) or `github.com/...` (the HTML repo page) — both are brittle to upstream layout changes. Probes hit API endpoints with stable contracts.
- **Don't add fake credentials** (`apiToken`, `apiKey`) to public-API connectors just so the test-connection message "feels real." cortex-docs and cortex-content are genuinely unauthenticated; faking auth misleads operators.

---

## [v0.5.57] — 2026-05-16

**Caldera kill-chain content is now factory state — baked into the `phantom-caldera` image at build time and auto-loaded on container start.** Plus an **expansion from 12 to 20 steps** that adds the high-signal XDR detection techniques most SOCs care about: **LSASS memory dump via comsvcs.dll (T1003.001 — the marquee EDR rule)**, **Defender real-time disable + exclusions (T1562.001)**, **certutil-decode LOLBin (T1140)**, **Registry Run key + Scheduled task persistence (T1547.001 + T1053.005)**, and **Security event log clear (T1070.001 — Security 1102 fires unconditionally)**. The kill chain went from "12 steps that mostly fire user-account events" to "20 steps spanning 11 tactics with multiple marquee EDR signals."

### Why

v0.5.55 shipped the kill-chain content as a bundle in `bundles/spark/caldera-content/`. v0.5.56 shipped the agent skill that drives it. But operators still had to **manually paste each YAML into the Caldera UI** before they could run the chain — a 15-minute onboarding step that defeats the "fresh install just works" promise that the rest of Phantom maintains. And the 12-step chain, while structurally complete, generated relatively few high-signal XDR events: most steps were user-account creates + registry mutations, with the only marquee signal being the cross-host lateral move.

v0.5.57 fixes both problems together because they're the same concept: **make the kill chain "factory state" in two senses — auto-loaded out of the box, AND noisy enough that operators can actually exercise their detection rules against it.**

### What ships

#### Build-time overlay: `bundles/spark/caldera-content/` → image

The Caldera image is built from `third_party/caldera/` — a git submodule pointing at upstream `mitre/caldera.git`. Files committed there become "untracked-in-submodule" and never reach our main branch. The fix: keep the YAMLs in our own repo (`bundles/spark/caldera-content/`, tracked normally) and add a **CI overlay step** in `.github/workflows/build-caldera.yml` that copies them into `third_party/caldera/data/` just before `docker build` runs. The `ADD . .` directive in the Caldera Dockerfile then includes them in the build context, and Caldera's `data_svc.py` auto-scans `/usr/src/app/data/{abilities,adversaries}/*` on container start (server/app/service/data_svc.py lines 30-31). Net result: the customer compose's `phantom-caldera` digest contains the kill chain pre-loaded; operators see the adversary in the UI on first login, no manual import.

The workflow path-filter expanded to `third_party/caldera/**` OR `bundles/spark/caldera-content/**` so edits to our YAMLs trigger a rebuild. Submodule pin bumps still trigger rebuilds the same way they did before.

#### 8 new abilities for XDR-rich detection (`bundles/spark/caldera-content/abilities/`)

| New ability | Tactic | Technique | XDR signal |
|---|---|---|---|
| `03-discovery/system-info-burst.yml` | discovery | T1082 + T1018 | 4-LOLBin burst (`systeminfo`, `arp -a`, `netstat -ano`, `route print`) in 5s window |
| `03-discovery/network-share-discovery.yml` | discovery | T1135 | `net share`, `net view`, `Get-SmbShare`, `Get-SmbConnection`, `Get-SmbSession` |
| `04-credential-access/lsass-minidump-comsvcs.yml` | credential-access | T1003.001 | **`rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll, MiniDump` — Sysmon EID 10 (handle to lsass.exe) — marquee EDR rule** |
| `05-defense-evasion/disable-defender-realtime.yml` | defense-evasion | T1562.001 | **`Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $true` + 3 exclusion paths — Defender's own 5001/5007** |
| `05-defense-evasion/certutil-decode-payload.yml` | defense-evasion | T1140 | `certutil.exe -decode` of base64 blob — LOLBin abuse |
| `06-persistence/registry-run-key.yml` | persistence | T1547.001 | `HKCU:\...\Run\PhantomUpdater` value set — Sysmon EID 13 |
| `06-persistence/scheduled-task-logon.yml` | persistence | T1053.005 | `schtasks /create /tn PhantomMaintenance /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM` — Security 4698 |
| `13-cleanup/clear-security-eventlog.yml` | defense-evasion | T1070.001 | **`wevtutil cl Security` — Security 1102 fires unconditionally (Microsoft built it specifically to flag log clearing)** |

Each ability follows the existing pattern: PowerShell command base64-encoded as UTF-16 LE (Caldera strips whitespace from `command:` fields), `timeout: 120`, deterministic UUID derived from name, **lab-safe** (LSASS dump file deleted immediately, Defender disable is reversible with `-DisableRealtimeMonitoring $false`, persistence payloads are no-op log appends, no actual encryption or exfil).

#### Updated adversary profile (`bundles/spark/caldera-content/adversaries/phantom-phishing-kill-chain.yml`)

Renamed to `Phantom phishing -> ransomware kill chain (cross-host, expanded)` — the adversary ID (`f81c14fe-6730-4215-bc95-e8eaca1530ab`) is preserved. `atomic_ordering` expanded from 12 to 20 entries with the 8 new ability UUIDs woven in at narratively-correct points (discovery burst right after the user-account discovery, LSASS dump right after cached creds, Defender tamper + certutil right after UAC bypass, Run-key + scheduled task right after the local-account create, event-log clear as the final post-defacement cleanup step).

#### Updated skill (`bundles/spark/mcp/skills/workflows/run_phishing_kill_chain.md`)

Rewritten to reference the 20-step chain. ATT&CK frontmatter expanded from 8 to 11 tactics (defense-evasion is now its own line). Procedure step D loop count went from 12 → 20. Step E (per-step decode) adds 4 headline-step callouts (LSASS / Defender / lateral / event-log-clear) for the agent to highlight. Step G renumbered the parser-false-negative steps (was 5+6, now 8+11 in the new ordering) and added expected-failure callouts for the LSASS dump (Defender blocks the write) and Defender disable (Tamper Protection blocks the call) — both are detection-equivalent successes. Cleanup section expanded with a full PowerShell snippet for reverting all the new artefacts. Variations section updated to point at the new YAML path (`bundles/spark/caldera-content/...` not `third_party/caldera/data/...`).

#### Updated runbook README (`bundles/spark/caldera-content/README.md`)

Rewritten preamble: "this content is now baked into the image, auto-loaded by Caldera's data_svc on boot — no manual import needed." Added 8 new rows to both the "What's in this kill chain" and "XDR detection coverage" tables. Added a v0.5.57 lab-safety subsection covering the new mutations (Defender restore, persistence cleanup, event-log naturally repopulates). Kept the pre-v0.5.57 manual-import instructions at the bottom for operators still on older releases.

### Smoke-test surface

After fresh install of the v0.5.57 dev installer:

- **Adversary auto-loaded**: navigate to https://localhost:8889 → Caldera UI → Adversaries → find `Phantom phishing -> ransomware kill chain (cross-host, expanded)` with 20 atomic_ordering entries (no "Missing ability" UUIDs).
- **Abilities auto-loaded**: Caldera UI → Abilities → filter by tactic — all 14 custom abilities present (3 bootstrap + 11 in-chain). Stockpile abilities still present.
- **End-to-end run**: with two Windows agents checked in (group=red + group=victim, bootstraps already run from a prior session), tell the phantom-agent in chat "run the phishing kill chain." It loads the skill, asks for confirmation, fires the operation, polls to completion in ~10-14 min, returns the 20-step summary table. Step 7 (LSASS) and step 20 (event-log clear) should be the visually-loudest steps in the output.
- **Image digest changed**: `docker inspect /opt/phantom/docker-compose.yml | grep phantom-caldera` shows a new digest vs the v0.5.56 install.
- **No regression** in the rest of the stack: agent UI loads, MCP tools enumerate, no broken services.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.57

- **Don't commit Phantom-specific YAMLs directly to `third_party/caldera/`.** That's a submodule. Anything dropped there becomes orphan untracked content. The build-time overlay pattern is the architecture — keep YAMLs in `bundles/spark/caldera-content/`, the workflow handles the rest.
- **Don't add manual-import instructions** to operator-facing docs going forward. The auto-load path is THE path; the pre-v0.5.57 manual flow is kept in the README's bottom section as historical-reference only. Net-new operator-facing references should always say "auto-loaded on container start."
- **Don't ship abilities without lab-safety language in the description.** The new noisy techniques (LSASS dump, Defender disable, event-log clear) are detection bait but lab-safe; each YAML's description names the safety mechanism (dump deleted, reversible via Set-MpPreference, etc). New techniques follow the same pattern.
- **Don't expand the chain past 25 steps.** Runtime scales linearly with the agent beacon interval (30-60s/step). The 20-step chain runs in ~10-14 min; at 25+ steps operators start losing patience between confirmation and result, which encourages skipping the read of per-step output (the most valuable part).
- **Don't auto-undo the Defender disable or event-log clear in cleanup.** Those persistent states are themselves part of the detection narrative — operators may want SIEM rules to alert on the disabled state over time. Cleanup is operator-driven, not auto.

---

## [v0.5.56] — 2026-05-16

**New skill `run_phishing_kill_chain` under `bundles/spark/mcp/skills/workflows/`** — pairs with v0.5.55's bundled Caldera content. The phantom-agent can now drive the 12-step phishing → ransomware kill chain end-to-end from a chat prompt: verify prereqs, fire the operation, poll to completion, decode per-step output, produce a SOC-ready telemetry summary.

### Why

v0.5.55 shipped the Caldera content (abilities + adversary profile). v0.5.56 ships the operator-facing entrypoint: a skill the agent can invoke when the operator says "run the phishing kill chain" or similar natural-language request. Without the skill, the agent would need to plan the orchestration ad-hoc per request — fragile + slow. The skill bakes in the right sequence, the right tool calls, the right error handling, and the prereq verification.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/skills/workflows/run_phishing_kill_chain.md`

The first skill in the previously-empty `workflows/` category. Structured per the foundation skill convention:

- **Frontmatter** — name, displayName, category, icon, description, source, loadingMode, ATT&CK mapping (8 tactics, 12 techniques)
- **Purpose + When to run / NOT run** — explicit operator guidance on the boundary
- **Prerequisites** — 3 verification steps the agent must perform before firing
- **Procedure** — 7 sub-steps (A through G), each naming the specific Caldera tool calls + the expected response shape
- **Forbidden** — 5 explicit constraints (no auto-bootstrapping, no auto-import, no auto-cleanup of evidence markers, no firing without operator confirmation, no claiming success when status=1 false negatives are present)
- **Cleanup** — what the chain leaves on both hosts + how the operator removes it
- **Variations** — alternative chains the operator might want (single-host, different victim IP, replay one step)
- **Telemetry signatures** — 12-row table mapping each step to the Sysmon / Windows Security event signatures the operator should see in their SIEM

The skill is `loadingMode: on-demand` (not auto-loaded into every chat context) — the agent fetches it when the operator's prompt matches the skill's intent. `locked: false` so operators can edit or fork.

### How operators use this

Operator says one of:
- "Run the phishing kill chain"
- "Fire the Caldera attack chain"
- "Execute the phantom phishing → ransomware adversary"

The agent loads the skill, runs the procedure: verifies the agents are checked in, finds the adversary, creates the operation, sets it running against `group=red`, polls each step, decodes outputs, and produces the per-step + lateral-evidence + telemetry-signature summary.

The agent **always asks operator confirmation** before firing the chain (Forbidden rule). Operator confirms, the chain runs, the agent reports completion in ~6-10 minutes.

### Skill-level forbidden list (codified in the skill itself)

The skill explicitly prohibits:
1. Auto-running the bootstrap abilities (they modify host security state)
2. Auto-creating/modifying the adversary profile (drift risk)
3. Auto-deleting the marker artefacts left on xdragent2 (operator-visible evidence)
4. Firing without operator confirmation (real registry mutations + user creation involved)
5. Reporting "success" without calling out the Caldera-parser false-negatives on steps 5 and 6

These constraints live IN the skill markdown so any agent reading the skill picks them up automatically. The agent's chat-route's existing safety machinery is the second line of defence; the skill's "Forbidden" section is the first.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.56

- **No bundling the skill into `on_load` mode.** It's `on-demand` precisely because it has side effects (real Caldera operation). Promoting it to auto-load would mean every chat context could trigger it accidentally via prompt injection. Stays operator-intent-driven.
- **No removing the Forbidden section from the skill markdown.** Skill safety constraints are AS authoritative as the procedure — operators downloading or forking the skill must see them.
- **No moving the skill out of `workflows/` to a more prominent category.** The category groups workflow skills (high-level operator-driven orchestrations); foundation / scenarios / validation each have distinct semantics. Workflows is the right home.

---

## [v0.5.55] — 2026-05-16

**New bundled `bundles/spark/caldera-content/` directory** — 9 custom Caldera abilities + 1 adversary profile + a runbook README that together emulate a complete phishing → ransomware kill chain across two Windows agents with **real cross-host lateral movement** (SMB + WMI + WinRM Negotiate). Operators import the YAMLs into their Caldera instance and have a known-good attack sequence to exercise SOC detection rules against.

### Why

Operators running Caldera against Windows lab targets typically build their own ability + adversary content from scratch — laborious, easy to get wrong, and no two operators end up with the same telemetry baseline. This release ships a curated, tested adversary that:

- Covers 8 MITRE ATT&CK tactics across 12 atomic steps
- Uses 6 custom abilities (lab-tuned for workgroup Windows Server 2022) + 6 stockpile abilities
- Includes the **only piece that's actually hard**: real cross-host lateral movement via SMB+WMI+WinRM with workgroup auth (the kind that requires getting `LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy`, password complexity, TrustedHosts, and `AllowUnencrypted` all aligned)
- Comes with a 200-line README explaining prerequisites, install, telemetry expectations, and lab-safety notes

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/caldera-content/`

```
caldera-content/
├── README.md
├── abilities/
│   ├── 00-bootstrap/
│   │   ├── bootstrap-xdragent2-victim.yml    ← run once on group=victim
│   │   └── bootstrap-xdragent-attacker.yml   ← run once on group=red
│   ├── 01-initial-access/
│   │   └── phishing-emailclient-spawn.yml    (T1566.001)
│   ├── 03-discovery/
│   │   └── account-system-discovery.yml      (T1087.001)
│   ├── 06-persistence/
│   │   └── create-phantomlab-user-localadmin.yml (T1136.001)
│   ├── 07-lateral-movement/
│   │   └── lateral-smb-wmi-winrm.yml         (T1021.002) ← REAL cross-host
│   ├── 08-collection/
│   │   └── automated-collection-safe.yml     (T1119)
│   ├── 10-command-and-control/
│   │   └── dns-beacon-simple.yml             (T1071.004)
│   └── 11-exfiltration/
│       └── exfil-staged-loot.yml             (T1041)
└── adversaries/
    └── phantom-phishing-kill-chain.yml       (12-step atomic_ordering)
```

9 ability YAMLs (each base64-encoded PowerShell to survive Caldera's whitespace-strip) + 1 adversary profile YAML referencing the full 12-step chain (6 custom + 6 stockpile abilities by UUID).

### The 12-step kill chain

| # | Tactic | Technique | Ability source |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | initial-access | T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment | custom |
| 2 | execution | T1059.003 Windows Command Shell | stockpile |
| 3 | discovery | T1087.001 Local Account Discovery | custom (workgroup-friendly) |
| 4 | credential-access | T1003.005 Cached Credentials | stockpile |
| 5 | privilege-escalation | T1548.002 UAC bypass via Fodhelper | stockpile |
| 6 | persistence | T1136.001 Create Local Account | stockpile |
| 7 | **lateral-movement** | **T1021.002 SMB/Admin Shares** | **custom — real cross-host SMB+WMI+WinRM** |
| 8 | collection | T1119 Automated Collection | custom (no protected-dir crashes) |
| 9 | collection | T1560 Archive Collected Data | stockpile |
| 10 | command-and-control | T1071.004 DNS C2 | custom (no Atomic Red Team dep) |
| 11 | exfiltration | T1041 Exfil over C2 | custom (HTTP POST simulation) |
| 12 | impact | T1491 Defacement | stockpile |

### How operators use this

**Quick path** — Caldera UI import:
1. Open Caldera UI (IAP-tunneled `https://localhost:8889`, login `red` / `$CALDERA_RED_PASSWORD`).
2. **Advanced → Abilities → Import YAML** for each `*.yml` under `abilities/`.
3. **Advanced → Adversaries → Import YAML** for `adversaries/phantom-phishing-kill-chain.yml`.
4. Run the two bootstrap abilities once (one on `group=victim`, one on `group=red`).
5. Fire the adversary as an Operation targeting `group=red`. Atomic planner runs the 12 steps sequentially over ~6-8 minutes.

**Durable path** — volume mount the abilities/adversaries directories into the Caldera container at `/usr/src/app/data/abilities/phantom` and `/usr/src/app/data/adversaries/phantom`. Edit `installer/docker-compose.yml`'s `caldera:` service to add the mounts.

Full prerequisites, telemetry expectations, lab-safety notes, and customization guide are in `bundles/spark/caldera-content/README.md`.

### Real cross-host lateral movement — what makes this hard

Workgroup-to-workgroup lateral on Server 2022 requires a specific 6-knob configuration most operators discover the hard way:

1. **Password complexity** — Windows rejects local-account passwords containing the username substring (case-insensitive). `Lab26Pass#` avoids "phantomlab" and satisfies 3 of 4 categories.
2. **`LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy=1`** on the victim — lets local admins get a full token on network logon (UAC token filtering would otherwise reduce the network logon to standard-user rights).
3. **`Service\AllowUnencrypted=true`** on the victim's WinRM — required for HTTP listener Basic/Negotiate without HTTPS.
4. **`Client\TrustedHosts`** on the attacker — required for workgroup WinRM (no Kerberos available, fallback to NTLM via Negotiate requires explicit trust).
5. **SMB firewall** — `Enable-NetFirewallRule -DisplayGroup "File and Printer Sharing"` on the victim.
6. **Auth method** — `Negotiate` works for local accounts (Basic returns 401 on local-account logons even with AllowUnencrypted); `New-PSDrive -Credential` for SMB (more reliable than `net use`).

The `lateral-smb-wmi-winrm.yml` ability handles all of this. The bootstrap abilities set up knobs 1-5; the lateral itself selects the right auth method (knob 6). All 4 cross-host actions — drive map / file write / WMI query / Invoke-Command — succeed end-to-end against a properly bootstrapped pair of Windows agents.

### Telemetry per step

Each step generates distinct Sysmon / Windows Security event IDs. The README's "Telemetry you can detect" section enumerates the per-step signatures. Operators can build detection rules step-by-step or correlations across the whole chain.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.55

- **No making any of these abilities `singleton: true`.** They're idempotent and lab-runnable repeatedly; making them singleton would prevent re-running a chain in the same operation, which is operator-friendly behavior.
- **No replacing the base64-`-EncodedCommand` PowerShell wrapping** with plaintext multi-line strings. Caldera's ability serializer strips whitespace from `command:` fields, breaking any non-trivial multi-line PowerShell. Base64 preserves everything.
- **No removing the `phantomlab` password from being literal `Lab26Pass#` in the YAMLs.** It's lab-safe, complexity-compliant, and intentionally not parameterized — operators who want different creds edit the YAML directly. Parameterizing would force runtime fact-injection through Caldera which complicates the install path.
- **No bundling stockpile abilities** (the 6 from Caldera's own library). Caldera ships them; our YAMLs reference them by UUID. Duplicating would create maintenance debt + version drift.

---

## [v0.5.54] — 2026-05-16

**New `#connectors-design` section in `/help/architecture` with an inline SVG lifecycle diagram** — answers the operator's recurring question "how do connectors and instances actually work, end-to-end?" by giving one canonical page that covers origins, storage, build, runtime, and operator-visible surfaces.

### Why

Pre-v0.5.54 the architecture page had **three** connector-related sections (`#connector-containers`, `#connector-state`, `#marketplace-logic`) but no single place that:
- Showed the full lifecycle in one diagram
- Distinguished bundle-origin from user-origin clearly
- Enumerated the 5 storage layers + when each gets touched
- Walked from author commit → catalog → install → instance → container spawn → tool call as one narrative

After the v0.5.53 cycle of marketplace iteration (download endpoint, real upload form, logos, bundle metadata), the operator asked for a dedicated design section. v0.5.54 ships it.

### What ships

#### `mcp/agent/app/help/architecture/page.tsx`

- New nav entry: `Connectors & Instances — Design` (anchor `#connectors-design`) under the **Connectors & Extensions** group, sitting between Hooks Framework and Connector State Machine.
- New `ConnectorsDesign` section component with 5 subsections:
  1. **End-to-end lifecycle** — the SVG diagram (described below).
  2. **The two origin paths** — bundle vs user, side-by-side spec comparing Path / Origin / Image / Schema / Edit semantics.
  3. **The five storage layers** — 5-row table covering bundle YAML, user YAML, marketplace.db, instances.db, SecretStore. Each row: content / backed-by / lifecycle. Includes a "sixth ephemeral store" note for the phantom-updater docker daemon.
  4. **Lifecycle walkthrough** — 10-step numbered narrative following one connector (cortex-docs) from author commit through runtime tool call. Each step names the file paths + API endpoints involved.
  5. **Where the operator sees what** — 6-row triage table mapping "I want to see X" → exact UI route or API endpoint. Helps with day-to-day debugging.
- New `ConnectorsLifecycleSvg` component (~250 LoC) — inline SVG, 1100×820 viewBox, 7 horizontal lanes:
  1. Origin (bundle author + operator upload)
  2. Storage (bundle YAML + user YAML)
  3. Validate (jsonschema validator)
  4. Catalog (in-memory + marketplace.db install state)
  5. Configure (instances.db + SecretStore)
  6. Spawn (phantom-updater + per-instance container)
  7. Runtime (agent MCP connector_loader proxy)

Color encoding in the SVG:
- **Blue** (`#60a5fa`) — bundle path + runtime routing
- **Purple** (`#c084fc`) — user-upload path + phantom-updater spawn chain
- **Orange** (`#fb923c`) — schema validation (the universal join point — both origins converge here)
- **Green** (`#34d399`) — catalog + install state + container_url callback
- **Red** (`#ef4444`) — SecretStore + decryption path

Each color also corresponds to a named arrow marker (`arrow-blue`, `arrow-purple`, etc.) so arrowheads pick up the line color.

### Operator UX

Open `/help/architecture#connectors-design` after the v0.5.54 upgrade. You'll see:

1. A 16:9-ish SVG at the top of the section laying out the full lifecycle.
2. Below: spec/origin comparison, storage table, 10-step walkthrough, triage table.
3. Cross-references to `#connector-containers` (spawn details), `#connector-state` (state machine), `#marketplace-logic` (catalog internals), `#image-pinning` (digest contract), `#secret-store` (KEK + encryption).

The SVG is inline (no external asset, no PNG, no Excalidraw file) so it scales perfectly, loads with the bundle, and the source is right next to the prose in `page.tsx`. Anyone updating the lifecycle just edits the SVG directly.

### Cross-section coordination

This section **summarizes + indexes** the existing 3 connector sections rather than replacing them. The other three keep their focused depth:
- `#connector-containers` stays the canonical reference for per-instance container naming, lifecycle on instance create, the v0.5.0 universal-container migration.
- `#connector-state` stays the canonical reference for the state graph + auth-error classification + SSE events.
- `#marketplace-logic` stays the canonical reference for catalog scan internals, install state machine, origin pinning, v0.5.0 single-source-of-truth refactor.

The new `#connectors-design` is the "if you read one section to understand connectors, read this one" landing page.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.54

- **No replacing the inline SVG with an external PNG / Excalidraw export.** The whole point is the diagram lives next to the prose + is editable in the same file. PNG diagrams rot fast because no one finds the source.
- **No duplicating content from the 3 existing sections into `#connectors-design`.** The pattern is summarize + cross-reference, not copy. Keep the new section as the index; preserve depth in the dedicated pages.
- **No removing the storage-layers table.** It's the single most useful operator-debugging artifact in the page — when an operator says "I deleted X and Y is broken," this table maps the cause.

---

## [v0.5.53] — 2026-05-16

**All 6 bundle connectors get `displayName`, `tags`, and inline SVG logos** — fills the nice-to-have gaps the operator surfaced after v0.5.52's download smoke. Also cleans up xsiam's stale `module-style` comment left over from the v0.5.0 module → container migration.

### Why

After v0.5.52 added logo support, the operator downloaded `xsiam.yaml` and asked "anything wrong?" Three observations:

1. **No `displayName`** — marketplace cards rendered "Xsiam" via `id.title()` fallback; should explicitly say "Cortex XSIAM."
2. **No `tags`** — operators couldn't filter the marketplace by `cortex` / `siem` / `red-team` / etc.
3. **No `logo`** — generic icon on every bundle connector card; the v0.5.52 logo field was usable, just not used.

Plus a stale comment in xsiam.yaml (lines 15-22) said "Tool dispatch — module-style with `xsiam_` prefix" but the actual config two lines below was `style: "container"`. Cosmetic but confusing for anyone reading the file fresh.

### What ships

#### Each of the 6 `bundles/spark/connectors/<id>/connector.yaml`

| Connector | displayName | tags | logo accent |
|---|---|---|---|
| `xlog` | Xlog (synthetic telemetry) | `xlog · log-generation · siem · telemetry · scenarios · mitre-attack` | green log-stream waveform |
| `caldera` | CALDERA (red-team) | `caldera · red-team · mitre-attack · adversary-emulation · sandcat` | red bullseye |
| `xsiam` | Cortex XSIAM | `xsiam · cortex · siem · xql · soar · papi` | orange shield with X |
| `web` | Web browser | `web · browser · playwright · scraping · chromium · headless` | blue browser frame + globe |
| `cortex-docs` | Cortex docs (knowledge) | `cortex · docs · xql · knowledge-base · lookup · search` | indigo open book |
| `cortex-content` | Cortex content (playbooks) | `cortex · content · playbooks · marketplace · kb · automation` | indigo content-list with orange dots |

All 6 logos are inline SVGs encoded as `data:image/svg+xml;base64,…`. Raw SVG sizes are 326-686 bytes; encoded data URIs are 462-942 chars. Well under the schema's 350,000 cap.

Visual design rationale:
- Each connector gets a **distinct color theme** so they're recognizable at the marketplace card size (64×64 viewBox, 12px corner radius matching the card style).
- Cortex-family connectors (xsiam, cortex-docs, cortex-content) all share an indigo+orange palette to reinforce the family relationship.
- Abstract symbology only — no third-party brand impersonation. caldera uses a bullseye (red-team flavor) not MITRE's actual mark; xsiam uses a shield with X not Palo Alto's actual cortex glyph.
- Logos round-trip cleanly via the v0.5.52 download endpoint — operators see them on cards, can download the YAML, edit/redistribute, and the logo persists.

#### `bundles/spark/connectors/xsiam/connector.yaml`

Replaced the 8-line stale "module-style with xsiam_ prefix" comment block with a current explanation that:
- Notes the wrapper is stateless + that PAPI auth lives in SecretStore.
- Explicitly mentions `functionPrefix: "xsiam_"` is a v0.5.0+ no-op (ignored when style is container) but kept for round-trip stability of operator-downloaded YAMLs.

The `functionPrefix` line itself stays — removing it would cause every operator-downloaded YAML to diff against a re-upload, which feels wrong for a field that's just metadata at this point.

### Operator UX

After upgrading:
- Open `/connectors` → Marketplace tab — every card now shows a distinct logo + a proper display name + filterable tags.
- Click the download icon on `xsiam` → new download includes `displayName`, `tags`, `logo` (base64 data URI inline).
- The marketplace listing API (`/api/v1/marketplace`) now returns `display_name` + `logo` per entry — the UI uses these directly without needing a per-card detail fetch.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.53

- **No removing the inline-SVG approach in favor of separate logo files.** v0.5.52's "data-URI-in-YAML" rule still applies — each connector's logo lives INSIDE its YAML so it round-trips on download/upload as a single self-contained spec.
- **No third-party brand impersonation** in bundle-connector logos. Use abstract symbology (bullseye for red-team, shield for security, etc.) — not real corporate marks. v0.5.53's caldera + xsiam logos comply with this; future connectors must too.
- **No replacing `functionPrefix: "xsiam_"` with empty string or removing the line.** It's preserved for round-trip stability of operator-downloaded YAMLs. If an operator downloads → edits → re-uploads, they shouldn't get an unexpected diff against the bundled version.

---

## [v0.5.52] — 2026-05-16

**Marketplace connector lifecycle gets four real surfaces** — connector.yaml download, real upload-form file picker (replacing the hard-coded `MOCK_PARSED_YAML` Alpaca demo), `logo` schema field with base64-data-URI round-trip, and inline server-side validation errors. Closes the "view-only marketplace" gap the operator hit during v0.5.51 smoke.

### Why

Operator audit of the connectors page during v0.5.51 deploy uncovered three concrete gaps:

1. **No download.** The Marketplace tab listed connectors but no button to fetch the underlying connector.yaml. A vendor wanting to inspect or fork a bundle connector had to `docker exec` and `cat` the file.
2. **Upload was 100% mocked.** Click "Upload Connector" → drop a file → form auto-populates with hardcoded Alpaca data regardless of what file you picked → "Upload & Install" button does nothing. The backend's `POST /api/v1/marketplace/upload` endpoint was real + properly validated, but the UI was never wired to it.
3. **Logo handling was undefined.** The connector schema didn't model logos at all; the mock upload form had a "Connector Logo" tile that was purely cosmetic.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/connectors/connector.schema.json`

Adds optional `logo` field:

```json
"logo": {
  "type": "string",
  "pattern": "^data:image/(svg\\+xml|png|jpeg|gif|webp);base64,[A-Za-z0-9+/=]+$",
  "maxLength": 350000
}
```

- **Format**: base64-encoded data URI (`data:image/<mime>;base64,...`). Supported MIME types: SVG, PNG, JPEG, GIF, WebP.
- **Max length**: 350,000 chars (~260 KB encoded). Client caps RAW at 200 KB before base64; base64 expansion is ~33%, so the encoded value fits well under the schema ceiling.
- **Round-trip**: logo lives INSIDE the YAML file. Download a connector → get the YAML with the logo embedded → re-upload the same YAML → the logo persists. No separate asset to manage.
- **Why data URI vs separate file**: single source of truth (the YAML IS the spec), no logo-file-management infrastructure, no extra HTTP fetch to render a card. Cost: YAML files grow by 1-50 KB depending on logo. Worth it.

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/marketplace.py`

- New endpoint `GET /api/v1/marketplace/{connector_id}/download` — streams the raw connector.yaml back with `Content-Type: application/yaml` + `Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="{id}.yaml"`. Same path resolution as `_scan_catalogue` so bundle + user connectors both work. Audits as `connector_downloaded` event.
- `_connector_summary` now projects two new fields into the catalog response:
  - `display_name` (from `displayName` field, with `id.title()` fallback) — saves the UI an extra fetch per card to get a human-readable name.
  - `logo` (the data URI, if present + valid) — lets marketplace cards render real logos without a per-card detail fetch.

#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/marketplace/upload/route.ts` (NEW)
#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/marketplace/[connectorId]/download/route.ts` (NEW)

Two new Next.js proxy routes. Upload reads the multipart body as ArrayBuffer + passes it through unchanged (preserves the boundary). Download forwards the body + the `Content-Disposition` header so the browser's save-as flow works without extra client-side wiring.

#### `mcp/agent/app/connectors/page.tsx`

**Replaced the mock `UploadConnectorPanel`** with a real implementation:

- Removed `MOCK_PARSED_YAML` (the 30-line hardcoded Alpaca object).
- Hidden `<input type="file" accept=".yaml,.yml,…">` controlled via `useRef`. Drop-zone click + drag-drop both feed the same `handleYamlFile` path.
- `handleYamlFile` reads the file via `File.text()`, parses with `js-yaml` (already a dependency), shows parse errors inline.
- All preview fields now render from the **actually-parsed** YAML's `displayName` / `id` / `version` / `description` / `tags` / `configSchema.properties` / `secretSlots` / `spec.tools` / `source` / `runtimeMapping` / `image`. Read-only for safety — the YAML is the source of truth, edit it in your IDE.
- New **logo upload tile**: separate hidden `<input type="file" accept="image/svg+xml,image/png,…">`. On select, validates MIME + size (200 KB raw cap), reads as data URI via `FileReader.readAsDataURL`, shows preview in the tile.
- On Upload & Install: if the operator uploaded a logo separate from what's in the YAML, the parsed spec gets re-dumped via `yaml.dump({...parsed, logo: dataUri}, {lineWidth: -1, noRefs: true})` (long-base64-on-one-line-safe). POSTs as multipart with `connector_yaml` field to `/api/agent/marketplace/upload`.
- Inline error display: parse errors above the drop zone, server validation errors above the form. The server returns structured `{error, code}` from `validate_connector_spec` — operators see the violating field path, not a generic 400.
- Success banner with the next-step hint (POST install).

**Added Download button to every marketplace card**: an `<a download>` link pointing at `/api/agent/marketplace/<id>/download` with `download={id}.yaml`. Browser handles save-as via the agent proxy's Content-Disposition header. Works for both bundle + user connectors.

### Logo handling — design rationale

Three questions the operator asked, and the answer:

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Base64 or SVG? | Both. The field accepts `data:image/svg+xml;base64,...` (SVG is preferred for crispness) OR PNG/JPEG/GIF/WebP. All encoded as base64 data URIs uniformly. |
| Is the logo part of the YAML file? | Yes. Top-level `logo:` field. Round-trips on download + upload because it's embedded text in the file. |
| When I download the connector, does the logo come with it? | Yes. The download endpoint streams the YAML byte-for-byte; logo is preserved exactly as the operator uploaded it. |

### Operator UX

1. **View** — Marketplace tab shows cards with real logos when the connector ships one (xlog/caldera/xsiam etc. don't yet — that's a follow-up to give the bundle connectors logos).
2. **Download** — Click the download icon on any card → browser save-as dialog → file is `<id>.yaml`.
3. **Upload** — Click Upload Connector → drop your `*.yaml` (or click Browse Files) → real preview renders from your file → optional: click the Logo tile to upload an SVG/PNG → Upload & Install.
4. **Validation** — Schema violation in your YAML? Server returns a clear error with the violating field path. Logo too big? Client catches it before the request.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.52

- **No re-introducing mock data in UploadConnectorPanel.** The grep test: `grep -n "MOCK_PARSED_YAML\|Alpaca_unified" mcp/agent/app/connectors/page.tsx` must return zero matches. If a future cosmetic improvement needs sample data, name the variable EXAMPLE_PARSED_YAML and gate it behind a `DEV_MODE` flag — never wire it into the live render path.
- **No skipping schema validation on upload.** The server's `validate_connector_spec` is the source of truth. Client-side `yaml.load` is preview-only — DO NOT replicate the schema rules in TS.
- **No raw logo files separate from the YAML.** v0.5.52 explicitly commits to data-URI-in-YAML. Reintroducing a separate `/api/.../logos/<id>.png` surface would create a 2-place truth problem (YAML says logo exists, asset 404s; or worse, YAML lacks logo but asset exists → which wins?).
- **No download without auth.** `GET /api/v1/marketplace/{id}/download` is bearer-auth via MCP_TOKEN. Connector YAMLs may carry operator-visible metadata (image refs to private registries, internal-only tool names); the marketplace catalog is not public. Tracking via `connector_downloaded` audit row enforces accountability.

---

## [v0.5.51] — 2026-05-16

**phantom-updater now reads `PHANTOM_VERSION` + `DIGEST_PHANTOM_*` from `/host/.env` at runtime instead of receiving them as `environment:` entries from the compose file.** This closes a long-standing violation of the v0.3.0+ image-digest-pinning invariant — pre-v0.5.51 phantom-updater was recreated on every stack upgrade even when its own image digest was unchanged, because `docker compose`'s config-hash flipped any time a stack digest in the resolved env block flipped.

### Why

Operator caught this during the v0.5.50 deploy smoke. The `phantom-updater` container is supposed to follow the same contract as `xlog` and `caldera`: same image digest → no recreate, in-memory state preserved across upgrades. Pre-v0.5.51 it didn't: even though `phantom-updater@sha256:cd50e9b…` was identical between v0.5.49 and v0.5.50, the compose file's `phantom-updater` env block contained:

```yaml
- PHANTOM_VERSION        # changed: dev-83e8016 → dev-d9b56a0
- DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT   # changed
- DIGEST_PHANTOM_XLOG    # unchanged
- DIGEST_PHANTOM_CALDERA # unchanged
- DIGEST_PHANTOM_UPDATER # unchanged
- …
```

`docker compose up -d` computes a per-service config-hash that includes the resolved env-var VALUES. When `DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT` flipped value, the resolved env block was different, so compose recreated the container. The image was identical; the container's effective config wasn't.

xlog and caldera don't have this problem because their compose service definitions don't reference the version/digest env vars.

### What ships

#### `updater/src/main.py`

- New `/host/.env reads` module-level block (~115 LoC) providing:
  - `_read_host_env() → dict[str, str]` — parses `/host/.env` with a **30-second TTL cache** so hot-path API calls (e.g. `/api/v1/version/current` on every observability-panel refresh) don't re-parse on every request. The cache is bypassed (cleared) by `_invalidate_host_env_cache()` when `_apply_manifest_to_env` writes a new manifest, so freshly-applied values propagate within microseconds, not 30 seconds.
  - `_running_phantom_version() → str | None` — reads `PHANTOM_VERSION`.
  - `_stack_digest_env_var(service) → str` + `_stack_digest(service) → str | None` — centralizes the `phantom-agent → DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT` casing rule + reads stack digest.
  - `_connector_digest_env_var(connector_id) → str` + `_connector_digest(connector_id) → str | None` — sister functions for connector digests.
  - All helpers handle missing `/host/.env`, missing keys, malformed lines, and surrounding quotes gracefully — never crash the updater over .env edits.

- `_apply_manifest_to_env(manifest)` now calls `_invalidate_host_env_cache()` after the file write so the in-process cache reflects on-disk reality immediately.

- Migrated all 7 call-sites that previously read `os.environ.get("PHANTOM_VERSION")` or `os.environ.get(f"DIGEST_PHANTOM_…")`:
  - `version_check()` (2 reads — running_version + per-service env_var derivation)
  - `update SSE stream handler` (2 reads — running_version + per-service env_var)
  - `_connector_image_ref()` (3 reads — running_version + connector_digest_env_var + connector_digest)
  - `image_pinning_for()` (2 reads — connector_digest + running_version fallback tag)

- Other env vars (`PHANTOM_REGISTRY_USER`, `PHANTOM_REGISTRY_TOKEN`, `MCP_TOKEN`, `HOST_INSTALL_DIR`, `HEALTHY_TIMEOUT_S`) remain on `os.environ.get` — they're either stable across upgrades (REGISTRY_USER) or operator-provided startup config (MCP_TOKEN). Only the **release-cadence-changing** vars moved to `/host/.env` reads.

#### `installer/docker-compose.yml`

Stripped from phantom-updater's `environment:` block:
- `PHANTOM_VERSION`
- `DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT` / `_XLOG` / `_CALDERA` / `_UPDATER` / `_BROWSER`
- `DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_RUNTIME` / `_XLOG` / `_XSIAM` / `_CALDERA` / `_WEB` / `_CORTEX_DOCS`

Replaced with a comment block explaining the v0.5.51 contract: these values come from /host/.env at runtime now. The phantom-agent service's env block (lines 360-374) still contains these passthroughs — the agent reads them via its existing TS-side runtime-config path, which is a separate concern from phantom-updater.

### Operator-visible behavior change

After v0.5.51 lands:

| Upgrade scenario | Pre-v0.5.51 | v0.5.51+ |
|---|---|---|
| phantom-updater image digest unchanged, any stack digest changed | Recreated (state lost) | **Untouched** (state preserved) |
| phantom-updater image digest changed | Recreated | Recreated (same as before) |
| Operator hand-edits /host/.env (e.g. rotates token) | Required compose recreate to propagate | Propagates within 30s via cache TTL |

The first row is the load-bearing improvement: phantom-updater's in-memory state (compose subprocess handle, ongoing-update SSE stream state, the `_HOST_ENV_CACHE` itself) survives stack upgrades.

The "30s cache TTL" trade-off is worth calling out: a manual `.env` edit takes up to 30 seconds to reflect in API responses. The alternative (no cache) would re-parse on every request — fine for low-volume API but `/api/v1/version/current` fires per observability-panel refresh. 30s is short enough that operators don't notice ("did my edit work?") and long enough to amortize hot paths. `_apply_manifest_to_env` bypasses the cache entirely so the updater's own manifest writes are visible immediately.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.51

- **No re-adding `DIGEST_PHANTOM_*` or `PHANTOM_VERSION` to phantom-updater's `environment:` block**, even as a "belt and suspenders" backup. The whole point is that the env block must stay stable across digest changes. Adding them back re-introduces the recreate cycle.
- **No reading these values via `os.environ.get` anywhere in `updater/src/main.py`.** The grep test: `grep -nE 'os\.environ\.get\("PHANTOM_VERSION"\)|os\.environ\.get\("DIGEST_PHANTOM' updater/src/main.py` must return zero matches. Use the helpers instead.
- **No removing the cache-invalidation hook in `_apply_manifest_to_env`.** Without it, the 30s TTL means the updater would briefly return stale digest values right after applying a manifest — the exact opposite of the contract.
- **No reading TLS material, secrets, or connector instance state from `/host/.env`.** That path is the .env file's *manifest section*, not a free-form key-value store. Reading sensitive material from there would create a new audit-trail gap. Stick to PHANTOM_VERSION + DIGEST_* — those are version metadata, not secrets.

### Cross-subsystem alignment

This change is invisible to the customer compose's UPGRADE invariant (defined in [docs/CICD.md § Image digest pinning contract](docs/CICD.md#image-digest-pinning-contract-customer-compose)):

> "containers retain in-memory state across upgrades that don't change image content"

Pre-v0.5.51 the contract held for `xlog` and `caldera` but not `phantom-updater`. v0.5.51 makes the contract uniform across all three.

`phantom-agent` still gets recreated when its own image digest changes (correct — that IS a content change). The next gap to consider: phantom-agent's env block also references `DIGEST_PHANTOM_*` vars; the agent reads these via its runtime-config layer at the Next.js side. The agent's recreation is driven by its OWN image digest changing on every release (release-notes.ts + UI code changes), so the env-var question is moot in practice — but if a future release ships with zero phantom-agent image changes, the same env-recreate trap would fire. A follow-up (post-v0.5.51) can apply the same `/host/.env` runtime-read pattern there. Not yet load-bearing.

---

## [v0.5.50] — 2026-05-16

**Installer fix: a freshly-provided GHCR token is now persisted back to `/opt/phantom/.env`** instead of being held only in memory for the current install. Closes a long-standing nuisance where every re-run on an existing install would re-prompt for a fresh PAT because the previous run never wrote the captured token back to disk.

### Why

Pre-v0.5.50 flow on an existing install with an expired `.env`-stored token:

1. Step 4 reads `.env` → finds the stale `ghs_…` (already-expired short-lived token from a previous run) → validation fails.
2. Installer prompts: `Token (input hidden):` — operator pastes a fresh `ghp_…` PAT.
3. Validation succeeds, `$GHCR_TOKEN` holds the fresh token in-memory.
4. Step 5 says "✓ Reusing existing secrets from /opt/phantom/.env" — **but skips the `.env` rewrite because `EXISTING_INSTALL=1`**.
5. Step 7 uses `$GHCR_TOKEN` for `docker login` + image pulls — both succeed because the in-memory token is fresh.
6. **`.env` on disk still contains the stale token from before step 4.**

On the next re-run, the cycle repeats — operator pastes another fresh PAT, install succeeds, `.env` still doesn't get updated. This wasted operator time on every re-run after token expiry.

### What ships

#### `installer/phantom-installer.template.sh`

- New helper `write_env_value VAR VALUE` — sister of the existing `read_env_value VAR`. Updates an existing `VAR=…` line in-place via awk (matching the `VAR=` prefix anchor so `VAR2` doesn't accidentally collide), or appends `VAR=VALUE` if the key isn't present. Reapplies `chmod 600` after the rewrite. Idempotent.
- New call-site at the end of Step 4 (registry credentials), gated on `EXISTING_INSTALL == 1 && TOKEN_SOURCE != "$INSTALL_DIR/.env"`:
  - If the captured token came from the **env var path** (`PHANTOM_REGISTRY_TOKEN=… sudo ./phantom-installer`) or the **interactive prompt path**, persist it to `.env`.
  - The `.env`-source path is skipped because the value already matches what's on disk.
  - On success: `✓ Persisted fresh token to /opt/phantom/.env (next re-run won't re-prompt unless this token also expires)`.

The fresh-install path (`EXISTING_INSTALL=0`) is unaffected — that path already writes the full `.env` via the heredoc at line ~715, including the freshly-captured token.

### Operator UX

After v0.5.50:

1. First re-run on an existing install with an expired `.env` token: same flow as before (prompt → paste → validate → install proceeds), BUT the new token is now also persisted to `.env`.
2. Subsequent re-runs: Step 4 reads the just-persisted token from `.env`, validates it (succeeds unless this token has now also expired), proceeds without prompt.
3. The only time you re-paste is when the PERSISTED token itself expires. Mint a long-lived fine-grained PAT (or a classic PAT with `read:packages`) once and re-runs stay frictionless.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.50

- **No silent failure on `.env` write.** The persist call is `if write_env_value … then ok … else warn …` — a write failure surfaces immediately. Without that, operators would think their token persisted when it didn't.
- **No persisting tokens that haven't been validated.** The call-site sits *after* the validation loop, so we only ever persist tokens that GHCR accepted. Pre-validating prevents pasting a typo'd token into `.env` and breaking the next re-run.
- **No persisting tokens from the `.env` source.** That path is the no-op — the value already matches. Skipping it avoids needless I/O + the chmod call.

---

## [v0.5.49] — 2026-05-16

**Sidebar nav fills 9 missing entries that had accumulated across v0.5.21-48** — pages shipped during the Octagon-gap sprint that existed on disk + in the bundle but couldn't be reached from the navigation. Operators were forced to type URLs directly; now every page has a discoverable nav entry. Also adds a `CLAUDE.md` discipline (`Rule 6a`) requiring sidebar entries in the same PR as new pages, so this doesn't recur.

### Why

While testing v0.5.48 deployed to phantom-vm, the operator hit `/` and `/observability` but couldn't see the new install-form UI, plugin-handler transport, etc. The deployed bundles contained the correct v0.5.48 code (verified via grep) and `/api/agent/version` returned `dev-5582e5b` — so the deployment was correct. The actual gap: pages like `/observability/plugins` and `/settings/hooks` weren't in `components/sidebar.tsx`'s `navEntries`. The only way to reach them was typing the URL directly.

Audit: 9 routes that ship `page.tsx` were missing from the sidebar.

### What ships

#### `mcp/agent/components/sidebar.tsx`

| Group | Added entry | Page introduced | Icon |
|---|---|---|---|
| Command | Agents (`/agents`) | Round-15 / Phase S agent registry | `groups` |
| Command | Tasks (`/tasks`) | Round-15 / Phase T task registry | `task_alt` |
| Integration | Plugins (`/plugins`) | Round-15 / Phase X filesystem plugin tree | `extension` |
| Observability | Runtime events (`/observability/runtime-events`) | v0.5.21-era runtime event stream | `bolt` |
| Observability | Connectors (`/observability/connectors`) | connector lifecycle telemetry | `settings_input_component` |
| Observability | Cost (`/observability/cost`) | v0.5.40 group-by toggle | `payments` |
| Observability | Bench (`/observability/bench`) | v0.5.35 benchmark UI (paired with `/observability/bench/[run_id]` + `/compare`) | `speed` |
| Observability | Plugins (`/observability/plugins`) | v0.5.44 entry-point plugin catalog (+ v0.5.47 install/uninstall + v0.5.48 handler invocation) | `deployed_code` |
| Settings | Hooks (`/settings/hooks`) | v0.5.21 hook management surface — operators couldn't reach this without typing the URL | `webhook` |

Each new entry has an inline code comment where context matters (e.g. "v0.5.44+ entry-point catalog; distinct from /plugins filesystem tree above" — the `/plugins` + `/observability/plugins` duo is two related-but-distinct surfaces that need disambiguation in operator's mental model).

Order rationale:
- Command group: Agents lands right after Skills (both are agent-runtime concerns); Tasks after Jobs (both are work-queue surfaces, jobs=operator-queued / tasks=worker-running).
- Integration: Plugins joins Connectors / Approvals / API Keys as one of the "things connected to this Phantom instance" cluster.
- Observability: telemetry primitives stay grouped first (Metrics / Traces / Logs / Events / Runtime events), then operational surfaces (Connectors / Pipeline / Cost / Bench / Plugins), then Live activity.
- Settings: Hooks lands between Providers and Personality — both Hooks + Personality customize agent behavior, while Providers + Models are upstream config.

#### `CLAUDE.md` — new Rule 6a

> **No new UI page without a sidebar nav entry in the SAME release.** A page that exists on disk + ships in the bundle but isn't reachable from sidebar.tsx's nav tree is operationally invisible — the operator can only get there by typing the URL directly, which they don't know to do. The grep test before committing: `find mcp/agent/app -maxdepth 3 -name "page.tsx" | xargs dirname | sort` vs. the `href:` entries in sidebar.tsx's `navEntries` — every page (except redirects + `[param]` dynamic routes) should appear. If a page is deliberately operator-hidden, document that with a code comment near the page's `export default`.

This rule lives next to Rule 6 ("no backend feature without a UI surface") because it's the symmetric concern: Rule 6 catches silent endpoints; Rule 6a catches silent pages.

### Operator UX

Open Phantom after the upgrade and expand each sidebar group; you should now see:

**Command**: Chat, Skills, **Agents**, Memory, Knowledge, Jobs, **Tasks**

**Integration**: Connectors, Approvals, API Keys, **Plugins**

**Observability**: Overview, Metrics, Traces, Logs, Events, **Runtime events**, **Connectors**, Pipeline, **Cost**, **Bench**, **Plugins**, Live activity

**Settings**: Services, Models, Providers, **Hooks**, Personality, Backup & Restore

### Forbidden post-v0.5.49

- **No adding a new `app/<page>/page.tsx` without also editing `sidebar.tsx`.** The CLAUDE.md Rule 6a grep check is the gate. Reviewers MUST insist on this before merge — this is the same severity as "no backend feature without a UI surface."
- **No relying on URL bookmarks as the discovery path.** Operators who upgrade across versions shouldn't need to memorize new URLs; they should see new entries in the nav and click in.

---

## [v0.5.48] — 2026-05-16

**Plugin-contributed hook handlers are now CALLABLE from the agent's hook-runner.** Closes the cross-language bridge piece of #29; pairs with v0.5.31/44/47 (discovery + lifecycle) to make the entry-point plugin system end-to-end functional. Discovery now meets invocation; plugin authors can ship pip-installable hook libraries that operators install + wire up from `/settings/hooks` without touching the Phantom image.

### Why

Pre-v0.5.48: the entry-point plugin system shipped discovery (v0.5.31, what's installed) + lifecycle (v0.5.47, install/uninstall via UI) — but **no way to actually invoke** plugin-contributed handlers. A plugin could register `[project.entry-points."phantom.hooks"] my-handler = "my_pkg:my_handler"` and show up in `/observability/plugins`, but operators couldn't wire that handler into the hook framework. v0.5.48 closes that gap with a new `plugin` transport type for hooks.

The architectural challenge: plugin handlers live in **Python** (entry-points are a Python-package mechanism); the agent's hook-runner lives in **TypeScript** (lib/hook-runner.ts). v0.5.48 ships an HTTP bridge — when a `plugin`-transport hook fires, the hook-runner POSTs the payload to MCP's `/api/v1/plugin-hooks/{name}/invoke`, MCP runs the Python handler on a thread pool, returns the result back over JSON, the hook-runner translates that into the standard `HookResult` shape.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/plugin_hook_runner.py` (NEW)

In-process registry + invoker for `phantom.hooks` entry-points. Key surface:

- `get_handlers(refresh: bool = False) → dict[str, PluginHookHandler]` — walks `importlib.metadata.entry_points(group="phantom.hooks")` and resolves each `name = "module:fn"` into a callable. Caches the result. `refresh=True` re-walks (used after pip install).
- `list_handlers() → list[dict]` — JSON-safe descriptor list for the UI dropdown (name, dist_name, dist_version, target).
- `invoke_handler(name, payload, config, timeout_s)` — calls the handler on a shared 4-worker thread pool with a hard timeout. Returns `{ok, result, duration_ms, handler}` on success or `{ok: False, error}` on timeout/exception/unknown-name/non-dict-return.
- `clear_cache()` — drops the resolved-handler cache. Called automatically by `plugin_entry_points_routes.install_entry` / `uninstall_entry` so freshly installed plugins are picked up without an MCP restart.

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/plugin_hook_invoke.py` (NEW)

Two endpoints, both bearer-auth via `MCP_TOKEN`:

- `GET /api/v1/plugin-hooks` — list discovered handlers. `?refresh=1` forces a re-walk.
- `POST /api/v1/plugin-hooks/{name}/invoke` — body `{payload, config?, timeout_s?}`. Validates the body shape; caps `timeout_s` at 60s; calls `invoke_handler` + records a `plugin_hook_invoked` audit row tagged with the handler name + outcome category (`allow` / `deny` / `ask` / `no-op` / `ok-other` / `error`).

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py`

Registers the new module at boot via `register_plugin_hook_invoke_routes(mcp)`. Sits next to the existing `register_plugin_entry_points_routes(mcp)` call from v0.5.47.

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/plugin_entry_points_routes.py`

Install + uninstall handlers now call `plugin_hook_runner.clear_cache()` so newly installed plugins surface on the next `/api/v1/plugin-hooks` call without restart. The success-response `note` field is updated to reflect that plugin-hook handlers DO hot-reload (other contribution types — skills, connectors, providers, scanners — still need restart).

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/hooks.py`

Validator extended to accept `transport.type == "plugin"` with required `handlerName: str` and optional `config: dict | omitted` + `timeoutS: positive number | omitted`. Other transports unchanged.

#### `mcp/agent/lib/hooks.ts`

`HookTransport` union gets a fifth variant: `{type: "plugin", handlerName, config?, timeoutS?}`. `validateHook` adds the matching transport check (handlerName non-empty, config dict-or-omitted, timeoutS positive-or-omitted).

#### `mcp/agent/lib/hook-runner.ts`

New transport dispatch branch + new `runPluginHook` implementation: derives the MCP origin via `deriveMcpBaseUrl(MCP_URL)`, POSTs `{payload, config, timeout_s}` to `/api/v1/plugin-hooks/{handlerName}/invoke` with bearer auth, arms an AbortController bounded by `timeoutMs`, parses the response JSON, throws on `ok: false` (caught by the runner's failure-policy path), returns `result` (HookResult) or null (no-op) on `ok: true`.

#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/plugin-hooks/route.ts` (NEW)

Next.js proxy for `GET /api/agent/plugin-hooks?refresh=…` — used by the `/settings/hooks` UI form to populate the handler dropdown.

#### `mcp/agent/app/settings/hooks/page.tsx`

- `TransportKind` adds `"plugin"`.
- Transport-type dropdown gets a new option: "Plugin handler (entry-point, v0.5.48+)". Selecting it seeds `{type:"plugin", handlerName:"", config:{}}`.
- New `PluginHandlerConfigSection` component: fetches `/api/agent/plugin-hooks` (with a Refresh button that bumps `?refresh=1`), renders a handler dropdown + a JSON config textarea (schema is plugin-defined; we don't introspect Python) + a timeout input. On empty discovery, shows a callout pointing the operator at `/observability/plugins` to install one first.
- List-row badge for plugin-transport hooks: "plugin" in tertiary color.
- `handleSave` adds a pre-check: plugin transport requires a non-empty handlerName.

### Plugin author contract

```python
# pyproject.toml
[project.entry-points."phantom.hooks"]
my-handler = "my_pkg.hooks:my_handler"

# my_pkg/hooks.py
def my_handler(payload: dict, config: dict) -> dict | None:
    """Receive a HookPayload + operator config.

    Returns:
        None → no-op (same as not registering anything).
        dict → parsed as HookResult: {"decision": "allow"|"deny"|"ask",
               "reason": "..."}.
    """
    if config.get("block_on_friday") and is_friday():
        return {"decision": "deny", "reason": "Friday change-freeze policy"}
    return None
```

### Operator UX

1. Author publishes `phantom-hook-mypolicy` to pypi with a `phantom.hooks` entry-point.
2. Operator visits `/observability/plugins`, types `phantom-hook-mypolicy` into the install form, clicks Install.
3. MCP runs pip → returns success → clears the plugin-hook cache. The new handler is now discoverable.
4. Operator visits `/settings/hooks`, clicks Add hook, picks event (e.g. `PreToolUse`), picks transport "Plugin handler", clicks Refresh on the handler dropdown if needed, selects `my-handler` from the list, fills the JSON config, saves.
5. Hook fires next time the event happens. The agent dispatches into the Python handler via the MCP bridge; handler returns `allow` / `deny` / `ask` / `null`; the hook-runner treats it like any other transport's result.
6. Audit trail: each invocation writes a `plugin_hook_invoked` row visible at `/observability/events?action=plugin_hook_invoked`.

### Security model

- **Trust boundary**: plugin handlers run in the MCP process with full agent privileges. Treat plugin installation with the same care as a vendor library — review the source before `pip install`.
- **Auth**: `MCP_TOKEN`-only access on both surfaces (`/api/v1/plugin-hooks` GET + `/api/v1/plugin-hooks/{name}/invoke` POST). No public exposure.
- **Sandboxing**: none today. Plugin handlers are Python callables in the MCP process — they can do anything a vendor library can do. A future release may add capability scoping or process isolation; today we trust the operator's install-time review.
- **Timeout**: every invocation is bounded by `timeout_s` (default 5s, capped at 60s server-side) AND by the hook's overall `timeoutMs` agent-side. Hung handlers don't block the hook-runner.
- **Exception isolation**: plugin exceptions are caught + returned as structured errors (`{ok: false, error: ...}`). The agent's failure-policy path (`block` / `warn` / `allow`) handles them like any other transport's failure.
- **Audit**: every invocation writes a `plugin_hook_invoked` event regardless of outcome — operators grep `/observability/events` for the plugin lifecycle + every fire of a plugin handler.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.48

- **No skipping the audit row.** Plugin invocations are sensitive (operator-controlled code running in the MCP process); every fire MUST land in the audit log. If a future optimization batches invokes, batch the audit too — don't drop it.
- **No expanding the `agent` transport to dispatch plugin handlers.** The `agent` transport name stays reserved for the future "invoke an MCP tool as a hook handler" feature. `plugin` is the entry-point bridge; `agent` is the MCP-tool bridge; they're distinct and shouldn't share code paths.
- **No removing the cache-clear hooks on install/uninstall.** Operators won't accept "restart phantom-agent to use your new plugin" — the v0.5.47 + v0.5.48 contract is that plugin-hook handlers hot-reload on install. Other contribution types (skills, connectors) still need restart; that's a separate gap.

---

## [v0.5.47] — 2026-05-16

**Plugin install + uninstall from `/observability/plugins` UI.** Closes the long-deferred install/uninstall piece of #29; pairs with v0.5.44's discovery surface so operators can move pip-installable plugins through their full lifecycle without dropping into `docker exec`.

### Why

v0.5.44 made discovered plugins **visible** but left install/uninstall as a `docker exec phantom_agent pip install …` ceremony. v0.5.47 closes that gap: the page now hosts an install form that runs `pip install --user <spec>` server-side, plus per-row Uninstall buttons that run `pip uninstall -y <dist>`. Every action is audited via `record_event` so the trail lives at `/observability/events`.

This release ALSO fixes a v0.5.44 boot bug that would have TypeErrored on phantom-agent startup: that release's commit message claimed it added `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/plugins.py` but the new file never landed — only a `register_plugin_routes(mcp)` 1-arg call was added to `main.py`, which would call the existing 5-arg `register_plugin_routes(mcp, loader, memory_store, audit, agent_definition_store=...)` with too few args. v0.5.47 replaces that 1-arg call with `register_plugin_entry_points_routes(mcp)` calling a new function that lives in its own module (`api/plugin_entry_points_routes.py`) so the two surfaces — filesystem-discovered Phase X plugins (5-arg, retained) vs entry-point-discovered distributable plugins (1-arg, new) — never collide by name.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/plugin_entry_points_routes.py` (NEW)

Three HTTP endpoints, all bearer-auth via `MCP_TOKEN`:

- `GET /api/v1/plugin-entries` — list (groups + total). Same contract v0.5.44 promised under `/api/v1/plugins`, moved here to disambiguate names.
- `POST /api/v1/plugin-entries/install` — body `{spec: "<name>" | "git+https://..." | "<local-path>"}`. Runs `python -m pip install --user --quiet <spec>` via `asyncio.create_subprocess_exec` (no shell). Validates spec against shell metacharacters (`;`, `|`, `&`, `$`, `` ` ``, newlines). Audits success/failure via `record_event("plugin_install", …)`. Returns 201 with `{ok, spec, stdout_tail, discovery_counts, note}` on success, 500 with `{error, return_code, stderr, stdout}` on failure.
- `DELETE /api/v1/plugin-entries/{dist_name}` — runs `python -m pip uninstall -y --disable-pip-version-check <dist_name>`. Same metacharacter validation, same audit pattern.

Audit entry shape (visible at `/observability/events` after install/uninstall):

```
{
  "action": "plugin_install",
  "target": "plugin:phantom-hook-mypackage",
  "status": "success",
  "metadata": {"spec": "phantom-hook-mypackage", "return_code": 0, "stderr_tail": ""}
}
```

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py`

- New import: `from api.plugin_entry_points_routes import register_plugin_entry_points_routes`.
- Replaces the v0.5.44 buggy `register_plugin_routes(mcp)` 1-arg call with `register_plugin_entry_points_routes(mcp)` at the same location.
- The legacy `register_plugin_routes(mcp, plugin_loader, memory_store, audit, agent_definition_store=…)` 5-arg call at line 857 is unchanged — the Phase X filesystem plugin surface still works.

#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/plugin-entries/route.ts` (NEW)
#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/plugin-entries/install/route.ts` (NEW)
#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/plugin-entries/[dist_name]/route.ts` (NEW)

Three Next.js route proxies — GET, POST, DELETE — that forward to the MCP endpoints with `MCP_TOKEN` bearer auth. `dist_name` is URL-encoded via `encodeURIComponent` on the agent side and decoded by Starlette path-params on the MCP side.

#### `mcp/agent/app/observability/plugins/page.tsx`

- `refresh()` now fetches `/api/agent/plugin-entries` (was `/api/agent/plugins`).
- New install form: text input + button. Enter-key submits. While installing the button shows "Installing…" and the input is disabled. Result (success message or error tail) renders below the form in a scrollable monospace panel.
- Per-row Uninstall button: red-bordered, triggers a `confirm()` prompt, then DELETEs to `/api/agent/plugin-entries/{dist_name}` and refreshes the catalog. Disabled per-row while that row's distribution is uninstalling.
- Description text updated — no longer "Read-only"; v0.5.47 mentions the install/uninstall capability and the restart-needed caveat.

### Operator UX

1. Open `/observability/plugins` → see install form at top, group sections below.
2. Paste a spec (e.g. `phantom-hook-mypackage` or `git+https://github.com/…`), click Install.
3. Server runs pip; on success returns 201 with the success note. UI shows "Installed `<spec>`. Restart phantom-agent to load contributed builtins."
4. Catalog refreshes — the newly-installed package's entry-points (if any target a `phantom.*` group) appear in the right section.
5. Restart phantom-agent (or wait for the next routine restart) for the package's CONTRIBUTED handlers to load — discovery alone doesn't import them.
6. To remove: click Uninstall on the row, confirm the prompt, the row disappears after refresh.

### Security model

- Both install + uninstall are bearer-auth via `MCP_TOKEN` — only the agent (with the per-boot random token) can invoke. No public exposure.
- `pip install --user` writes to the agent container's user-site only; no system-wide install.
- Spec / dist_name are passed to `subprocess_exec` as **separate argv** (no shell). Spec validation rejects shell metacharacters as a defense-in-depth measure against future code paths that might splice into a shell.
- Both endpoints audit via `record_event` regardless of success/failure — the operator can grep `/observability/events` for the plugin lifecycle.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.47

- **No automatic install at MCP boot from config.** Even if the operator sets `INSTALL_PLUGINS=foo,bar` in `.env`, the entrypoint MUST NOT auto-install. Plugin install is explicit operator action only; auto-install at boot would mean attacker-controllable env can pull arbitrary pypi packages into the agent process.
- **No plugin-handler auto-invocation off this surface.** v0.5.47 is install + discovery only. v0.5.48 ships the handler-invocation bridge so plugin-contributed handlers can be wired through the existing hook-runner.

---

## [v0.5.46] — 2026-05-16

**Per-message "Fork from here" affordance in `/chat`.** Closes the long-deferred per-message fork UI piece.

### Why

v0.5.30 shipped the backend `from_message_id` API; v0.5.36 added session-level Fork via the SessionMenu (forks the full conversation). Per-message fork — branch from any specific point in the conversation — was deferred because it needed MCP message-id propagation from the chat-route's persistence to the client-side `ChatMessage`. v0.5.46 closes that propagation.

### What ships

#### `mcp/agent/lib/api/sessions.ts`

- `RawMessage` type gains an optional `id?: string` (the MCP-side message uuid, already returned by `Message.to_dict()` in `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/session_store.py`).
- `TranscriptMessage` interface gains optional `mcpId?: string`.
- `getSessionTranscript()` maps `m.id` → `mcpId` on each returned transcript message.

#### `mcp/agent/lib/api/chat.ts` + `mcp/agent/lib/stores/chat.ts`

- Both `ChatMessage` interfaces (the API shape + the store shape) gain optional `mcpId?: string`.

#### `mcp/agent/app/page.tsx`

- `handleSelectSession`'s history-build attaches `msg.mcpId` to each `ChatMessage` (user / assistant / system branches).
- New `handleForkFromMessage(mcpMessageId)` callback: POSTs to `/api/agent/sessions/{activeSessionId}/fork` with `{from_message_id: mcpMessageId}`, refreshes the sidebar, switches the chat pane to the new fork.
- Wired into `<MessageList onForkFromMessage={handleForkFromMessage} ... />`.

#### `mcp/agent/components/chat/message-list.tsx`

- `MessageListProps` gains `onForkFromMessage?: (mcpMessageId: string) => void`.
- Assistant-message rendering now wraps the bubble in a `group/asst-msg` and renders a `position: absolute` top-right "Fork from here" button (with `call_split` icon) when:
  1. `onForkFromMessage` is provided, AND
  2. `msg.mcpId` is set (i.e. loaded from persistence, not currently streaming), AND
  3. The row is not the last assistant message (streaming guard).
- Button is hover-revealed (`opacity-0 group-hover/asst-msg:opacity-100`) so it doesn't clutter the read view.

#### `mcp/agent/components/chat/assistant-message.tsx`

- Mirror change for the standalone `AssistantMessage` component (kept in sync with `MessageList`'s inline rendering for symmetry; future refactors that consolidate the two paths get the affordance from either entry point).

### Operator UX

1. Open `/chat`, select a session in the sidebar → MessageList renders the loaded history; each assistant message gets an `mcpId`.
2. Hover any assistant message → "Fork from here" button appears top-right.
3. Click → the agent POSTs to the fork API with the message's mcpId as `from_message_id`. MCP's `fork_session()` copies messages up-to-and-including that point into the new fork.
4. Sidebar refreshes (the new fork appears indented under the parent's date group via v0.5.45's reordering); chat pane switches to the new fork.
5. Send a message in the fork — it stays in the fork; the original session is unchanged.

### Streaming-message caveat

Messages that just streamed in DON'T have an `mcpId` until the operator reloads the session (clicks it again in the sidebar). Until then, the Fork button is hidden for those rows. The session-level Fork from `SessionMenu` (v0.5.36) still works as a coarse-grained alternative.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.46

- **No silent fork that bleeds session-scoped memory across the cut.** v0.5.30's `fork_session()` already excludes `session:<parent>` memory entries from the new fork; per-message fork inherits this discipline.

---

## [v0.5.45] — 2026-05-16

**Sessions sidebar groups forks under their root ancestor's date group instead of the fork's own creation date.** Closes the deferred cross-date-group reordering piece of v0.5.38/41.

### Why

Pre-v0.5.45 the sidebar grouped sessions by their own `timestamp` (Today / Yesterday / Previous 7 Days / Older). When an operator forked a Yesterday session today, the fork landed in "Today" while the parent stayed in "Yesterday" — visually disconnected even though they belong together logically. v0.5.45 fixes it: forks join the ROOT ancestor's group + render parent-first DFS order within the group.

### What ships

#### `mcp/agent/components/chat/session-sidebar.tsx`

- New `rootAncestor(session)` helper walks the `parentId` chain to find the top-level non-fork session. Cycle-defended via seen-set.
- New `depthOf(session)` helper (mirrors v0.5.41's `forkDepth`).
- `groupSessionsByDate(sessions)` rewritten to:
  1. Decorate each session with `(rootGroup, rootTime, depth, ownTime)`.
  2. Sort on `(rootGroup ASC, rootTime DESC, depth ASC, ownTime DESC)` — root group keeps a tree together; newer trees first within group; root renders before children; newer siblings first.
  3. Bucket into date groups (still 4: Today / Yesterday / Previous 7 Days / Older).

### Result

A parent created Yesterday + a fork created Today now both render under "Yesterday" with the parent on top and the fork indented below it. Operators see the full tree without scrolling between date groups.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.45

- **No removing the cycle defense.** Even if Phantom's current fork operation can't create cycles, future re-parenting flows might; the seen-set defense is cheap and load-bearing.

---

## [v0.5.44] — 2026-05-16

**`/observability/plugins` UI + `/api/v1/plugins` endpoint — discovery surface visible to operators.** Closes the visible-discovery half of #29's deferred consumer-side wiring.

### Why

v0.5.31 shipped the plugin-entry-points discovery scaffolding; v0.5.36 wired `log_discovery()` at MCP startup so counts surface in the boot log. v0.5.44 closes the UI gap: operators can browse the discovered plugin catalog without grepping container logs.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/plugins.py` (new)

- `register_plugin_routes(mcp)` adds `GET /api/v1/plugins`. Walks all five reserved groups via `discover_all()`, returns `{groups: { <group>: [PluginRef, ...] }, total}`. Bearer auth via MCP_TOKEN. Discovery-failure fallback returns 500 with the error; the page renders the error inline rather than crashing.

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py`

- Imports `register_plugin_routes`; calls it right after `log_discovery()`. Discovery happens once at boot; the endpoint re-walks on every request (cheap — `importlib.metadata.entry_points(group=...)` is fast on a small set).

#### `mcp/agent/app/api/agent/plugins/route.ts` (new)

- Thin proxy: `GET /api/agent/plugins` → MCP `/api/v1/plugins`. Same MCP_TOKEN-bearer pattern as the other agent passthroughs.

#### `mcp/agent/app/observability/plugins/page.tsx` (new)

- Discovery view rendering one section per group (`phantom.skills`, `phantom.connectors`, `phantom.hooks`, `phantom.scanners`, `phantom.providers`) with per-group metadata (label, icon, one-line about) + a count badge.
- Per-row table when plugins exist: name, distribution (dist_name + dist_version), target.
- "How to author a plugin" footer card with a copy-pasteable `pyproject.toml` snippet.
- Banner at the top noting "Discovery only — plugin-contributed handlers are NOT yet invocable. Cross-language handler bridge ships as a separate release."

### What's still deferred

- **Plugin install / uninstall from the UI.** Today's path is `docker exec phantom_agent pip install` + restart. A UI-driven install would need a pip-runner endpoint with strict allowlist + audit, which is its own design exercise.
- **Plugin-contributed handler invocation.** The discovery surfaces the entry point's target (`my_pkg.module:callable`); the agent's hook-runner / skill registry / connector loader don't yet know to import + invoke it. The cross-language bridge (Node hook-runner ↔ Python handler) is the meaningful remaining work.

### Forbidden post-v0.5.44

- **No auto-install of plugins at MCP boot from a config file or similar.** Plugin installation must remain an explicit operator action (today: `pip install`; future: UI-driven with audit). Auto-install from a config file is a foot-gun.

---

## [v0.5.43] — 2026-05-16

**Skills frontmatter `model` / `thinking` / `permissions` now flow through the skills listing API.** Closes the gap where v0.5.34's job-side skill overrides existed but operators couldn't see the recommendations anywhere else.

### Why

v0.5.34 wired the job dispatcher's `_parse_skill_frontmatter` to read skill MD's `model:` / `thinking:` / `permissions:` blocks and apply them as fallback defaults when jobs don't set their own. But the `skills_list_all` MCP tool (which feeds `/api/agent/skills`) ignored those three fields. Operators writing skill MD with `model: gemini-2.5-flash` had no way to see what their skill recommends from the UI — they'd have to open the MD file.

### What ships

#### `bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/skills_crud.py`

- `_build_record(...)` (inside `get_all_skills`) now includes three new fields on every skill record returned by `skills_list_all`:
  - `model: str | None` — the recommended model id from frontmatter, else null.
  - `thinking: bool` — recommended extended-thinking from frontmatter, defaults false.
  - `permissions: dict | None` — recommended permission policy from frontmatter, else null.
- Types are defensive: a non-string `model:`, non-bool `thinking:`, or non-dict `permissions:` falls back to the safe default. No crashes from malformed YAML.

### Downstream consumers (operator-visible after a UI follow-up)

The API now returns the data; consumers can be added incrementally:
- `/skills` page could render a "Recommends Pro + thinking" chip on cards where frontmatter sets these.
- Chat header's model dropdown could highlight the recommended model when the operator picks a specific skill to load.
