## [v0.3.4] — 2026-05-10

Two bug fixes in the `/skills` UI surface, both surfaced during v0.3.3 smoke testing on bupa-engine.

### Bug #1 — skills delete fails with confusing JSON parse error

**Symptom:** clicking the trash icon on any skill card returned `Unexpected non-whitespace character after JSON at position 2` to the operator. Skills delete had been broken for the entire v0.3.x line — no single skill could be removed via the UI.

**Root cause:** parameter-name drift between the Phase-11 gated wrapper at [bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py:1229](bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py:1229) (which is what [usecase/connector_loader.py](bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/connector_loader.py) registers as the `skills_delete` MCP tool) and the underlying [skills_crud.skills_delete](bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/skills_crud.py) function. The wrapper's signature was `async def skills_delete(name: str)`; the underlying function (and the legacy MCP Tool inputSchema in `skills_crud.py`) both expected `file_path`. FastMCP auto-derives a Pydantic validator from each tool's signature at registration time, so when the Next.js [/api/skills route](mcp/agent/app/api/skills/route.ts) DELETE handler called `mcpClient.callTool("skills_delete", { file_path: filePath })`, Pydantic rejected the call as "Unexpected keyword argument 'file_path'" and emitted a 2-line error string starting with `2 validation errors for call[skills_delete]\n...`. That error string got wrapped into the tool's `result.content[0].text`, which the Next.js side then `JSON.parse`'d — choking on the literal `2` character at byte-index 2 (immediately after the opening quote in the JSON-encoded text).

The "position 2" in the error message was a coincidence of the message text starting with the digit `2`; under different validation errors the position would have been different. This made the diagnostic harder than it should have been.

**Fix:** rename the wrapper's parameter from `name` → `file_path` to match the underlying function. Add a comprehensive docstring explaining the bug class so this doesn't regress. Also update the wrapper to JSON-parse the underlying `skills_crud.skills_delete` result (which returns a JSON-encoded string) so the gate's caller gets a structured dict consistent with the other Phase-11 gated wrappers in `self_mod_tools.py` (`jobs_delete`, `settings_reset`, etc.).

**Architectural sub-fix exposed by the parameter rename:** with the validator no longer rejecting the call, execution reached `gate_and_execute()` — which then blocked for the gate's default 5-minute timeout waiting on operator approval that never came (the operator was sitting at the UI Delete button, not at the `/approvals` queue). Empirically confirmed on phantom-vm: post-fix the SSE stream emitted `: ping - ...` keep-alives for 5 minutes, then returned `{"error":"tool 'skills_delete' approval timeout (approval_id=...)"}`. New error was parseable JSON (bug #1's symptom went away) but the UX was strictly worse than the original — a 5-minute spinner replaced the instant error.

The first attempt at fixing this (commit `b6900e8`, since superseded) sent `X-Phantom-Approval-Bypass: 1` from the Next.js side, relying on `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/trigger_context.py:TriggerContextMiddleware` to set a contextvar that `gate_and_execute` would then read. **Empirically the contextvar does NOT propagate from the Starlette middleware into the FastMCP streamable-HTTP tool dispatcher** — the agent's INFO log showed `approval bypass active for this request (trigger=ui:skills:delete)` from the middleware, then immediately afterward `ApprovalsBus.request id=... origin=unknown` from the gate, meaning `get_current_approval_bypass()` returned False inside the tool's async context. Suspected cause: FastMCP spawns the tool execution in a child asyncio task whose context was captured BEFORE the middleware ran, but the precise FastMCP-internal mechanism wasn't worth deep-diving since a cleaner architectural fix exists.

**The cleaner fix — REST endpoint that bypasses the gate by design.** Other resources (jobs, instances, providers, settings, personality, hooks, tasks, audit, KB) all expose dual surfaces on the MCP:

  1. **Tool path** — `tools/call name=<tool>` → goes through Phase-11 gated wrappers in `usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py`. Used by the chat agent for self-modification; gate fires unless the chat session has `approval_mode=bypass`.
  2. **REST path** — `/api/v1/<resource>/...` → goes through `bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/<resource>.py` route handlers that call the underlying CRUD functions directly. Used by the Next.js operator UI; gate is bypassed structurally because the REST handler doesn't go through the gated wrapper.

Skills were the **last destructive resource without its REST counterpart** — the only paths into skills mutation went through the gated tool wrapper. v0.3.4 closes that gap by adding [bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/skills.py](bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/skills.py) with `GET /api/v1/skills`, `GET/PUT/DELETE /api/v1/skills/{file_path:path}`, and `POST /api/v1/skills`, then routing the Next.js [/api/skills DELETE](mcp/agent/app/api/skills/route.ts) handler through `proxyToMcp(request, "/api/v1/skills/<path>")` instead of `mcpClient.callTool("skills_delete", ...)`. The Phase-11 gated tool wrapper at `self_mod_tools.skills_delete` is preserved unchanged — it still catches chat-driven (agent self-mod) deletions, which is exactly its purpose. Audit row for operator-direct deletes records `actor=user:operator` (set via `set_current_actor` in the route handler) so post-hoc review distinguishes operator-driven from agent-driven activity.

The other skills mutations (`POST` create, `PUT` update) are NOT gated in the manifest's `humanRequired` list, so they could continue to use the MCP tool path. But routing them through the REST endpoint is the architecturally consistent choice — and the new `api/skills.py` module supports them all, so a future patch can migrate the Next.js POST/PUT handlers without further server-side work.

**Why this slipped past CI:** there is no end-to-end test that exercises the full Next.js → MCP-stream → FastMCP-validator → gate path for skills_delete with the documented input shape. The validator + gate only fire at runtime when the tool is invoked, so static analysis (tsc, lint, build) couldn't catch either symptom. v0.3.4 doesn't add the missing test (out of scope for a patch); a follow-up should add a thin integration test that asserts `DELETE /api/v1/skills/<file_path>` with bearer auth returns `{success: true}` within ~5 seconds.

### Bug #2 — Skills page renders canonical `name` instead of operator-friendly `displayName`

**Symptom:** every skill card on `/skills` shows the snake_case canonical identifier (e.g. `cortex_xql_query_authoring`) as its title — confusing for operators who didn't author the skill, since the canonical name is meant for the agent's chat-prompt + job-binding paths, not the UI.

**Root cause:** the SkillCard JSX in [mcp/agent/app/skills/page.tsx](mcp/agent/app/skills/page.tsx) rendered `{skill.name}` only. The operator-friendly `displayName` has been correctly populated for several releases — the MCP's [skills_crud.\_build_record](bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/skills_crud.py) extracts it from MD frontmatter `display_name:` with fallbacks to first H1 heading, then filename stem; the page's `liveRowToSkillDef` mapper correctly assigned it to `skill.displayName`. But the JSX render never used it. Tooltip aria-labels (Download/Delete/Enable/Disable) DID use displayName, so the data was reachable from the component scope — only the visible card title was wrong.

**Fix:** invert the visual emphasis on the card. `displayName` is now the primary `<h3>` in headline font (text-lg, font-medium); `name` moves to a small monospace subtitle line under it (text-xs, font-mono, on-surface-variant/60) so operators can still grep by it during troubleshooting. The category label shifts down one line.

### Why these two fixes ship together

Both surfaced during the same v0.3.3 smoke session on bupa-engine, both touch the `/skills` operator surface, and both are one-file fixes. Bundling them avoids two separate v0.3.4 + v0.3.5 patch releases for what an operator perceives as one cluster of skills-page polish.

### Files changed

- [bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py](bundles/spark/mcp/src/usecase/builtin_components/self_mod_tools.py) — `skills_delete` Phase-11 gated wrapper: parameter rename `name` → `file_path` to match underlying function + JSON-decode of underlying result + comprehensive comment. The gated wrapper itself is unchanged in purpose: it still catches chat-driven self-mod attempts; the REST endpoint below carries operator-direct UI traffic.
- [bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/skills.py](bundles/spark/mcp/src/api/skills.py) — **NEW MODULE.** Operator-direct REST endpoints for the skills resource: `GET /api/v1/skills`, `GET/PUT/DELETE /api/v1/skills/{file_path:path}`, `POST /api/v1/skills`. Mirrors the dual-surface pattern that already exists for jobs/instances/providers/settings/etc. Sets `actor=user:operator` on the audit context so post-hoc review can distinguish operator-driven from agent-driven activity.
- [bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py](bundles/spark/mcp/src/main.py) — wire `register_skill_routes(mcp)` into the boot sequence next to the other route registrations.
- [mcp/agent/app/api/skills/route.ts](mcp/agent/app/api/skills/route.ts) — DELETE handler now uses `proxyToMcp(request, "/api/v1/skills/<path>")` instead of `mcpClient.callTool("skills_delete", ...)`. file_path is path-segment-encoded to handle category subdirectories cleanly.
- [mcp/agent/app/skills/page.tsx](mcp/agent/app/skills/page.tsx) — SkillCard JSX renders displayName as primary, name as monospace subtitle.

### Migration impact

PATCH release. SELECTIVE recreation per the v0.3.0 digest-pinning architecture: only `phantom-agent` rebuilds (it embeds both the page.tsx UI fix AND the bundled MCP wrapper signature fix); `xlog`, `caldera`, `phantom-browser`, `phantom-updater` all retag from v0.3.3 with identical content digests. caldera red-team operation state, xlog streaming workers, browser CDP cache, updater state all preserved across the upgrade. In-flight chat sessions are dropped on the phantom-agent recreation; skills + jobs + connectors + secret store + KEK persist (volume-backed).

---

## [v0.3.3] — 2026-05-10

A targeted patch to the `reset-ui-password.sh` recovery tool — fixes the silent-bug class that surfaced during v0.3.2 smoke testing on bupa-engine: **the script defaulted to the hardcoded username `phantom`, which is wrong for any install whose configured `UI_USER` is something else** (which is essentially every production install, since the setup wizard prompts for an operator username during first-run). The result was a successful-looking reset (audit event landed, hash written) followed by a failed login (the agent's login route enforces single-user mode by rejecting any username that doesn't match the configured `UI_USER`, returning 401 before checking the password hash). Also: the script now prints a prominent username banner before the password prompt so an operator can abort if the resolved username doesn't match the account they log in with.

The reset tool is the only file that changes. No image rebuilds. The fix lands in the next phantom-installer binary published by `release.yml` + the `reset-ui-password.sh` standalone Release asset.

### What the bug looked like

A real customer-state install (bupa-engine, `UI_USER=ayman`):

```
$ sudo /opt/phantom/reset-ui-password.sh
UI username [phantom]: ←enter (defaulted to phantom)
New password: ••••••••••
Confirm password: ••••••••••
✓ Password reset complete for user 'phantom'.
```

Then in the browser:
- Username: `ayman` (the configured operator) → 401 Invalid credentials (because `phantom`'s hash got reset, not `ayman`'s)
- Username: `phantom` (the script's default) → 401 Invalid credentials (because the agent's login route rejects any non-`UI_USER` username before hash check)

The audit log showed a successful `ui_password_set` event for `user:phantom` — making it look like the reset worked. The login failure looked like a typo. Diagnostic took ~10 minutes of forward-tracing the auth code path.

### Fix shape

Two architectural surfaces have to align for password reset to work:
1. **MCP side** stores hashes by username under `/app/data/secrets/ui/auth/<user>/password_hash` (multi-user-ready schema)
2. **Next.js login route** enforces single-user — username must equal the configured `UI_USER` from `setup.json`, otherwise 401 before any hash check

The reset tool's hardcoded `phantom` default came from the platform name, not from the configured operator. The fix: read `UI_USER` from the same `setup.json` the agent reads, with layered fallbacks.

### Username resolution (new behavior)

Resolution order:

1. `--user <name>` on the command line (explicit override)
2. `UI_USER` from `/app/runtime/setup.json` inside the container (the canonical source — same file the agent's auth code reads)
3. `UI_USER` from the container's env (legacy fallback for installs that pre-date setup.json's `UI_USER` field)
4. Hardcoded `phantom` (last resort; warned about loudly because it almost never matches a real install's configured operator)

The resolved username + its source is printed in a **prominent banner** before the password prompt:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Resolving Phantom UI account to reset...                       │
│                                                                 │
│    Username : ayman
│    Source   : setup.json UI_USER
│    Container: phantom_agent
│                                                                 │
│  IMPORTANT: this MUST match the username you log into the       │
│  Phantom UI with. The agent's login route rejects any other     │
│  username with 401 BEFORE checking the password — single-user   │
│  enforcement, not a hash mismatch. If the resolved username     │
│  above is not the account you log in with, hit Ctrl-C now and   │
│  re-run with --user <correct-username>.                         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The banner is shown even when `--user` is passed explicitly — operators who passed `--user` by mistake (typo, copy-paste from a stale runbook) get a chance to abort here before any state changes.

### Updated success message

The post-reset banner now shows resolved username + source + hash file path + the audit event name, plus a curl-based diagnostic command if the operator still can't log in (isolates browser-cookie issues from real verify-path failures):

```
✓ Password reset complete.

    Username    : ayman
    Source      : setup.json UI_USER
    Hash file   : /app/data/secrets/ui/auth/ayman/password_hash (inside phantom_agent)
    Audit event : ui_password_set (visible in /observability/events)

Next steps:
  1. Open the Phantom UI in your browser.
  2. Sign in with username 'ayman' and the password you just set.
     (Use a private/incognito window if your browser cached an old
     phantom_auth cookie or has stale autofill from before the reset.)
  3. Optionally rotate again from /profile after sign-in...
```

### Migration impact

- **No image rebuilds.** The reset tool is shipped as: (a) a heredoc embedded in the `phantom-installer` binary, (b) a standalone Release asset, (c) a file in the install kit tarball. v0.3.3's `release.yml` regenerates all three with the fixed script. Customers downloading v0.3.3+ get the fix automatically.
- **No customer state to migrate.** Existing installs keep whatever password they have. The fix only affects future invocations of the reset tool.
- **Operators on v0.3.x with the old reset script** can update by either re-running the v0.3.3 phantom-installer (which refreshes `/opt/phantom/reset-ui-password.sh`) or downloading just the `reset-ui-password.sh` Release asset and dropping it in place.

### Why this lands as v0.3.3

The fix is small (one file, ~80 lines net), surgical (no image rebuilds, no schema changes, no operator-facing breaking changes), and ships an operator-experience repair for a bug class that's been latent in every release since the script first landed in v0.1.35. Selective recreation under v0.3.x's digest-pinning architecture means: only the regenerated `phantom-installer` binary changes; xlog/caldera/agent/browser/updater all retag from v0.3.2 with identical digests. v0.3.2 → v0.3.3 is effectively zero-downtime for customer in-memory state.

---

## [v0.3.2] — 2026-05-10

A release-discipline patch focused on **upgrade ergonomics**. Three customer-experience gaps surfaced while smoke-testing v0.3.1 against bupa-engine (a real v0.2.x install upgrading to v0.3.1):

1. New image-default skills don't propagate to existing customer volumes (operator had to `docker exec cp -r /app/mcp/skills-default/* /app/skills/` manually after every release). v0.3.2 makes this automatic.
2. The `FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1` operator override documented in CLAUDE.md was wired into the dev compose at v0.2.5 but never propagated to the customer compose at `installer/docker-compose.yml`. Customers couldn't use the documented path.
3. The new `cortex-docs` connector shipped in v0.3.1 wasn't visible in the operator's `/marketplace` UI — the marketplace endpoint hand-curates its connector list and the new entry was missing.

All three are operator-visible papercuts on the upgrade path; v0.3.2 fixes them. No new features. No breaking changes. v0.3.0 → v0.3.1 → v0.3.2 hops are selective per the digest-pinning architecture: only `phantom-agent` recreates (it embeds the entrypoint change + the new marketplace entry); xlog/caldera/browser/updater retain in-memory state.

### Skills auto-merge: per-release marker in the volume

`entrypoint.sh` §1 (the skills bootstrap) is refactored to use a `phantom_mcp_skills/.seeded_version` marker file. Boot logic:

| Volume state | Action |
|---|---|
| `FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1` set | Always merge; stamp marker with running `PHANTOM_VERSION` |
| Volume empty (fresh install) | Seed; stamp marker |
| Marker missing OR marker version != running version | **Merge image defaults; stamp marker** — the new auto-rollout path |
| Marker matches running version | No-op (operator deletions stick) |

The merge is `cp -r /app/mcp/skills-default/* /app/skills/` — same MERGE-not-REPLACE semantics as before:

- Files in image only → copied IN
- Files in volume only (operator-created skills, retired-but-on-disk legacy ones) → **stay**
- Files in both → image version overwrites the volume copy

What this changes per upgrade type:

- **Fresh install on v0.3.2**: same as before — empty volume → seed → marker. No behavior change.
- **v0.3.1 → v0.3.2**: marker doesn't exist (v0.3.1 didn't write one) → merge → marker stamped `0.3.2`. The 14 image-baked skills appear without operator action.
- **v0.2.x or v0.1.x → v0.3.2**: same as v0.3.1 → v0.3.2 — marker missing, merge fires automatically. No more `docker exec` workaround.
- **v0.3.2 → v0.3.2 restart**: marker matches, no merge, operator deletions stick.
- **v0.3.2 → v0.3.3 future upgrade**: marker stale, merge fires automatically, the v0.3.3 skill set rolls out.
- **Operator wants to permanently retire an image-default skill**: Currently — delete the file from the volume + accept that the next upgrade will re-introduce it. A bundle-level skill denylist is the right long-term fix; tracked separately.

### `FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC` finally wired through customer compose

The `phantom-agent` service's `environment:` block in [`installer/docker-compose.yml`](installer/docker-compose.yml) gains a bare-name `- FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC` entry. Customers using the documented operator override now actually flow the env value into the container:

```bash
# This now works on customer installs (was a no-op pre-v0.3.2):
sudo FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1 docker compose -f /opt/phantom/docker-compose.yml \
  up -d --force-recreate phantom-agent
```

With the marker-driven auto-merge above, this override is rarely needed — its primary use case is "operator manually deleted a default skill on v0.3.x and wants it back without waiting for a new release." Documented in CLAUDE.md's force-reseed section.

### `cortex-docs` connector in `/marketplace`

`mcp/agent/app/api/marketplace/connectors/route.ts` gains a 5th entry alongside xlog/caldera/xsiam/web. The endpoint hand-curates marketplace specs derived from each connector's `connector.yaml`; v0.3.1's cortex-docs addition stayed inside the bundle but never made it into this surface, so operators couldn't find it via the UI.

The new entry surfaces the standard install flow: operator opens `/marketplace`, finds cortex-docs (publisher: kite-production, category: Security, tags: cortex/xql/documentation/research/palo-alto), reads the long description + per-tool docs + setup guide, clicks "Add instance", saves with default config (no required fields — the docs API is public). From that moment on, the agent advertises `cortex-docs/*` tools and the `cortex_xql_query_authoring` skill is operational.

**Critical principle reinforced**: connector instances are operator-created via the UI, NOT auto-migrated by the installer. v0.3.2 deliberately does NOT add an env-var auto-migration entry for cortex-docs (an earlier proposal that I walked back). Connectors with auto-migration entries (caldera, xsiam, xlog) are legacy patterns that mix install-time defaults with operator consent; the modern path — used by web (v0.1.27) and now cortex-docs — is consistent: marketplace → install → operator-driven instance creation. Existing instances created before any upgrade are preserved untouched; new instances are never created by the upgrade itself.

### Migration impact

- **v0.3.1 → v0.3.2**: selective recreation (only `phantom-agent` rebuilds, embeds entrypoint + marketplace changes). Skills auto-merge fires on first boot of v0.3.2 — operator sees their `/skills` page populate with any skills they were missing from previous releases (without losing customer-created ones). cortex-docs becomes findable in `/marketplace`.
- **v0.2.x or v0.1.x → v0.3.2**: same one-time container recreation as the v0.2.x → v0.3.x boundary in v0.3.0. Volumes preserved. Skills auto-merge fires on first boot. Operator finds cortex-docs in `/marketplace`, decides whether to install.
- **Customers already on bupa-engine-style state** (v0.3.1 + manual `docker exec` skill merge from a prior conversation): the v0.3.2 marker file gets written on next boot regardless of whether the merge "needs" to happen, so the auto-merge path picks up cleanly going forward.

### Implementation footprint

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `mcp/agent/entrypoint.sh` | Skills bootstrap refactored to marker-driven auto-merge (one-time refactor; ~60 lines including comments) |
| `installer/docker-compose.yml` | Added `- FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC` to phantom-agent env block |
| `mcp/agent/app/api/marketplace/connectors/route.ts` | Added cortex-docs entry (109 lines: 6 tools + 3 config fields + setup guide + version history) |

No image rebuilds beyond `phantom-agent` are needed (its source changed). caldera/xlog/browser/updater retain v0.3.1 digests via release.yml's conditional rebuild logic.

---

## [v0.3.1] — 2026-05-10

First feature release on the v0.3.x baseline. Adds the **`cortex-docs` tool connector** + the **`cortex_xql_query_authoring` foundation skill** — together, the agent now searches and looks up Palo Alto Networks Cortex public documentation (XDR / XSIAM / XSOAR / Cortex Cloud / AgentiX / Xpanse / XQL) to ground XQL query authoring in authoritative syntax docs.

This is also the **first release that exercises the v0.3.0 digest-pinning pipeline for a brand-new connector image** — no special handling, just an additional `phantom-connector-cortex-docs` line in the manifest. The new connector retains in-memory state across subsequent upgrades exactly the way caldera and xlog do; the architecture pays off on the very next connector we add.

### New tool connector — `cortex-docs`

A module-style Python connector wrapping four upstream scripts from the operator's `cortex-deep-search_sharable` skill kit (preserved verbatim under the connector's `src/` directory). The originals were authored as standalone CLIs; the connector layer adds a thin wrapper that catches `SystemExit` raised by the scripts' `sys.exit(1)` HTTP-error paths so a transient docs-API outage produces a structured error return instead of taking down phantom-agent.

Six tools exposed under the `cortex-docs/` namespace:

| Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `cortex-docs/search` | Full-text search across Cortex public docs (auto-detects product mentions in the query when `product` is omitted; supports explicit scope: `agentix` / `xdr` / `xsiam` / `xsoar` / `xql` / `cloud` / `dspm` / `cspm` / `ciem` / `xpanse`) |
| `cortex-docs/suggest` | Autocomplete for partial queries — useful for disambiguating noisy input |
| `cortex-docs/xql_lookup` | Focused lookup for an XQL stage or function. Searches with stage/function-aware query expansion, ranks results against authority heuristics (boosts canonical syntax pages, demotes release notes), fetches the top topic with auto-children fallback for stub DITA containers, returns answer-ready payload with title + summary content + reader URL + source citation |
| `cortex-docs/fetch_topic` | Full topic content given `map_id` + `topic_id` from a search hit |
| `cortex-docs/fetch_toc` | Full TOC for a publication — useful for browsing topic families |
| `cortex-docs/deep_research` | Heavyweight (1-3 min) multi-section research planner. Reserve for actual deliverables (whitepapers, partner briefings, migration guides). DO NOT call from the standard query-authoring path. |

**Connector style**: `module` (in-process Python). The upstream scripts are stdlib-only (urllib + json + re + argparse + pathlib), so no daemon, no per-instance container, zero extra Python deps. The Dockerfile exists for the v0.2 container-mode future per [`/help/architecture#connector-containers`](mcp/agent/app/help/architecture/page.tsx) — image gets built and digest-pinned uniformly with the rest of the connector family.

**No required configuration**. The Cortex docs API is public; no credentials. Optional `plannerModel` config flows through to `cortex-docs/deep_research`'s LLM-driven planner; absent the model, the planner falls back to its built-in heuristic plan and works fully offline-of-LLM.

### New foundation skill — `cortex_xql_query_authoring`

Orchestrates the operator's primary use case: **author authoritative XQL queries by chaining the operator's own example-query knowledge base with Palo Alto's official Cortex docs**. Six-step workflow:

1. Restate the query intent (dataset class, time window, output shape, product context).
2. Embedding-search the operator's KB for ~5 similar example queries (uses the agent's existing `knowledge_search` family — NOT a cortex-docs tool).
3. Parse those examples to extract XQL stages (`filter`, `comp`, `alter`, `dedup`, etc.) and functions (`arrayindexof`, `json_extract_scalar`, etc.) plus dataset names + lookups.
4. For each unique stage/function name, call `cortex-docs/xql_lookup` (parallelized when supported) to get the canonical Cortex doc topic.
5. Author the query using the KB examples as the **pattern prior** (org idioms, dataset names) and the cortex-docs lookups as the **syntax reference**.
6. Emit the query with per-stage/function citations.

The skill is also clear about what it doesn't do: when the operator's KB has no relevant examples, it tells them and offers a `cortex-docs/deep_research` follow-up (explicit opt-in, not part of the default flow because of the wall-clock cost).

### Bundle manifest

- `toolConnectors[]` extended with `cortex-docs` (`required: false` — agent functions without it; query authoring just falls back to operator-KB-only mode with a confidence note).
- `tools.allow` extended with `cortex-docs.*` — read-only, no approval-gating.
- 15 unit tests in `bundles/spark/connectors/cortex-docs/tests/test_connector.py` cover the SystemExit-translation wrapper contract (agents see `{ok: false, error}` instead of phantom-agent dying), the success-path payload shapes, and the `__all__` surface check. Live-API smoke ran during development against the public Cortex docs API and returned canonical `dedup` / `filter` / `arrayindexof` topics with full URLs in <2 seconds.

### Release pipeline (digest manifest extension)

`release.yml` now publishes 11 image digests instead of 10:

```ini
PHANTOM_VERSION=0.3.1
DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT=...
... (5 stack)
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_RUNTIME=...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_XLOG=...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_XSIAM=...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_CALDERA=...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_WEB=...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_CORTEX_DOCS=...      # NEW
```

The new key flows through the standard pipeline: written into the embedded manifest in `phantom-installer`, attached as a Release asset, and applied to `/opt/phantom/.env` at install time. The customer compose forwards it to `phantom-updater`'s environment block; the updater's `KNOWN_CONNECTORS` set was extended to recognize `cortex-docs` for the future container-mode flip.

### Migration impact

- **Customers on v0.3.0 → v0.3.1**: download the v0.3.1 phantom-installer binary, run it. Selective recreation kicks in: `phantom-agent` is the only stack-tier service whose source changed (it embeds the new connector code), so it gets recreated; xlog, caldera, browser, updater retain their content digests and **stay running with their in-memory state intact**. This is the digest-pinning architecture paying off in the first non-baseline release.
- **Customers on v0.2.x or earlier**: see the v0.3.0 entry's migration impact + [`installer/MIGRATION-FROM-V02X.md`](installer/MIGRATION-FROM-V02X.md). The migration is a one-time hop; once on v0.3.0 baseline, v0.3.0 → v0.3.1 is the selective form above.
- **Source-of-truth note**: the upstream scripts in `/Users/ayman/Documents/Coding/myworkassistant/cortex-deep-search_sharable/` are NOT modified by this release. The connector keeps a verbatim copy under `bundles/spark/connectors/cortex-docs/src/` so future upstream re-syncs are a `cp -f` away.

### Why this lands as v0.3.1, not v0.3.0+follow-up

By the v0.3.0 release-discipline rule (one breaking-change boundary, no follow-up debt), v0.3.0 had to ship cleanly with the digest-pinning architecture and nothing else. The cortex-docs work was scoped immediately after — independent commits, no breaking change, additive feature surface. v0.3.1 is the natural shape: same v0.3.x major, additive minor.

---

## [v0.3.0] — 2026-05-10 — **MAJOR (breaking change — new installer required)**

End-to-end **image digest pinning** replaces tag-based image references for the customer-facing stack. Container recreation now tracks **image content** (sha256), not version label — caldera + xlog + browser + updater retain in-memory state across upgrades that don't touch their source.

This is a backward-incompatible change: pre-v0.3.0 phantom-installer binaries cannot install or upgrade to v0.3.0+. Customers download a new v0.3.x installer (sealed to a single version because the digest manifest is embedded at build time) and run a one-time migration. Subsequent v0.3.x → v0.3.x+1 upgrades are selective and content-aware.

The v0.2.5 work prepared during the v0.2.4 → v0.2.5 cycle (FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC compose plumbing, doc cleanups, enterprise-discipline mandates, IAP tunnel port mappings) folds into this release — those commits are on main, never tagged independently, and ship under the v0.3.0 banner.

### Headline change: digest pinning

Pre-v0.3.0, every release recreated all five containers because the compose file's `image: foo:${PHANTOM_VERSION}` string changed even when release.yml's conditional-rebuild logic had retagged caldera/xlog/browser from the previous version's same-byte image. Compose treated the version-tag swap as a spec change and recreated the container regardless of whether image bytes had moved.

v0.3.0 swaps the entire stack to digest pinning:

```yaml
# Before (v0.2.x):
image: ghcr.io/kite-production/phantom-caldera:${PHANTOM_VERSION:-VERSION_DEFAULT}

# After (v0.3.0+):
image: ghcr.io/kite-production/phantom-caldera@${DIGEST_PHANTOM_CALDERA:-sha256:invalid_digest_run_installer_first}
```

When release.yml retags caldera from the previous version's digest (because no source changed), the digest in the new manifest equals the digest in the old manifest. The customer's `.env` `DIGEST_PHANTOM_CALDERA` value doesn't change. Compose's spec hash for caldera doesn't change. **Caldera doesn't recreate.** Same logic for xlog, phantom-browser, phantom-updater. Only services whose image content actually moved get touched.

The fail-loud fallback (`:-sha256:invalid_digest_run_installer_first`) is intentional — when an operator's `.env` is missing a `DIGEST_*` value, docker compose fails immediately with a clear hint pointing at the installer rather than silently pulling a wrong image.

### The release manifest

Each release publishes a `release-manifest-vX.Y.Z.env` file as a GitHub Release asset:

```ini
PHANTOM_VERSION=0.3.0
DIGEST_PHANTOM_AGENT=sha256:abc...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_XLOG=sha256:def...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CALDERA=sha256:ghi...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_UPDATER=sha256:jkl...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_BROWSER=sha256:mno...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_RUNTIME=sha256:pqr...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_XLOG=sha256:stu...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_XSIAM=sha256:vwx...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_CALDERA=sha256:yz0...
DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_WEB=sha256:123...
```

Same content is **embedded into the phantom-installer binary** at build time (via a new `__INSTALLER_DIGEST_MANIFEST__` substitution marker in [`installer/build-phantom-installer.sh`](installer/build-phantom-installer.sh)). The installer is fully self-contained — no runtime fetch from GitHub Releases needed during fresh installs. The Release asset exists separately so the phantom-updater service can fetch it during in-app upgrades, and so external automation (customer's deployment pipeline, audit tools) can pin to specific digests programmatically.

### Each phantom-installer is sealed to one version

Pre-v0.3.0, one installer binary could install any version (because tag refs let you pull any tag). v0.3.0+ each binary contains ONE manifest, so it can install ONE version. The `--upgrade-to N.N.N` flag now errors out with a clear pointer when N.N.N doesn't match the binary's stamp:

```
ERROR: --upgrade-to v0.3.1 doesn't match this binary's version (v0.3.0).

In v0.3.0+ each phantom-installer binary is sealed to a single version
because it embeds the digest manifest for that version's images. To
install v0.3.1, download the v0.3.1 installer binary…
```

Customers download the right binary for the version they want. The mental model becomes: "one binary = one installable version", consistent with how production-grade container deployments (Kubernetes, ECS, Nomad) treat image references.

### Phantom-updater redesign

The in-app updater (used by the "Update now" button in the agent UI sidebar) now drives a manifest-based upgrade flow:

1. Resolve target version from the latest GitHub Release (was: highest semver tag on GHCR — Releases is more authoritative because it carries exactly customer-installable versions, not pre-release CI builds).
2. Fetch `release-manifest-vTARGET.env` from that Release.
3. Compare each managed service's current digest vs. the manifest's target digest. If a service's digest is unchanged, skip it (no recreate).
4. Pull each changed image by digest (`docker pull foo@sha256:...`).
5. Apply the manifest to `/host/.env` (strip stale `PHANTOM_VERSION` + `DIGEST_PHANTOM_*` lines, append the new manifest as a clean block — same logic the installer uses for fresh-install/upgrade paths).
6. `docker compose up -d --no-deps <changed services>`. Compose sees the new digests in `.env`, recreates only those services. Containers whose digest didn't change keep running.
7. Wait for healthy.

Per-instance connector containers (the v0.2 design) also use digest pinning. The updater's `_connector_image_ref()` reads `DIGEST_PHANTOM_CONNECTOR_<ID>` from its environment when starting a new instance container — so each instance is reproducibly bound to a specific image-byte hash. Tag-pinning is a fail-loud fallback (operators see a yellow "tag (legacy)" badge in `/observability/connectors` if any container is in fallback mode).

### Operator-visible surfaces

Three new/extended UI surfaces:

- **`/observability/connectors`** gains an "Image digests" panel above the connector state-machine list. Two sub-tables: 5 stack-tier service rows (with pinned/legacy badge + 12-char-truncated digest + full digest in tooltip), and per-instance connector rows (variable). Operators can audit at a glance which exact image bytes each container is running.
- **`/api/agent/version`** extended to return an optional `digests` map. Backward-compatible: pre-v0.3.0 callers reading only `.version` still work.
- **`/api/agent/digests`** (new endpoint) — comprehensive view: stack-tier digest list (synchronous env-only) + per-instance connector digest list (proxied to phantom-updater's new `/api/v1/connectors/digests`). Used by the observability panel; canonical incident-response endpoint for "exactly which image bytes is each running container?".

### Migration impact

- **Fresh installs (v0.3.0+):** download the v0.3.x phantom-installer, run it. The binary embeds the v0.3.0 manifest; install proceeds normally. Operators see the new "Image digests" panel in `/observability/connectors` after first login.
- **Upgrade from v0.2.x:** **one-time recreation of all containers.** The compose file's image-ref shape changes from tag-based to digest-based, so docker compose treats every service as a spec change and recreates them. Volumes preserved (operator data, secrets store, KEK, on-disk caldera + xlog state). In-memory state lost during this single hop — caldera red-team operations, xlog streaming workers, in-flight chat sessions. Subsequent v0.3.x → v0.3.x+1 upgrades retain in-memory state for unchanged services.

  See [installer/MIGRATION-FROM-V02X.md](installer/MIGRATION-FROM-V02X.md) for the step-by-step migration guide.

- **Upgrade from v0.3.x → v0.3.x+1:** selective recreation. Changed-digest services recreate; unchanged-digest services keep running. No special migration steps; just download the new installer binary and run it (or use the in-app "Update now" button).

### Bundled fixes from the unreleased v0.2.5

The following work was prepared during the v0.2.4 → v0.2.5 cycle but never tagged as v0.2.5 — it ships under v0.3.0:

- **`FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1` now flows through to the container.** Pre-fix, [CLAUDE.md](CLAUDE.md) and the v0.2.4 migration impact section both promised that operators could run `FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1 docker compose up -d --force-recreate phantom-agent` to merge fresh image-baked skill templates into the live `/app/skills/` volume — but the variable was declared in `entrypoint.sh` and never wired into the compose `environment:` block, so the documented re-seed path was a silent no-op. v0.3.0 wires it through (one-line compose addition + comment block) for both the dev compose and the customer compose. The bug existed since v0.1.33 (when the agent-image-baked skill volume landed); the documented path now works as advertised.
- **`.env.vm` no longer spews `command not found` on source.** The repo-root `.env.vm` (operator-local credentials, gitignored) had been structured with a multi-line JSON value for `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS`. bash's `set -a && source .env.vm` parses line-by-line as shell assignments, so each indented JSON line tripped on the colon and emitted `command not found` errors. v0.3.0 documents the canonical Google Cloud SDK pattern (env var holds a path, not the JSON itself) with the JSON stored at `.gcp/service-account.json` (gitignored, mode 0600).
- **`VM_REMOTE_REPO` documented to point at the runner workspace.** Operator-local `.env.vm`'s `VM_REMOTE_REPO` had drifted to a stale legacy directory; the live runner workspace is `/home/ayman/actions-runner/_work/phantom/phantom`. CLAUDE.md now documents the correct path with a `docker inspect ... working_dir` recipe for verifying.
- **Approval-gates table + IAP tunnel recipes.** CLAUDE.md was unclear about which actions need approval (build = no, smoke test = no, release tag = yes). v0.3.0 adds an explicit approval-gates table at the top of "Remote-first workflow" and the canonical IAP service-port tunnel mappings (3000→3001 with +1 offset, MCP 8080→8081, Caldera 8888→8889, xlog 8999→9000) with bring-up-if-down recipes.
- **Enterprise discipline mandates** in CLAUDE.md: three new sections — pre-build context refresh (read these docs before any non-trivial build), quality-first principle (5 rules + anti-patterns list), observability verification during smoke test (what you smoke-test must be visible in `/observability/*`).

### Implementation footprint

8 files changed, 1,560 insertions / 204 deletions in the digest-pinning core (commit `35f6d9f`). Plus this release's docs touch CLAUDE.md, CHANGELOG.md, release-notes.ts, the architecture page, the installer's `.env.example` + `MIGRATION-FROM-V02X.md`, the user guide, and journeys.ts.

Pre-deploy gate: `tsc --noEmit` clean across all touched files; `npm run build` compiles all routes including new `/api/agent/digests`; updater's `python ast.parse` clean on the refactored main.py; installer build sanity (synthetic manifest substitution succeeds, output passes `bash -n`).

## [v0.2.4] — 2026-05-10

A skill-catalog curation + analytics-rules-coverage release. Adds the **`bootstrap_dataset_fields`** utility skill (solves XSIAM's "can't field-map what doesn't exist" chicken-and-egg), prunes the default skill list down to the curated set the platform actually delivers value with — removing pre-v0.1.33 evocatively-named scenarios, foundation stubs, and validation stubs that no longer reflect what xlog can realistically simulate. Display names refreshed across the kept skills to be human-readable 2-4 word phrases instead of the snake-case-everything format that surfaced in the UI. Plus an **analytics-rules audit pass** that backfills the network 5-tuple baseline (`src_ip`, `src_port`, `dst_ip`, `dst_port`, `protocol`, plus `user`/`hostname` when relevant) across every kept skill — and adds two new standalone scenarios (`port_scan_detection`, `large_file_upload_exfil`) that operators specifically asked for after running through XSIAM's port-scan and exfiltration analytics rule families.

### New: `foundation/bootstrap_dataset_fields`

Solves the XSIAM (and any modern SIEM's) chicken-and-egg problem: **you cannot model or field-map a dataset that has never received data.** Configuring an XSIAM ingestion broker for `vendor_proofpoint_tap_raw` and immediately opening the field-mapping UI shows nothing — XSIAM hasn't seen any rows yet. The platform won't let you write a parser for telemetry it has never seen.

The skill seeds each dataset in the operator's technology stack with ~100 events containing the maximum relevant set of fields from xlog's catalog. After running, every dataset in XSIAM has populated rows with realistic field shapes, and field-mapping work can proceed.

What it does, end-to-end:

1. Reads the operator's stack via `phantom_get_technology_stack`
2. For each (vendor, product, category) entry, picks the matching field template (curated per-class lists embedded in the skill body — firewall, EDR, NDR, proxy, email-gateway, DNS, WAF, load-balancer, VPN, cloud, SaaS)
3. Fires `xlog.create_data_worker` once per stack entry, all in parallel — `count: 100`, `interval: 0.1`, `duration_seconds: 10`
4. Total wall-clock time: ~10 seconds regardless of stack size
5. Total events generated: 100 × N stack entries (e.g. for a 13-source stack: 1,300 events covering 13 datasets)

**Why parallel data workers, not a scenario:** the scenarios tool exists for narrative kill-chains where stages are coupled. This skill is per-source independent bootstrap; parallel workers give per-source independence (one worker failing doesn't block others) and per-source observability via `xlog.list_workers`.

The 11 per-class field templates are starting opinions, embedded in the skill body. **Operators can edit any class's field set via `/skills` → Save** and the agent picks up the change on next invocation. If a class isn't covered (operator has a stack class outside the templates), the agent falls back to a minimal-fields recipe and surfaces a warning.

### Skill catalog curation

Image-baked skill list trimmed from 21 to 13. Kept set is the v0.2.2-overhauled vendor-agnostic batch + the new bootstrap utility + the two new standalone analytics-rules-coverage scenarios:

| Category | File | Display name |
|---|---|---|
| `foundation/` | `bootstrap_dataset_fields` | **Bootstrap dataset fields** |
| `scenarios/` | `bruteforce_vpn_to_lateral` | **VPN brute force chain** |
| `scenarios/` | `phishing_to_cloud_takeover` | **Phishing cloud takeover** |
| `scenarios/` | `dns_tunneling_c2` | **DNS tunneling C2** |
| `scenarios/` | `web_app_to_webshell_to_exfil` | **Web shell exfiltration** |
| `scenarios/` | `malicious_email_to_endpoint_persistence` | **Email malware persistence** |
| `scenarios/` | `oauth_consent_to_cloud_pivot` | **OAuth consent abuse** |
| `scenarios/` | `lolbin_lateral_movement` | **LOLBin lateral movement** |
| `scenarios/` | `recon_to_account_probe` | **External recon scan** |
| `scenarios/` | `insider_saas_data_exfil` | **Insider SaaS exfiltration** |
| `scenarios/` | `cloud_privilege_escalation` | **Cloud privilege escalation** |
| `scenarios/` | `port_scan_detection` *(new)* | **Port scan detection** |
| `scenarios/` | `large_file_upload_exfil` *(new)* | **Large file upload exfil** |

12 skills retired from the image:

- 7 pre-v0.1.33 evocatively-named scenarios: `alpha_and_omega`, `crossroads`, `everything_is_now`, `ink_and_foil`, `knock_knock`, `midnight_butterfly`, `sic_mundus_creatus_est` — kept in v0.2.2 for backward muscle-memory but operators have moved to the vendor-agnostic batch
- 2 foundation stubs: `create_device_topology`, `generate_shared_iocs` — were 270-byte placeholders never filled in
- 2 validation skills: `xsiam_top20_coverage`, `validate_ioc_correlation` — superseded by the v0.2.2 batch's per-skill rule-trigger tables (the 10 scenarios collectively cover all 20 rules `xsiam_top20_coverage` was a catalog of)
- 1 workflow: `purple_team_exercise` — too aspirational for current xlog capabilities; reservable for the v0.3 workflow-engine work

### Analytics-rules audit — network 5-tuple baseline

Operator feedback after running through XSIAM analytics rules with v0.2.2 skills: **"the minimum I should see in any network event is source IP, source port, protocol, user, and hostname when those make sense for the data source class."** Several stages in the v0.2.2 batch had `src_ip` + `dst_ip` + `dst_port` but were missing `src_port` and/or `protocol` — common omission because the synthetic data still parses fine in XSIAM, but it leaves correlation queries weaker (the same scan/upload pattern looks the same regardless of which ephemeral port the workstation chose, so analysts pivoting on `src_port` see nothing).

v0.2.4 audits all 10 v0.2.2 scenarios + the bootstrap field templates and backfills the missing tuple components in network-relevant stages:

| Skill | Stage | Fields added |
|---|---|---|
| `dns_tunneling_c2` | Stage 1 (DGA probes) | `src_port`, `dst_port: 53`, `protocol: udp/tcp` |
| `dns_tunneling_c2` | Stage 2 (encoded subdomain tunnel) | `src_port` (different ephemeral set than Stage 1 — distinct sessions), `dst_port: 53`, `protocol: udp/tcp` |
| `phishing_to_cloud_takeover` | Stage 2 (proxy click-through) | `src_port`, `dst_port: 443`, `protocol: tcp` |
| `web_app_to_webshell_to_exfil` | Stages 1+2 (WAF SQLi probes + injection) | `src_port`, `protocol: tcp` |
| `recon_to_account_probe` | Stage 3 (WAF directory traversal) | `src_port`, `protocol: tcp` |
| `insider_saas_data_exfil` | Stage 3 (firewall outbound volume) | `src_port` |
| `bootstrap_dataset_fields` | DNS template | `src_port`, `dst_port: 53`, `protocol: udp/tcp` |
| `bootstrap_dataset_fields` | WAF template | `src_port`, `protocol: tcp` |
| `bootstrap_dataset_fields` | Load-balancer template | `src_port`, `protocol: tcp` |

The baseline `src_port` lists use realistic ephemeral-range values (`49152`, `51234`, `57890`, `60123`) that match the IANA dynamic/private port range — important for analytics rules that distinguish "ephemeral high-numbered" from "well-known low-numbered" source ports. Stages that already had the full 5-tuple (firewall stages in `bruteforce_vpn_to_lateral`, NDR stages, EDR stages with command-line-only context where ports don't apply) were left unchanged.

### Two new standalone analytics-rules skills

The v0.2.2 batch covers analytics rule families *as part of multi-stage chains* — `recon_to_account_probe` includes a port-scan stage as its opener; `insider_saas_data_exfil` includes a personal-cloud-upload stage. But operators on real engagement work asked for **focused, single-purpose skills** that fire just one rule family at high fidelity, without the chain context. Two new skills target the two most-frequently-requested:

#### `scenarios/port_scan_detection` *(new)*

Three-pattern reconnaissance skill focused exclusively on port-scan detection. Each pattern fires a different XSIAM analytics rule and represents a different attacker tradecraft:

| Stage | Pattern | Detection rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Horizontal scan** — one source IP → many internal IPs on the **same** port (the "find all SSH boxes" sweep) | Port Scan Detection (single source) |
| 2 | **Vertical scan** — one source IP → one internal IP across **many** ports (port enumeration) | Port Scan Vertical |
| 3 | **Distributed scan** — many source IPs → many destinations on common service ports (slow stealth scan) | Distributed Port Scan |

Each stage emits firewall events with the full 5-tuple populated (`src_ip`, `src_port`, `dst_ip`, `dst_port`, `protocol: tcp`, `action: deny`/`drop`/`reset`) plus optional WAF events for layer-7-visible probe patterns. Total ~600 events across the three stages in ~3 minutes wall-clock. MITRE coverage: `TA0043: Reconnaissance` (T1595.001 IP block scanning, T1595.002 vulnerability scanning).

#### `scenarios/large_file_upload_exfil` *(new)*

Three-pattern exfiltration skill focused exclusively on large outbound-upload patterns. Operators wanted a focused exfil skill (separate from the multi-stage `insider_saas_data_exfil` chain) that hammers the personal-cloud-storage rule family from three different angles:

| Stage | Pattern | Detection rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Workstation → file-sharing service** outbound bulk upload via HTTPS (proxy-visible) | Data Exfiltration to Personal Cloud Storage (Rule #20) |
| 2 | **Server → file-sharing service** outbound bulk upload (anomalous direction — web servers should rarely initiate outbound to personal cloud) | Anomalous Outbound Volume from Server |
| 3 | **SaaS bulk-download → personal-cloud re-upload** (M365 OneDrive download chain followed by Dropbox upload from same workstation, tied by user+IP across both data classes) | Bulk SaaS Download Pattern |

Insider profile: `r.kowalski@bupa.example`, workstation `wks-rkowalski-01` at `10.10.20.88`. Each stage emits proxy + firewall events with `bytes_sent` distributions in the 5-50 MB range, `category: file-sharing` / `personal-cloud-storage`, `domain: dropbox.com`/`drive.google.com`, plus high-volume `bytes_sent`/`bytes_received` imbalance. MITRE coverage: `TA0009: Collection` (T1213.001/002), `TA0010: Exfiltration` (T1041, T1048.003, T1567.002).

### Display name refresh

Pre-v0.2.4 the skills used arrow-and-hyphen technical descriptions like `Brute force → VPN compromise → Lateral movement` as their `displayName`, which surfaced in the UI as long visual tokens that crowded the `/skills` list. v0.2.4 shortens each to a 2-4 word human-readable phrase. The model-facing canonical name (snake_case file basename) stays the same — that's what the agent's chat-prompt sees and what skill-binding jobs reference. Only the human-facing label changes:

| File | Was | Now |
|---|---|---|
| `bruteforce_vpn_to_lateral.md` | "Brute force → VPN compromise → Lateral movement" | **VPN brute force chain** |
| `phishing_to_cloud_takeover.md` | "Phishing → Credential harvest → Cloud takeover" | **Phishing cloud takeover** |
| `web_app_to_webshell_to_exfil.md` | "Web app compromise → Web shell → Data exfiltration" | **Web shell exfiltration** |
| `malicious_email_to_endpoint_persistence.md` | "Malicious email → Endpoint compromise → Persistence" | **Email malware persistence** |
| `oauth_consent_to_cloud_pivot.md` | "OAuth consent abuse → Cloud privilege pivot" | **OAuth consent abuse** |
| `lolbin_lateral_movement.md` | "Living-off-the-land lateral movement" | **LOLBin lateral movement** |
| `recon_to_account_probe.md` | "External recon → Web app discovery → Account probe" | **External recon scan** |
| `insider_saas_data_exfil.md` | "Insider data exfiltration via SaaS" | **Insider SaaS exfiltration** |
| `cloud_privilege_escalation.md` | "Cloud privilege escalation chain" | **Cloud privilege escalation** |

`dns_tunneling_c2.md` already had a short displayName ("DNS tunneling C2") and was unchanged.

### Migration impact

- **Fresh installs (v0.2.4+)** ship 13 skills total in the image, all realistic for current xlog (1 foundation utility + 12 attack scenarios; the 12 includes the 10 v0.2.2 vendor-agnostic chains + the 2 new standalone analytics-rules-coverage scenarios)
- **Existing installs upgrading** retain whatever's seeded in their `phantom_mcp_skills` named volume; the 12 deletions don't auto-remove from populated volumes. Operators wanting a clean slate have three options:
  - `docker compose down -v` (destructive — drops other state too)
  - `docker exec phantom_agent rm /app/skills/scenarios/<file>.md` for surgical removal of specific old skills
  - `docker compose up -d --force-recreate -e FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC=1 phantom-agent` — re-seeds /app/skills from /app/mcp/skills-default. **NOTE:** this is a MERGE not a REPLACE — it copies image defaults into the volume but does not remove files NOT in the image. So old scenarios stay unless surgically removed first.

The display-name refresh is automatic on upgrade — the agent reads the updated frontmatter from disk on every `skills_list_all` call.
