## [v0.2.3] — 2026-05-09

Two operator-reported issues. Truncation in chat responses + missing wire events in session exports — both surfaced during v0.2.2 attack-chain skill execution on `bupa-engine`. Diagnostic discipline: cross-referenced against the working `Trevor_-_Bot.yml` Slack integration that also runs on Vertex/Gemini with heavy tool-calling traffic; the only architectural difference was a hardcoded `maxOutputTokens: 4096` cap in Phantom's chat handler.

### Truncation fix — `maxOutputTokens` cap removed entirely

The Phantom chat handler hardcoded `maxOutputTokens: 4096` on every Gemini API call (in three callsites in [mcp/agent/app/api/chat/route.ts](mcp/agent/app/api/chat/route.ts) — main loop at line 928, partial-summary fallback at line 2606 with `2048` (even smaller), retry path at line 2668). Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview's natural output ceiling is 65,536 tokens. Phantom was capping at 6.25% of that. Multi-step responses with rich tool-output narration — the v0.1.36 backup/restore plan, the v0.2.2 attack-chain skill executions, anything with multiple stages of explanation — routinely hit the cap mid-sentence. Operators saw `finishReason: MAX_TOKENS` in telemetry but no operator-actionable error, just truncated content.

v0.2.3 removes the cap entirely at all three callsites (the `generationConfig` field is now optional in the type and unset in every call). Gemini decides its own output length. The model writes as much as the response naturally needs. Same approach as the working `Trevor_-_Bot` integration which never set the cap.

The context-budget reservation (`reservedOutput` in the chat route, used to trim INPUT context to leave room for output) bumps from `4096` to `65536` to match the model's natural ceiling. This is INPUT-side accounting (how much context we trim to fit), NOT an output cap. Gemini still writes as much as the response needs; we just leave room.

The pre-existing v0.1.28 work raised the **tool-result REPLAY ceiling** from 500 bytes to 1 MiB — that's an INPUT-side concern (truncating tool results when re-sending in the next turn). Different limit. The OUTPUT-side `maxOutputTokens: 4096` was never touched in v0.1.28 and is what bit operators on multi-stage skill executions.

Visible side effect of the fix: chat responses that were silently truncating now complete naturally. Token costs may rise slightly on long responses (the model writes more text when it has room), but the operator-visible quality of multi-stage attack-chain plans is dramatically improved.

### Wire-event trace export — new `events` format on `/sessions/{id}/export`

Operator complaint: the live telemetry panel shows wire events (tool_call, tool_result, meta, model, etc.) but the existing markdown/json/yaml session exports only contain persisted message turns. v0.2.3 adds a new `events` format to the existing `/api/v1/sessions/{id}/export` endpoint that derives a flat event-list timeline from the session's messages + their meta blobs. Same data the live telemetry panel reconstructs after a session reload, exposed as a downloadable JSON file.

What the events export contains, per persisted message:

| Source message role | Events emitted |
|---|---|
| `user` | 1 event: `{ts, type: "user_message", content, meta}` |
| `assistant` | 1 event: `{ts, type: "assistant_text", content, meta}` (meta carries run_id, model, finish_reason, etc) |
| `tool` | 2 events: `{ts, type: "tool_call", tool, args}` + `{ts, type: "tool_result", tool, result, status, duration_ms, error}` |

What it does NOT contain: streaming-only events (`text_delta` chunks, `cache_hit` details, `turn_cost`, `done`) because those are SSE-stream-only and never persisted. For those, capture from the live SSE stream during execution.

Output shape:

```json
{
  "session": { ... },
  "events": [
    {"ts": "2026-05-09T16:08:33Z", "type": "user_message", "content": "...", "meta": {}},
    {"ts": "2026-05-09T16:08:45Z", "type": "tool_call", "tool": "skills_list_all", "args": {}},
    {"ts": "2026-05-09T16:08:45Z", "type": "tool_result", "tool": "skills_list_all", "result": "...", "status": "success", "duration_ms": 12340},
    ...
  ],
  "event_count": 16,
  "schema_version": 1,
  "exported_at": "2026-05-09T17:30:00Z",
  "note": "Events are DERIVED from persisted messages..."
}
```

UI plumbing: the chat-header export dropdown now shows two sections — "Session transcript" (yaml/json/markdown, existing behaviour) and "Wire-event trace" (Events JSON, new). Same divider+section-header pattern in the session-sidebar per-session menu. Downloaded file lands as `session-<short-id>.events.json` so it's distinguishable from the full-session JSON export at a glance.

### Internal lessons

- **The cap was an artifact of pre-Vertex-cache-era constraints.** The 4096 number predates v0.1.7's introduction of `cachedContent.create()` for the system prompt and v0.1.25's Vertex/Gemini 429 backoff — the days when every output token was a full per-call cost concern. With prefix caching, multi-MB tool replays, and 1M-token context windows on modern Gemini models, that cap is solving a problem that's no longer load-bearing.
- **Cross-checking against working integrations is high-leverage debugging.** The `Trevor_-_Bot.yml` Slack-side agent uses the same MCP server, same Gemini SDK, same manual tool-call loop, same 429 backoff strategy. The working pattern was already in-house; we just had to compare to find the one config line that was different.

---

## [v0.2.2] — 2026-05-09

A scenario-skill overhaul. The 12 v0.1.33 advanced attack-scenario skills (`ransomware_double_extortion`, `apt_long_dwell_espionage`, `business_email_compromise`, etc.) were aspirationally sophisticated — they encoded attack chains the platform couldn't yet realistically simulate end-to-end with the current xlog vendor/product/observable inventory. Operators on customer-facing deployments hit the limit when invoking them: the chains referenced telemetry shapes xlog could approximate but not faithfully reproduce. **v0.2.2 replaces those 12 scenarios with 10 new vendor-agnostic, field-specific skills tuned to xlog's actual capabilities.**

### What's new — 10 vendor-agnostic attack-scenario skills

Each speaks data-source CLASSES (firewall, EDR, NDR, proxy, email-gateway, DNS, WAF, VPN, SaaS, cloud) and resolves to the operator's specific vendor/product at runtime via `phantom_get_technology_stack`. The same skill works on any customer's stack — Palo Alto + F5 + Vectra + MDE on one deployment, CrowdStrike + Fortinet + ExtraHop + Defender on the next, no skill body changes needed.

| Skill | Attack chain | Triggers XSIAM rules |
|---|---|---|
| `bruteforce_vpn_to_lateral` | Password spray → focused brute force → impossible-travel sign-in → NDR detects east-west SMB/RPC | 1, 2, 3, 4, 12 |
| `phishing_to_cloud_takeover` | Phishing email → click → impossible travel → OAuth consent abuse → forwarding rule → bulk SaaS download | 4, 8, 9, 10, 11, 20 |
| `dns_tunneling_c2` | DGA probes → long-subdomain tunneling → firewall confirms outbound to attacker NS | 5, 6, 7 |
| `web_app_to_webshell_to_exfil` | WAF SQLi probes → successful injection → web shell drop + first call → outbound exfil from web tier | 15, 16, 20 |
| `malicious_email_to_endpoint_persistence` | Email macro doc → Office spawns PowerShell → LSASS dump → defender disable + scheduled task | 9, 13, 14, 19 |
| `oauth_consent_to_cloud_pivot` | OAuth consent phishing → impossible travel → forwarding rule → cloud IAM SA key creation | 4, 10, 11, 17 |
| `lolbin_lateral_movement` | EDR sees signed-binary chain (certutil/mshta/wmic/rundll32/regsvr32) → NDR sees lateral SMB/RPC → AMSI bypass + defender stop | 12, 13, 19 |
| `recon_to_account_probe` | Firewall sees port scan → proxy sees directory-traversal probes → WAF SQLi recon → VPN account probing | 1, 7, 8, 15 |
| `insider_saas_data_exfil` | Privileged user bulk-downloads SaaS → workstation uploads to personal cloud storage → firewall confirms outbound volume | 11, 20 |
| `cloud_privilege_escalation` | Cloud SA + key creation → role-binding to Owner → data access from new region using new SA's key | 4, 17, 18 |

### Format characteristics

- **Vendor-agnostic body language**: skill text says "the firewall in your stack", never "Palo Alto firewall"; recipes use placeholder syntax (`<stack[firewall].vendor>`) the agent resolves at runtime
- **Field-specific recipes**: every stage names exact xlog field keys (from the 1,169-field catalog) populated with realistic values that fire the targeted rules
- **Field semantics blocks**: each stage explains WHY each populated field matters and what value range fires the analytic — turns the skill from "copy-paste recipe" into a teaching artifact
- **Narrative thread block**: shared IPs/hosts/users/sessions across all stages so analysts pivoting in the SIEM can follow the chain end-to-end
- **Verification table**: per-skill objective checklist for "did all expected alerts fire?" with troubleshooting branches when any didn't
- **"If missing from stack" branches**: graceful degradation when the operator's tech-stack lacks a class the skill depends on (e.g. NDR missing → substitute firewall east-west events)
- **Pre-flight reminder**: every skill instructs the agent to call `phantom_get_technology_stack` once at start and build the category lookup before any stage executes
- **Tear-down + adapting-per-deployment footers**: `xlog.list_workers` / `xlog.kill_worker` cleanup + a note on swapping illustrative IPs/users for customer-specific values

### XSIAM analytics coverage

The 10 skills together trigger all 20 of the validation/`xsiam_top20_coverage` rules. Average ~3.4 rules per skill; every rule appears in at least 1 skill, most in 2-3.

### What stays untouched

The 7 pre-v0.1.33 evocatively-named scenarios (`alpha_and_omega`, `crossroads`, `everything_is_now`, `ink_and_foil`, `knock_knock`, `midnight_butterfly`, `sic_mundus_creatus_est`) remain in `scenarios/` — they're cultural artifacts, operator-known names, and weren't part of the v0.1.33 advanced-batch the user wanted overhauled. A v3 pass to bring them into the same vendor-agnostic discipline can happen later if the operator wants full uniformity.

The `validation/xsiam_top20_coverage.md` skill stays at its v0.1.33 form for now; refreshing it to point at the new vendor-agnostic recipes is queued for a follow-up release.

### Migration impact

- **Fresh installs (v0.2.2+):** 22 skills total in the image (24 - 12 + 10), all of which are realistic for xlog
- **Existing installs upgrading:** their `phantom_mcp_skills` named volume still has whatever was originally seeded; the 12 v0.1.33 deletions in this release don't auto-remove anything from a populated volume. Operators who specifically want to retire the v0.1.33 scenarios from their volume should `docker compose down -v` (drops volume — destructive) or manually `docker exec phantom_agent rm /app/skills/scenarios/<file>.md`. **Most operators won't need to do this** — the 12 v0.1.33 skills still work in the volume, they're just no longer the recommended attack-simulation surface.

### Skill bodies

Every skill body is ~230-410 lines of structured runbook with explicit per-field semantics. Total: ~3,010 lines of new skill content across the 10 files.

---

## [v0.2.1] — 2026-05-09

A targeted follow-up to v0.1.36 closing one upgrade-path gap. The v0.1.36 phantom-installer started shipping `reset-ui-password.sh` via an embedded heredoc — but customers who upgrade their stack with `phantom-installer --upgrade-to 0.1.36` using their existing OLDER (pre-v0.1.36) installer binary do not get the script extracted, because the older binary's template lacks the heredoc block. They get every container-side feature (backup/restore, sidebar reorg, connector refactor) but no on-disk recovery tool. v0.2.1 makes that recoverable in three layered ways without requiring a re-download of the installer binary.

### Recovery tool delivered via the agent image (in-product self-heal)

- **`/api/agent/recovery/reset-ui-password`** — new agent-side endpoint, cookie-auth gated, serves the canonical `reset-ui-password.sh` bytes. Baked into the phantom-agent image at `/app/tools/reset-ui-password.sh` (Dockerfile `COPY installer/reset-ui-password.sh /app/tools/...`). Anyone who upgrades the agent image to v0.2.1+ via any path — fresh installer, `--upgrade-to`, manual `docker compose pull` — gets this endpoint.
- **UI affordance** — `/settings/backup-restore` grew a "Recovery tools" section with a **Download `reset-ui-password.sh`** button. Click → browser saves the file → operator copies to `/opt/phantom/` (the page shows the `install -m 755 ...` one-liner). One-click recovery for anyone who skipped the heredoc-extraction path.

### Installer template self-check (GitHub release fallback)

The v0.2.1 installer template gains a defensive self-check that runs after the heredoc block:

```bash
if [[ ! -s "$INSTALL_DIR/reset-ui-password.sh" ]] || \
   grep -q '__INSTALLER_RESET_PASSWORD_SH__' "$INSTALL_DIR/reset-ui-password.sh"; then
  # heredoc didn't produce a usable script — fall back to GH release
  curl -fsSL "https://github.com/.../v$WANT_VERSION/reset-ui-password.sh" \
    -o "$INSTALL_DIR/reset-ui-password.sh"
  chmod 755 "$INSTALL_DIR/reset-ui-password.sh"
fi
```

This is forward-looking insurance: any future template change that accidentally breaks the substitution (e.g. dropping `__INSTALLER_RESET_PASSWORD_SH__` from `markers` in `build-phantom-installer.sh`) auto-heals from the GitHub release. In offline/air-gapped environments where curl fails, the operator gets a clear hint pointing at the in-product UI fallback (Settings → Backup & Restore → Recovery tools → Download), which doesn't need external network.

### Standalone release asset

`release.yml` now uploads `reset-ui-password.sh` (and `.sha256`) as standalone GitHub Release assets alongside the existing four installer artifacts. This is what the installer self-check curls; it also lets operators wget just the script directly:

```bash
curl -L -o reset-ui-password.sh \
  https://github.com/kite-production/phantom/releases/download/v0.2.1/reset-ui-password.sh
sudo install -m 755 reset-ui-password.sh /opt/phantom/
```

### Why three layers

The three paths cover orthogonal failure modes:

| Path | Failure mode it covers |
|---|---|
| Agent-image-baked + UI download | Customer never re-downloads installer; agent image upgrades retroactively give them recovery |
| Installer self-check + GH release fallback | Customer DOES re-download installer; even if a future heredoc breaks, fallback heals it |
| Direct GH release wget | Operator wants just the script, no UI access, no installer re-run |

Customers who upgraded v0.1.34 → v0.1.36 with their old installer binary should now see the Recovery tools section in `/settings/backup-restore` once they upgrade further to v0.2.1 (any path — fresh installer, `--upgrade-to`, manual pull). One click + one shell `install -m 755` lands them with a working recovery tool.

---

## [v0.1.36] — 2026-05-09

A wide release. Five deliverables: (1) caldera + xsiam connector tool-call paths now read from the InstanceStore at runtime, completing the single-source-of-truth migration that v0.1.34 started for xlog; (2) a customer-facing CLI for resetting a forgotten Phantom UI password, shipped in the install kit; (3) a pre-deploy drift check that compares the VM's `docker-compose.yml` to the canonical local file so silent drift can never mask a sync; (4) the operator UI's admin pages (Models, Providers, plus the new Backup & Restore) consolidated under the **Settings** group in the sidebar; (5) **a complete backup and restore feature** — one zip captures personality, connector instances + cleartext secrets, runtime jobs, memory entries, all skills, and knowledge bundle docs; restore replays them onto a destination deployment in dependency order.

### Backup & Restore (`/settings/backup-restore`)

Operators get a single **Download backup** button that produces `phantom-backup-<timestamp>.zip` containing the deployment's complete operator-owned state. Companion **Restore** flow accepts the same zip on any deployment (the same one, a fresh install, a different host with a different `PHANTOM_SECRET_KEK`) and replays each section.

What's in the zip:
- `manifest.json` — schema_version, phantom_version, created_at, section counts, restore order, plaintext-secrets warning
- `personality.json` — the SqlitePersonalityStore blob
- `instances.json` — InstanceStore rows + **cleartext** connector secrets (so the destination's KEK can re-encrypt on restore)
- `memory.json` — Memory entries with embedding BLOBs **stripped** (they're dim-bound; the destination re-embeds on next semantic search)
- `jobs.json` — runtime job definitions only (manifest jobs reseed from `manifest.yaml` at boot)
- `skills/<category>/<file>.md` — every operator skill MD preserving category structure
- `knowledge/<kb-name>/<doc-id>.md` — knowledge bundle doc content for reference (read-only at runtime)

Restore order: personality → instances+secrets → skills → memory → knowledge (no-op) → jobs. The order matters because runtime jobs may reference connector tools (e.g. `phantom_create_data_worker`) that need their instance to exist + be enabled before the first cron tick. Personality goes first because it has no dependencies and applies cleanly even if a later section fails.

API surface:
- `GET /api/agent/backup` — auth-gated via `phantom_auth` cookie; assembles zip and streams it as a download. Server-side calls `/api/v1/instances?include_secrets=true` (a v0.1.36-introduced MCP flag, bearer-gated, mirroring v0.1.34's ProviderStore detail-endpoint pattern) plus `/api/v1/personality`, `/api/v1/memories`, `/api/v1/jobs`, `/api/v1/kbs`, and the `skills_list_all` + `skills_read` MCP tools. Per-section try/catch — one failing section emits a `backup_warnings[]` entry in the manifest rather than killing the whole backup.
- `POST /api/agent/restore` — multipart upload, optional `?dry_run=true` to preview, optional `?force=true` to overwrite name collisions (default = skip). Returns `{applied: {section: count}, skipped: {...}, errors: [...], warnings: [...]}`. Personality is always overwritten; everything else is upsert-or-skip.

UI: `/settings/backup-restore` page with three sections — Download, Restore (file picker → preview manifest + section counts → Apply with optional force checkbox), and Caveats (memory embeddings stripped, knowledge read-only at runtime, manifest jobs not exported, etc).

Idempotence + safety:
- Cleartext secrets in the zip → operator-sensitive; manifest carries an explicit warning, UI surfaces it next to the download button.
- `PHANTOM_SECRET_KEK` does NOT need to match across source and destination — the zip carries cleartext, the destination's SecretStore re-encrypts under its own KEK on write.
- 100 MB upload cap on restore (size check before parsing — bouncing fast on giant uploads beats parser hangs).
- Schema version pin in the manifest; restore rejects unknown schemas with a clear "supported: [N]" error rather than crashing on a future format.

The MCP-side `_instance_to_dict()` helper grew a `secret_store` parameter so it can return `inst.resolved_secrets(secret_store)` instead of the path-form `secret_refs` when `redact_secrets=False`. Default behavior unchanged — every existing caller still gets `{slot: "***"}`.

### Sidebar reorganization — Models + Providers + Backup & Restore under Settings

Pre-v0.1.36 the **Command** group held Chat, Skills, Memory, Knowledge, Jobs, **Models**, **Providers** — a mix of "I do this every session" (chat, skills, memory) and "I configure this once at install and rarely touch" (models, providers). The mental model split was wrong; the sidebar now lines up with how operators actually use the surfaces.

After:
- **Command** — Chat, Skills, Memory, Knowledge, Jobs (the day-to-day actions)
- **Settings** — Services, Models, Providers, Personality, Backup & Restore (the operator-once-and-rarely-touch admin surfaces)

No backend changes. Active-state highlighting in the sidebar already used a `findActiveGroupId` walker that checks `children.some(c => isActiveHref(c.href))` — moving an entry between groups Just Works. The `isActiveHref` catchall (`pathname.startsWith(href)`) handles `/models` and `/providers` (root-level paths) without needing any per-route override even though they sit alongside `/settings/*` children now.

### Caldera + xsiam connector tool-call paths read InstanceStore at runtime (single-source-of-truth, completed)

v0.1.34 brought xlog onto the InstanceStore-backed pattern (lifespan resolver, no env fallback, no probe-then-flip). Caldera + xsiam shipped that release "partially conformed" — their probes read from InstanceStore correctly, but the tool-call code was still importing `from config.config import get_config` and reading env-driven pydantic settings. That worked in practice when the env values matched the InstanceStore, but a divergence (operator edits one without the other) would have produced a green probe + a 401 on the next tool call.

v0.1.36 closes that gap:

- **Caldera** ([bundles/spark/connectors/caldera/src/connector.py](bundles/spark/connectors/caldera/src/connector.py)): the `from config.config import get_config` import is gone. New helper `_get_caldera_config()` calls `store.list_for("caldera")`, reads `instance.config.baseUrl`, and pulls `apiKey` via `instance.merged_config(store._secret_store)` — that single call gets both non-secret config and decrypted secrets in one shot. The `_caldera_request()` chokepoint runs the helper on every call, so all ~30 caldera tools transparently migrate without per-tool edits.

- **XSIAM** ([bundles/spark/connectors/xsiam/src/connector.py](bundles/spark/connectors/xsiam/src/connector.py)): three callsites of the deleted `get_config()` import migrated.
  - `_get_fetcher()` (used by every `xql.*` + `get_issues` + `get_endpoint_alerts` tool) now reads `papiUrl`, `papiAuthHeader`, `papiAuthId` from the merged dict on every fetcher build.
  - `xsoar_command` reads `playgroundId` via the central `_get_xsiam_config()` helper.
  - `send_webhook_log` reads `webhookEndpoint` + `webhookKey` via the same helper.

- **Visible side effect for misconfigured deployments**: the new resolvers raise rather than fall back to env. A deployment that was quietly running on env-driven config without a populated InstanceStore now surfaces as `caldera instance has no apiKey configured` (or the xsiam equivalent) on first tool call. The fix is to populate the instance via `/connectors`, which is the spec — but it's a visible change for anyone who never went through the setup form for those connectors. Same principle as v0.1.34's retirement of `setup.json`: no silent self-healing, the gap surfaces explicitly so it can be fixed at the source.

Net effect: a `/connectors` edit on caldera baseUrl/apiKey, or any of the six xsiam fields (papiUrl, papiAuthHeader, papiAuthId, playgroundId, webhookEndpoint, webhookKey), propagates to the next tool call immediately. No MCP restart, no env-var stamping, no probe-vs-tool-call divergence. Architecture spec at `/help/architecture#connector-state` is updated to reflect the completed state.

### Forgot-UI-password CLI (`reset-ui-password.sh`)

Customers who forget their Phantom UI password used to have an unpleasant recovery path: either `docker compose down -v` (which also wipes job history, audit log, instance configs, marketplace state) or manual SQLite surgery on the SecretStore. Both are nuclear options for a routine credential lapse.

`reset-ui-password.sh` is a small shell script shipped in the customer install kit alongside `phantom-installer`. Run it on the VM where the stack lives; it:

1. **Prompts for username + new password** (interactively, with confirmation), or accepts `--username` and `--password-file` for non-interactive use. The password is read via `read -s` (no echo to terminal) and is never passed via argv/env.
2. **Reads `MCP_TOKEN` from the running phantom-agent container's `/proc/1/environ`** rather than asking the operator to supply it. The token is per-boot random by default, so the operator may not have it; the script fishes it out of the container's environment block over `docker exec` so it lives in the script's process memory only for the duration of the curl call.
3. **POSTs to the MCP's `/api/v1/ui/auth/password` endpoint** via curl, piping the JSON body via stdin (`--data-binary @-`) so the password never appears on the command line.
4. **Verifies the new hash landed** by checking the response code + a follow-up read of the SecretStore path.

Result: the operator can sign in with the new password immediately, every other piece of stack state is untouched, and `history | grep <password>` returns nothing because the password was never in argv or env-var form.

The script is embedded into the customer single-file installer via the same `__INSTALLER_*__` placeholder pattern that the compose YAML uses ([installer/build-phantom-installer.sh](installer/build-phantom-installer.sh) gained a third marker `__INSTALLER_RESET_PASSWORD_SH__` plus a dedicated heredoc terminator `_PHANTOM_RESET_PASSWORD_HEREDOC_END_` so the embedded payloads can't collide). Multi-file kit packing in `release.yml` also picks up the script via `cp installer/reset-ui-password.sh "$KIT/reset-ui-password.sh"` so the kit-format users get the same recovery path.

Documentation lands in `/help/user` as Scenario 3d — Forgot UI password (CLI reset, v0.1.36+).

### VM compose drift check (pre-deploy gate, MANDATORY)

The v0.1.34 TLS smoke test caught a stale VM compose by accident: `docker-compose.yml` on phantom-vm had drifted from the canonical local file by weeks — xlog and caldera lacked the `/tls` volume mount despite the local file having it. `docker compose up -d --force-recreate` didn't help because the VM's spec was stale. The drift was invisible until the smoke test happened to surface it.

`scripts/check-vm-compose.sh` makes the check explicit:

- Reads VM credentials from `.env.vm` (the same convention as the rest of the dev workflow).
- Opens an IAP tunnel + runs `cat $VM_REMOTE_REPO/docker-compose.yml` over SSH, then `cmp -s` against the local file.
- Exit 0 if identical (sync-to-VM is safe), exit 1 if drifted (with a unified `diff -u` printed to stderr and a corrective `scp` command suggested in the error output).
- `--quiet` flag suppresses the diff body for terse CI usage.

CLAUDE.md's pre-deploy gate now mandates running this script before any `tar+scp` sync to the VM, alongside the existing `tsc --noEmit` + `npm run lint` + `npm run build` gate. The cost is ~5 seconds per deploy; the saved cost is the kind of multi-hour TLS-smoke debug we did in v0.1.34 when nobody knew the VM compose had drifted.

CLAUDE.md also picked up a parallel note: when sync-from-macOS via `tar`, set `COPYFILE_DISABLE=1 tar --no-xattrs ...` so AppleDouble sidecars (`._<filename>`) don't pollute the VM tree. We caught this when the v0.1.36 build-alignment audit found 3,002 `._*` files on the VM left behind by previous macOS-tar syncs — harmless for builds, but they made content-addressable hash comparisons noisy until we filtered them out.

---

## Notes on what's NOT in this release

- **Caldera HTTPS** still pending — same status as v0.1.34. The stock caldera entrypoint doesn't read `/tls/{cert,key}.pem` into `conf/server.{crt,key}`. Tracking as a follow-up.
- **Per-instance container migration** for xlog/xsiam/caldera (the v0.1.31 web pattern) is still scheduled for v0.2.0. The v0.1.36 InstanceStore refactor is orthogonal — it cleans up where the in-process tool-call code reads its config from, regardless of which runtime mode the connector runs in.

## Known issues

- Same Vertex placeholder credential issue carried over from v0.1.33 / v0.1.34. Operator action: set a real `GEMINI_API_KEY` or replace the placeholder JSON with a real GCP service-account key via `/providers`.

---

## [v0.1.34] — 2026-05-09

A foundational refactor plus the polish work that surfaced its motivating bugs. The headline change is the **setup-architecture refactor**: setup.json and .env.generated are gone, all operator-typed credentials live in their canonical stores (ProviderStore, InstanceStore, SecretStore), the setup-completed signal is a single flag file, and post-install partial updates to /providers and /connectors no longer touch the legacy storage at all. Documentation in `/help/architecture` becomes the canonical specification, and CLAUDE.md mandates checking it before any change to setup-adjacent code. Plus: plugin-contributed skills, help-page UX overhaul, single-shot setup, pre-deploy gate, providers-page editability + UI_PASSWORD validation fixes, TLS deployment hygiene, and chat handler placeholder-credential detection.

### Setup architecture refactor — `/help/architecture` is the spec, code now matches

This is the lead change in v0.1.34 and the reason the release exists. Pre-v0.1.34, `setup.json` was the de-facto master store for every operator-typed credential — UI password (plaintext), Vertex JSON, connector creds, the lot. The MCP-side ProviderStore + InstanceStore were downstream consumers populated via a re-materialise call after `setup.json` was written. The agent's `/providers` page READ from `setup.json` and WROTE to `setup.json` first, then pushed to the MCP. The `setupComplete: true` flag lived inside `setup.json` mixed with operator data. `isSetupRequired` fell back to env-var presence when `setup.json` was missing. Everything was tangled.

The cracks showed: `/providers` Save threw `UI_PASSWORD is required` on env-only deployments because `writeRuntimeSetup` validated first-install requirements on every write. The Vertex JSON textarea was effectively read-only because of a `readOnly` attribute that blocked the very paste events the onChange handler was supposed to handle. The setup form re-appeared after a `setup.json` deletion even though auth was clearly bootstrapped (PBKDF2 hash in SecretStore, env had UI_PASSWORD). Each fix made the surface a little less wrong but didn't address the cause.

v0.1.34 fixes the cause. The architecture page at `/help/architecture#setup-wiring` got a full canonical specification — lifecycle, inputs, storage destinations per input, post-setup edit semantics, what does NOT exist post-setup, manifest binding schema. CLAUDE.md got a mandate: read that section before touching any of `app/api/setup/*`, `app/setup/*`, `lib/runtime-config.ts`, `app/api/agent/providers/*`, `app/providers/*`, `app/api/auth/change-password/*`, or `app/profile/*`. The spec wins; if the code disagrees, the code is the gap.

Then we closed the gap. The seven specific deviations the page used to list are all resolved:

1. **Provider data master is the ProviderStore.** Added `ProviderStore.update()` to the MCP and a `PUT /api/v1/providers/{instance_id}` route. The agent's `/api/agent/providers/config` GET now calls MCP `GET /api/v1/providers?provider_id=vertex` directly. PUT calls `PUT /api/v1/providers/{id}` for partial updates (or `POST /api/v1/providers` for first-time create). Neither path reads or writes `setup.json`. Field mapping at the agent boundary: `form.vertexProjectId` → `instance.config.project_id`, `form.vertexLocation` → `instance.config.region`, `form.vertexServiceAccountJson` → `instance.secrets.serviceAccountJson`. Untouched secret fields round-trip as the `***` redaction sentinel and are preserved server-side.

2. **`.env.generated` is no longer written.** `writeRuntimeSetup`, `serializeEnv`, and `validateServiceAccountJson` are deleted. The chat handler reads `runtimeConfig.GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` at ~16 sites; those sites are unchanged. What changed is `getEffectiveRuntimeConfig` — it now resolves the Vertex JSON from the ProviderStore via a new `lib/vertex-credentials.ts` module (30-second cache, cache-busted on `/providers` PUT). The chat handler thus reads from the ProviderStore through an existing API surface, with no per-site code change.

3. **Setup-completed flag has its own home.** A presence-only file at `/app/runtime/.setup_complete` on the persistent volume is the single signal. `setup.json` no longer carries it (and for new installs no longer exists at all). Legacy installs are auto-migrated: the first call to `isSetupRequired()` after upgrade reads any pre-existing `setupComplete: true` from a legacy `setup.json` and writes the new flag file in its place. Idempotent, one-shot, no operator action required.

4. **`isSetupRequired` consults only the flag.** No env-var fallback (`hasRequiredEnv` is gone). No field-completeness probing. Either the flag exists (setup done, render LoginScreen) or it doesn't (render SetupScreen). One signal, no ambiguity, deterministic across env-only and form-completed installs alike.

5. **First-install validation in one place.** `validateFirstInstallSetup()` is exported from `lib/runtime-config.ts` and called once from `/api/setup` POST. Post-install partial writes (`/providers`, `/connectors`) trust that auth bootstrap already happened — no false-positive "UI_PASSWORD is required" on flows that don't touch UI auth.

6. **`/api/agent/providers/config` GET returns clean shape.** Project ID and region come back in cleartext from the ProviderStore; the service-account JSON returns as the redaction sentinel. The agent's runtime never sees cleartext secrets on this read path. Cleartext is fetched only when the chat handler resolves Vertex creds, via the new `?include_secrets=true` query param on the MCP-side detail endpoint (bearer-auth gated).

7. **UI password rotation pattern extended to all settings pages.** `/profile` already wrote to the SecretStore directly. v0.1.34 extends the same pattern to `/providers` (writes to ProviderStore) and the existing `/connectors` (writes to InstanceStore — already conformed). `/api/setup` itself now writes the UI password hash to the SecretStore via `MCP /api/v1/ui/auth/password` during first install, so the legacy plaintext compare path in `/api/auth/login` is permanently dead code on new installs (kept only as a transition fallback for pre-v0.1.34 deployments).

Net effect on file system, post-refactor:
```
/app/runtime/                                         /app/data/secrets/
├── .setup_complete    (presence-only flag, 25B)      ├── ui/auth/<user>/password_hash
└── setup.json         (LEGACY — only on upgraded     ├── agents/<bundle>/providers/<id>/<slot>
                        installs; never written       └── agents/<bundle>/instances/<id>/<slot>
                        post-v0.1.34)
```

If you find a path that still touches `setup.json` for operator-typed values, or that reads creds from `.env.generated`, that's a regression — the spec at `/help/architecture#setup-wiring` is the contract.

### Plugin-contributed skills

The skills system grew a fifth source: **plugins**. Skills installed via plugin packages now live under `bundles/spark/mcp/skills/plugins/<vendor>/*.md` and surface in the UI under a new **Plugins** filter pill alongside the existing four categories. Path is authoritative — even if a plugin's frontmatter declares `category: foundation`, the skill buckets under Plugins (the original category is preserved as `declared_category` for the model's awareness). Plugin skills also get vendor-qualified canonical names (`<vendor>.<skill-name>`) so two plugins can ship a skill with the same short name without colliding.

Implementation: `skills_crud.py::get_all_skills` walks `plugins/<vendor>/*.md` as a separate pass after the four built-in categories; the agent's skills page extends `CategoryKey` and `SourceType` with `"plugins"` and renders a fifth pill. Existing plugin-less installs see no UI change.

### Skills page — count widgets + Import + workspace cleanup

- **Live counts.** The Total / Enabled / Categories widgets at the top of `/skills` were hardcoded `{total: 11, ...}` and quietly wrong any time the on-disk skill count drifted. Now derived live from the `/api/skills` response via `useMemo` over `skills.length`, `skills.filter(enabled)`, and `unique(category)`.
- **Import skill** — new button next to **Create Skill** in the page header. Drag a `.md` file (or pick via dialog), the client parses YAML frontmatter, posts to `/api/skills`, and the new skill appears in the list immediately. Imports always land under the category declared in the frontmatter; for plugin imports, place the file under `plugins/<vendor>/` first and the path-authoritative rule kicks in.
- **Production-Cluster-Alpha workspace selector removed** — that menu belonged to Spark workspaces, not to a single-instance Phantom agent. The chip never had any backend semantics on this surface.

### Skill detail panel — auto-load body

Click a skill in the list, the body now loads automatically. Pre-fix, the panel showed a placeholder line ("See bundles/spark/mcp/skills/...") and required clicking a **Load Body** button before the markdown rendered. Now a `useEffect(loadBody, [...])` fires on panel mount with a centered "Loading skill body…" card during the fetch, and a per-skill error card with **Retry** if the load fails. State machine: `"loading" | "loaded" | "error"` (3-value enum, not a boolean) so the loading shell doesn't flash on cached re-opens.

### Help pages — full-width layout, bigger fonts, collapsible nav

Both `/help/architecture` and `/help/user` were capped at `max-w-[920px]` / `max-w-3xl` with sub-12px sidebar fonts — content rivers wide screens with whitespace and the nav was eyestrain-tight. Plus the only collapse-like affordance was the "Help index" backlink, which navigated AWAY from the current page when clicked.

Three coordinated changes:
- **Full viewport width**: container bumped to `max-w-[1400px]` with `px-10` padding. Content now uses the screen on a 27" monitor.
- **Larger fonts**: sidebar items `text-sm` (was `text-[11px]`), group labels `text-xs` (was `text-[10px]`), descriptions `text-sm`, page heading `text-xl` (was `text-base`). Content scroll container has an explicit `text-base` baseline.
- **Collapsible nav rail**: chevron toggle in the sidebar header collapses to a 40px rail with chevron-right to expand. State persists in localStorage (separate keys per page: `phantom.help.architecture.sidebar-collapsed`, `phantom.help.user.sidebar-collapsed`). **Stays on the same page** — no route navigation. The "Help index" backlink is preserved as a separate route-navigation affordance.

### Pre-deploy gate (3-step build verification)

The v0.1.33 release shipped a Next.js Route contract violation (`export function detectPlaceholderCredential` in a `route.ts` file) that passed `tsc --noEmit` and `npm run lint` but failed `npm run build`. We didn't catch it because the local validation only ran the first two; the failure surfaced 90 seconds into a VM rebuild and required a separate verbose run to even read the error. Add 2-3 minutes per VM round trip and the cost adds up.

CLAUDE.md now codifies the rule: **`npx tsc --noEmit` + `npm run lint` + `npm run build` are all three required before sync-to-VM**. `tsc` and `lint` together don't catch Next.js's strict route validation; only `next build` does. The rationale and the v0.1.33 lesson live as a paragraph in CLAUDE.md so the rule sticks.

Companion fixes that pushed the rule:
- **Chat: detect placeholder Vertex credentials.** Pre-fix, an unfilled `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` JSON template hit Node's PEM decoder with `error:1E08010C:DECODER routines::unsupported` on every chat dispatch — including every scheduled job run. Now `lib/chat/detectPlaceholderCredential` recognizes `client_email: "REPLACE_WITH_..."` / `private_key: "..."` shapes and returns an operator-actionable error before the call ever reaches Vertex: "Credentials look like a template — replace with a real GCP service account key in Settings → Providers." `job-run-failed` notifications now fire with that message instead of the cryptic decoder line.

### Providers page — concrete UX bugs fixed (subsumed by the refactor above)

The setup-architecture refactor above is what *should* have shipped, but two operator-visible bugs needed individual fixes en route — both are now permanently resolved by the refactor, and they're called out here so the upgrade story is complete:

- **`UI_PASSWORD is required` on every `/providers` Save** (env-only deployments). Cause: `writeRuntimeSetup` validated `REQUIRED_SETUP_KEYS` on every write, even partial updates that didn't touch UI auth. Fix landed first as `validateFirstInstallSetup()` lifted into `/api/setup` only; the refactor then deleted `writeRuntimeSetup` entirely so the gate has no surface to fire from on post-install paths.
- **Service-account JSON textarea was effectively read-only** when a previous JSON had been saved. Cause: `readOnly={!vertexJsonVisible && !!form.vertexServiceAccountJson}` on the textarea blocked the very paste events the onChange handler was meant to handle. Fix: removed `readOnly`, rewrote onChange to strip leftover bullets when the user types/pastes into a masked field. After the refactor, `/providers` doesn't merge against setup.json at all — secrets round-trip cleanly via the MCP-side `***` sentinel handling.

Transitive bonus from the refactor: Project ID and Region now auto-populate on `/providers` page open, because the GET reads from the ProviderStore directly — there's no "no setup.json yet" state where the form would show empty.

### Connector URL resolution: InstanceStore is the single source of truth (xlog refactor)

The same architectural principle the setup refactor applied to providers + connectors — single source of truth, no silent self-healing — has been extended to connector URL resolution. v0.1.34 brings the xlog connector fully onto this pattern:

- **MCP-side**: `bundles/spark/mcp/src/service/phantom_mcp/server.py` lifespan now exposes a `get_xlog_url()` callable that hits `InstanceStore.list_for("xlog")` and reads `config.baseUrl` on every tool invocation. All 13 xlog tool call sites (workers, scenarios, observables, simulation_runs, field_info, data_faker) updated from `lifespan_context["xlog_url"]` (pinned at boot) to `lifespan_context["get_xlog_url"]()` (resolved live).
- **Agent-side**: new `mcp/agent/lib/xlog-url.ts::resolveXlogUrlFromStore()` calls MCP `/api/v1/instances?connector_id=xlog` and returns the live baseUrl. `getEffectiveRuntimeConfig().XLOG_URL` uses this resolver first, falling back to env only when no instance is configured (pre-setup window). The /api/agent/health probe and /api/agent/reports proxy both pick up the InstanceStore value automatically — both were previously reading `process.env.XLOG_URL` directly.
- **Removed workaround**: `entrypoint.sh` previously did a probe-then-flip of `XLOG_URL` at agent boot — quietly mutating process env to whichever scheme xlog answered on. That masked TLS rollouts and config drift behind silent magic. Block removed; XLOG_URL in env now stays as compose declares it, and only the InstanceStore is mutable post-setup (via /connectors).
- **Removed workaround**: the xlog probe in `connector_probes.py` previously did its own probe-then-flip when the configured URL failed connection. Removed in the same release; the probe now sends one request and surfaces the verbatim httpx error (e.g. `RemoteProtocolError: Server disconnected`) so operators see the real failure and update via /connectors.

Net effect: `/connectors` edits to xlog baseUrl propagate immediately to (a) the next tool dispatch, (b) the health probe, (c) the reports proxy, with no MCP restart, no env-var pinning, no hidden flip.

Caldera + xsiam connectors are partially conformed (probes read from InstanceStore correctly; tool-call paths still read from env-backed pydantic settings). Tracking as v0.1.35 — refactor those connectors' tool-call paths to mirror the xlog pattern.

### Test Connection actually tests now (was rubber-stamping)

Pre-v0.1.34 the `/providers` Test Connection button POSTed to `/api/agent/providers/vertex/test`, which didn't exist. The page interpreted the resulting 404 as a soft-success "Saved — validated at chat runtime", so placeholder service-account JSON got a green badge despite no test running. Operator only learned creds were broken at first chat dispatch.

Fixed by:
- New `/api/agent/providers/vertex/test` route that does a real OAuth2 token exchange via `google-auth-library` against `oauth2.googleapis.com`. JSON shape errors get a translated message ("private_key invalid PEM — re-paste from GCP Console"); everything else (Google's `invalid_grant`, network errors, etc.) passes through verbatim per operator's "no static heuristics" principle.
- Sentinel `***` in the body resolves to the currently stored Vertex JSON (lets operator validate stored creds without re-pasting).
- Caldera probe upgraded from unauthenticated `GET /` (login page, returns 200 to anyone) to authenticated `GET /api/v2/health` with the operator's API key in the `KEY` header. 401 → `is_auth_error=true` → UI flips to amber `needs-auth`. Detects wrong API keys at probe time instead of first tool call.

### Sign-out + bfcache hardening (security fix)

Critical UX hole: clicking the browser Back button after Sign-out restored the previous authenticated page from the browser's bfcache (back-forward cache) — including React state with `authenticated: true`. The user could then click around authenticated pages without the server ever being asked to re-validate. Sign-out HAD cleared the cookie, so the SECURITY boundary was correct (any authenticated API call would 401), but the UI didn't refresh, leading to a confusing "I'm signed out but I can still see my profile" experience.

Fixed by:
- AuthGate now listens for `pageshow` events with `event.persisted === true` (bfcache restore signal). On hit, resets state to `null` (showing the loading splash) and refetches `/api/auth/status`. Operator sees: bfcache splash → loading → LoginScreen.
- AuthGate also listens for `visibilitychange` (tab return) and re-fetches status when the tab becomes visible. Defense in depth across same-origin tabs.
- `/api/auth/status` and `/api/auth/logout` responses now carry `Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate, private` and `Pragma: no-cache`. Even if a layered cache mishandles the bfcache signal, the auth-status JSON itself is uncacheable.
- AuthGate's status fetch uses `cache: "no-store"` so the browser-level fetch cache also can't serve a stale `{authenticated: true}`.
- Logout cookie is cleared with both `maxAge: 0` and `Expires: new Date(0)` for cross-browser robustness.

### TLS deployment hygiene (smoke-test findings, no code change)

A full TLS smoke test on phantom-vm surfaced — and fixed — a deployment-only gotcha: when `docker-compose.yml` adds a *volume mount* to a service, plain `docker compose up -d` keeps the existing containers and silently skips the new mount. The agent had been running with a `/tls/cert.pem` in its container overlay layer (auto-generated at boot) that didn't match the `/tls/cert.pem` xlog and caldera mounted from the shared `phantom_tls` volume — same path, two different files. Node's `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/tls/cert.pem` was correctly set but pointed at the wrong cert, so `fetch` to `https://xlog:8000` failed with `DEPTH_ZERO_SELF_SIGNED_CERT` even with the trust override.

Fix is operational, not code: `docker compose up -d --force-recreate` after any compose change that touches volume mounts. Also confirmed the local repo's compose has the right mounts on all three services — the bug was that the VM's compose file had drifted from the canonical local copy (the VM is not a git repo, so changes have to be `scp`-ed). After sync + `--force-recreate xlog caldera phantom-agent`, all three services are on TLS, the cert is consistent across `/tls` mounts, and `agent → xlog` over HTTPS works end-to-end through the connector.

---

## Notes on what's NOT in this release

- **Caldera HTTPS**: the `/tls` cert is mounted into the caldera container per the compose file, but the stock caldera entrypoint doesn't read `/tls/{cert,key}.pem` into `conf/server.{crt,key}` — `local.yml` still declares `app.contact.http: http://0.0.0.0:8888` and the listener stays on plain HTTP. The agent's health probe correctly skips caldera (`url:""`) so this isn't blocking, but caldera-on-HTTPS is genuine debt. Tracking as a follow-up.
- **VM compose drift detection**: the v0.1.34 TLS smoke test caught a stale VM compose by accident. There's no automated check that the VM's `docker-compose.yml` matches the canonical local file. A pre-deploy hook that diffs them would prevent the class entirely; not in scope for this release.

## Known issues

- Same Vertex placeholder credential issue carried over from v0.1.33 on the canonical phantom-vm, but now surfaces as a clear "credentials look like a template" error in `job-run-failed` notifications instead of a decoder traceback. Operator action: set a real `GEMINI_API_KEY` or replace the placeholder JSON with a real GCP service-account key.

---

## [v0.1.33] — 2026-05-09

A skills overhaul. Phantom's MD runbooks under `bundles/spark/mcp/skills/` graduate from a static catalog with hardcoded UI metadata into a fully dynamic, operator-editable, model-aware system. Plus the operator-experience fixes from the v0.1.32 series that never got their own tag — jobs export/import, /profile sign-out, password change polish, the EnvSecretStore overlay, and a fully edit-able job form.

### Skills v0.1.33 — five-phase rework

- **YAML frontmatter is now the metadata source of truth.** Every skill MD starts with a `---`-delimited block containing `name`, `displayName`, `category`, `description`, `icon`, `source`, `loadingMode`, `locked`, and ATT&CK tactics. The backend (`skills_crud.py::parse_frontmatter`) parses it on every `skills_list_all` call; the `/api/skills` route returns the rich shape; `app/skills/page.tsx` fetches live and replaces its hardcoded fallback array on mount. New MDs added to disk now appear in the UI automatically — no more drift from skills landing on disk but not in the page array.
- **Full CRUD from the UI.** The skill detail panel grew **Download** (fetches the live MD as a `.md` file), **Save** (commits textarea edits via `PUT /api/skills` — backend writes a `.md.bak` first), and **Delete** (soft-delete to `/app/skills/.deleted/`). Locked skills (`locked: true` frontmatter) render Delete as disabled. The footer **Create Skill** button now actually creates: display name auto-derives the on-disk filename, category dropdown is the four valid values, and Submit composes minimal frontmatter and POSTs. UI-driven CRUD lands on the volume immediately — no `FORCE_SKILLS_SYNC` dance, no container restart.
- **The chat agent is now skill-aware.** Every chat turn's system prompt includes an `## AVAILABLE SKILLS` block listing every installed skill's name, display name, category, description, and ATT&CK tactics. ~2-3KB total for our 23 skills (vs ~50-150KB if we shipped bodies). The model picks a skill based on intent and calls `skills_read` to pull the full body when it decides to apply it. Implementation is in `lib/skills-registry.ts` and `lib/system-prompt.ts::renderSkillsBlock`.
- **Jobs can bind to a specific skill.** Prompt-action jobs grew an optional **Skill** dropdown in `/jobs/new`. Default "Let agent decide" preserves the old behavior (model picks based on intent). Picking a specific skill makes the run deterministic — the scheduler resolves the MD body at fire time and prepends it to the prompt inside `<skill name="…">…</skill>` tags. Useful for reproducible scheduled exercises that should always run the same runbook regardless of model drift.
- **Every scheduled job run now publishes a notification.** Two new manifest topics — `job-run-completed` (info) and `job-run-failed` (warning) — fire on every fire, with payload `{job_name, run_id, trigger, action_name, duration_ms, summary, error?}`. Skipped runs (cron-cap squelching) don't emit. Two more topics declared for future use: `approval-requested` and `marketplace-install`.

### XSIAM Analytics — top-20 simulatable rules

New validation-category skill `xsiam_top20_coverage.md` cataloging the 20 highest-feasibility Cortex XSIAM analytics alerts XLOG can trigger directly. Each entry has the verbatim alert name from Palo Alto's [XSIAM Analytics Alert Reference](https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-XSIAM/Cortex-XSIAM-Analytics-Alert-Reference-by-Alert-name/Cortex-XSIAM-Analytics-Alert-Reference), the required telemetry shape, an inline `xlog.create_data_worker` recipe, and a cross-reference to the broader scenario skill that exercises the same pattern. Coverage spans seven families: auth/identity, lateral movement, C2/network, cloud audit tampering, endpoint persistence + cred access, AD/Kerberos, Kubernetes.

### 12 new attack-scenario skills (heavy-volume kill chains)

Each models a complete attack chain across multiple ATT&CK tactics with 1k–13k synthetic events:

- `ransomware_double_extortion` — full LockBit-style chain: phish → PowerShell → LSASS dump → defender tamper → SMB lateral → bulk exfil → file encryption + Volume Shadow delete (~1.5k events)
- `apt_long_dwell_espionage` — slow-burn nation-state campaign with low-rate exploit + spray + DNS C2 + tunneled exfil over 12 simulated hours (~3k events)
- `insider_threat_data_theft` — DBA exfil with HR-resignation pivot, baseline + anomaly pattern (~2.5k events)
- `supply_chain_npm_compromise` — backdoored npm package fan-out across 30+ build hosts with same-IoC outbound signature (~2k events)
- `cloud_account_takeover_m365` — OAuth consent phishing → token sign-in → inbox-rule persistence → mailbox harvest via Graph (~2.4k events, 100% cloud audit telemetry)
- `phishing_credential_harvest_campaign` — 5k inbound phish → 0.6% click-through → SSO replay + MFA-push fatigue → VPN + RDP fan-out (~6k events)
- `web_app_sqli_to_rce` — WAF noise + SQLi + web shell + reverse shell + DB mass-export + paste/discord exfil (~3.8k events)
- `living_off_the_land_lolbin` — fileless attack via signed Microsoft tools (certutil/mshta/wmic/bitsadmin/msbuild) + WMI subscriber persistence + DoH C2 (~1k events)
- `cryptojacking_xmrig_botnet` — Redis CONFIG-SET → SSH key drop → Docker API container deploy → cron+systemd persistence + EDR disable + sustained mining-pool C2 (~2.7k events)
- `business_email_compromise` — 10-day quiet mailbox dwell + look-alike domain + inbox rule for evidence destruction + spoofed CEO wire instruction (~2.5k events, social-engineering only)
- `kubernetes_container_escape` — pod RCE → service-account token abuse → privileged pod with hostPath:/ → chroot escape → cluster-admin ClusterRoleBinding (~1.2k events, kube-audit heavy)
- `ddos_volumetric_layer7` — multi-vector availability attack: DNS+NTP+memcached amplification + TCP SYN flood + L7 HTTP-GET flood + Slowloris (~13k events)

### Documentation discipline (MANDATORY rule)

CLAUDE.md grew a "Documentation discipline" section that codifies the rule. Every release MUST refresh:

- `mcp/agent/app/help/architecture/page.tsx` — services, ports, AND every inter-service connection with auth class
- `mcp/agent/app/help/user/page.tsx` — operator-visible features tagged with the version that introduced them
- `mcp/agent/lib/journeys.ts` — user journeys for new flows
- `mcp/agent/app/skills/page.tsx` — array entry per new MD (until the dynamic-load refactor in this same release made it optional)
- CHANGELOG.md — operator-language deltas

The checklist runs **before** asking for release approval, parallel to the build-on-VM rule. Goal: prevent the drift that was happening — backend features merging without UI surfaces or docs, accumulating until users couldn't find what was shipping.

### v0.1.32-series operator UX fixes (folded into this release)

- **/profile page** with cookie-based auth, password change backed by `SecretStore`, and a Sign-out section. The sign-out card uses theme-aware `color-mix` amber so it reads correctly on both light and dark themes; sign-out redirects to `/` (was incorrectly going to `/login` which doesn't exist).
- **Change-password → re-login flow**: form fields now have `autoComplete="username"` / `current-password"` / `new-password` so password managers reflect the change. Server-side `_normalize_password()` strips whitespace symmetrically in `set_password()` AND `verify()` so a copy-paste with trailing space doesn't lock the operator out.
- **Jobs export + import** as separate flows. Two export options under the kebab — **Export definition (.json)** (definition only, importable as-is) and **Export runs (.json)** (definition + every run from the run-history table; for forensic snapshots — runs are NOT importable as run history per the policy that run history is read-only ground truth). Import button accepts either shape; if the file came from `-with-runs.json`, the runs array is silently dropped.
- **Job action types collapsed** to `{prompt, tool_call}`. Pre-v0.1.32 there was a third type `log` that bypassed the chat pipeline by calling `phantom_create_data_worker` directly. The boot migration normalizes existing rows: `chat → prompt` (rename), `log → tool_call(phantom_create_data_worker, request=…)` (lift the log-shortcut keys into a tool_call request). Idempotent; runs every boot so re-imports of pre-v0.1.32 jobs are also normalized.
- **Edit job** added to the kebab menu (between Pause and Duplicate). Opens the new-job form with every field pre-populated from the existing row — schedule (loaded as Custom + raw cron), timezone, action type and body, enabled flag, approval bypass. Name is locked (rename = delete + create new); manifest-source jobs accept the patch but the next reconciliation reverts it.
- **EnvSecretStore overlay** — pre-bake secrets via env vars without overwriting the at-rest `SecretStore`. At read time, env values shadow stored secrets so operators can override per-deployment without touching the encrypted store. Useful for pinning a connector instance to a specific API key per environment.
- **xlog kill_worker** MCP tool — stop a running synthetic-log worker by ID. Pre-v0.1.32 the only way to halt was `docker compose restart xlog` which dropped every worker indiscriminately; this tool is precise.
- **Approval card UX** — preamble before the card + always-visible key args. Pre-v0.1.32 the operator saw "Approve `phantom_create_data_worker`?" with the args collapsed; now the agent's preamble explains *why* it's calling the tool and the most relevant args (count, vendor, destination) render up-top so the operator can make a decision without expanding the JSON.
- **Marketplace install** decoupled from instances. Pre-fix, "installed" derived from "has at least one instance"; the fix introduces an explicit install state per connector that's persisted independently. **Uninstall** got its own button with loading state + toast feedback. **Create Instance** form now seeds `configValues` from `defaultValue` so the Create button is enabled out of the gate (was disabled until every field was touched, even if all defaults were valid).

---

## Notes on what's NOT in this release

A few audit findings from the broader backend↔UI sync survey were intentionally deferred — addressing them would have ballooned scope:

- **Workflows page** — `/api/agent/workflows` exists but is just 5 hardcoded prompt templates in `lib/agent-contract.ts`, not a workflow engine. Won't-fix as a UI surface; if a real workflow engine is needed later, it's a separate design conversation.
- **API Keys page** mock data — the API-keys feature doesn't exist yet on the backend either, so the UI placeholder stays until that's built.
- **Reports / Instances / Agent-Definitions** endpoints with no UI page — backend works via API; UI surfaces tracked for v0.1.34+.

## Known issues

- **Vertex/Gemini placeholder credentials** on the canonical phantom-vm cause `error:1E08010C:DECODER routines::unsupported` on every job run that dispatches a chat. Environmental, not code — operator must set a real `GEMINI_API_KEY` or replace the placeholder `GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS` JSON with a real GCP service-account key. Until then, `job-run-failed` notifications fire every job tick.

---

## [v0.1.31] and earlier

See git history (`git log v0.1.30..v0.1.31`, etc.) — pre-v0.1.33 releases didn't maintain a CHANGELOG. Going forward, every release MUST land an entry here per the Documentation discipline rule in CLAUDE.md.
